## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Acousto-Optic Modulator (AOM) System

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **functional architecture** of an AO modulator system, including the RF drive electronics, the acousto-optic interaction cell, and the optical output paths. This is a Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow because the physical geometry of a laser beam and acoustic transducer cannot be faithfully rendered in Mermaid.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    rfSource["RF Signal Source 50 MHz to 1 GHz"] --> amp["RF Power Amplifier"]
    amp --> match["Impedance Matching Network 50 Ohms"]
    match --> trans["Piezoelectric Transducer LiNbO3 or TeO2"]
    trans --> crystal["AO Crystal Interaction Medium TeO2 or Fused Silica"]
    laser["CW Laser Source HeNe or Diode Laser 633 nm"] --> bs1["Polarizing Beam Splitter"]
    bs1 --> crystal
    crystal --> d0["Zero Order Beam Photodiode D0"]
    crystal --> d1["First Order Diffracted Beam Photodiode D1"]
    crystal -- "Acoustic Wave Lambda" --> absorber["Acoustic Absorber Prevents Standing Waves"]
    d1 --> out["Modulated Optical Output to Fiber or Free Space"]

    classDef electronics fill:#e0f2ff,stroke:#0277bd,color:#000
    classDef optics fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100,color:#000
    classDef sensors fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#6a1b9a,color:#000
    class rfSource,amp,match,trans electronics
    class laser,bs1,crystal,absorber optics
    class d0,d1,out sensors
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **top horizontal chain** (blue nodes) is the **RF signal path**: source → amplifier → matching network → transducer.
- The **middle chain** (orange nodes) is the **optical beam path**: laser → beam splitter → AO crystal.
- The **bottom-right chain** (purple nodes) is the **output/detection path**.
- The **acoustic absorber** is critical: without it, reflected acoustic waves would create a standing-wave grating, ruining the modulation.

---

### 4.2 Block Diagram — AC Powder Electroluminescent (ACPEL) Display Cell

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph stack["Layered Cross Section of an AC Powder EL Cell"]
        top["Transparent Top Electrode ITO on Glass Substrate"]
        t1["Upper Dielectric Layer BaTiO3 or Y2O3"]
        phos["Phosphor Layer ZnS Mn Cu Embedded in Dielectric Binder"]
        t2["Lower Dielectric Layer BaTiO3 or Y2O3"]
        bot["Back Electrode Aluminum or Silver"]
    end
    driver["High Voltage AC Driver 100 to 220 Vrms at 50 to 1000 Hz"] --> top
    bot --> driver
    phos -- "Impact Excitation of Mn2+ Centers" --> emit["Photon Emission Lambda approx 585 nm Yellow Orange"]
    emit --> observer["Observer"]

    classDef dielectric fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000
    classDef electrode fill:#fce4ec,stroke:#880e4f,color:#000
    classDef active fill:#fff8e1,stroke:#ff6f00,color:#000
    class t1,t2 dielectric
    class top,bot,driver electrode
    class phos,emit,observer active
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **green-bordered nodes** ($t_1, t_2$) are the *dielectric barrier layers* that limit current and force the device into a capacitive (high-field, low-current) regime — essential for EL operation.
- The **pink-bordered nodes** are the *electrodes* and the *driver* — note that the AC nature of the driver means the field reverses every half-cycle, emitting one flash per half-cycle.
- The **yellow-bordered node** (`phos`) is the *active light-emitting layer* — ZnS:Mn,Cu is the canonical phosphor for orange-yellow AC-EL.

---

### 4.3 Signal-Flow Diagram — Information Path Inside an AO Deflector

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Input Electrical Signal Data or RF Carrier"] --> B["Frequency Synthesizer Sets Acoustic Frequency fs"]
    B --> C["Transducer Converts to Acoustic Wave in Crystal"]
    C --> D["Acoustic Wave Creates Moving Phase Grating"]
    D --> E{"Is fs within Bragg Bandwidth"}
    E -- "Yes" --> F["Light Bragg Diffracted Into First Order"]
    E -- "No" --> G["Light Not Diffracted Zero Order Only"]
    F --> H["Output Beam at Angle Delta Theta"]
    G --> I["Output Beam Straight Through"]

    classDef process fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#0d47a1,color:#000
    classDef decision fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000
    class A,B,C,D,F,H,I process
    class E decision
    class G output
```

This diagram clarifies the *binary switching behavior* of an AO device: it acts as a **diffractive optical switch** whose state is determined by whether the input RF frequency lies within the Bragg acceptance bandwidth of the AO cell.

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define the Acousto-Optic Effect. What is the role of a piezoelectric transducer in an acousto-optic device?**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> The acousto-optic effect is the phenomenon in which a light beam undergoes diffraction, modulation, or deflection when it interacts with an acoustic wave propagating through a transparent medium. The acoustic wave creates a periodic modulation of the refractive index of the medium, forming a phase grating that diffracts the incident light.
> *(1 mark for definition)*
> A **piezoelectric transducer** is bonded to the AO crystal and converts the input RF electrical signal into a mechanical (acoustic) vibration through the inverse piezoelectric effect. This acoustic wave then travels into the AO crystal to set up the refractive index grating. Without the transducer, there would be no acoustic wave and therefore no acousto-optic interaction.
> *(2 marks for transducer role)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**What is Electroluminescence? Differentiate between injection electroluminescence and intrinsic (high-field) electroluminescence.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> Electroluminescence (EL) is the phenomenon of light emission from a material when it is subjected to an electric field or electric current, without the need for thermal excitation.
> *(1 mark for definition)*
> **Injection EL** occurs at a forward-biased p–n junction; minority carriers (electrons and holes) are injected across the junction and recombine radiatively, releasing photons of energy approximately equal to the bandgap $E_g$. Examples: LEDs, laser diodes.
> *(1 mark)*
> **Intrinsic (high-field) EL** occurs in phosphor-dielectric sandwiches under a strong AC electric field. Electrons are accelerated by the field and impact-excite luminescent centers, which then emit photons upon de-excitation. Examples: AC powder EL panels, DCEL thin films.
> *(1 mark)*

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Derive the Bragg condition for the acousto-optic effect. Starting from the basic geometry of light scattering from acoustic wavefronts, show that $2 n \Lambda \sin\theta_B = \lambda$ for first-order symmetric diffraction. State the Klein–Cook parameter $Q$ and explain how it distinguishes between the Raman–Nath and Bragg regimes. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An acousto-optic deflector uses a tellurium dioxide ($\text{TeO}_2$) crystal with refractive index $n = 2.26$, acoustic velocity $v_s = 4260$ m/s, and an optical aperture of $D = 10$ mm. The optical source is a He-Ne laser ($\lambda = 633$ nm) and the RF driver can sweep the acoustic frequency over $\Delta f_s = 50$ MHz centered at $f_s = 100$ MHz. Calculate:
   (i) The acoustic wavelength $\Lambda$ at the center frequency.
   (ii) The Bragg angle $\theta_B$ at the center frequency (in degrees and milliradians).
   (iii) The total angular sweep $\Delta\theta$ of the first-order beam.
   (iv) The number of resolvable spots $N$.
   **(7 marks)**

---

> **Model Solution to Question A:**

> **(a) Derivation of the Bragg condition (7 marks):**
> *(Full derivation as given in Section 3.1 of these notes. Examiner expects: 1 mark for setup, 2 marks for path difference expression, 2 marks for interference condition, 1 mark for symmetric Bragg specialization, 1 mark for Klein-Cook discussion.)*
> 
> Final Bragg condition: $2 n \Lambda \sin\theta_B = \lambda$.
> Klein–Cook parameter $Q = 2\pi \lambda L / (n \Lambda^2)$.
> - $Q \ll 1$ → Raman–Nath (many orders).
> - $Q \gg 1$ → Bragg (single dominant order, $Q > 4\pi$ is a common design rule).

> **(b) Numerical solution (7 marks):**
> **Given:** $n = 2.26$, $v_s = 4260$ m/s, $D = 10$ mm $= 0.01$ m, $\lambda = 633$ nm $= 633 \times 10^{-9}$ m, $f_s = 100$ MHz $= 100 \times 10^6$ Hz, $\Delta f_s = 50$ MHz.
>
> **(i) Acoustic wavelength:**
>
> $$\Lambda = \frac{v_s}{f_s} = \frac{4260}{100 \times 10^6} = 4.26 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m} = 42.6 \, \mu\text{m}$$
> *(1.5 marks)*
>
> **(ii) Bragg angle at center frequency:**
>
> $$\sin\theta_B = \frac{\lambda}{2 n \Lambda} = \frac{633 \times 10^{-9}}{2 \times 2.26 \times 4.26 \times 10^{-5}}$$
>
> $$\sin\theta_B = \frac{633 \times 10^{-9}}{1.9255 \times 10^{-4}} = 3.288 \times 10^{-3}$$
>
> $$\theta_B = \arcsin(3.288 \times 10^{-3}) \approx 3.288 \times 10^{-3} \text{ rad} = 3.288 \text{ mrad}$$
>
> Converting to degrees:
>
> $$\theta_B = 3.288 \times 10^{-3} \times \frac{180}{\pi} = 0.1884^{\circ}$$
>
> *(2 marks)*
>
> **(iii) Total angular sweep:**
>
> $$\Delta\theta = \frac{\lambda \, \Delta f_s}{n \, v_s} = \frac{633 \times 10^{-9} \times 50 \times 10^6}{2.26 \times 4260}$$
>
> $$\Delta\theta = \frac{31.65 \times 10^{-3}}{9627.6} = 3.288 \times 10^{-6} \text{ rad} \;\; \text{???}$$
>
> **Correction — recompute step-by-step:**
>
> Numerator: $633 \times 10^{-9} \times 50 \times 10^6 = 633 \times 50 \times 10^{-3} = 31.65 \times 10^{-3} = 3.165 \times 10^{-2}$
>
> Denominator: $2.26 \times 4260 = 9627.6$
>
> $$\Delta\theta = \frac{3.165 \times 10^{-2}}{9627.6} = 3.288 \times 10^{-6} \text{ rad} = 3.288 \, \mu\text{rad}$$
>
> In degrees: $\Delta\theta = 3.288 \times 10^{-6} \times (180/\pi) = 1.884 \times 10^{-4} \,^{\circ}$
>
> *(1.5 marks)*
>
> **(iv) Number of resolvable spots:**
>
> $$N = \frac{D \, \Delta f_s}{n \, v_s} = \frac{0.01 \times 50 \times 10^6}{2.26 \times 4260}$$
>
> $$N = \frac{5 \times 10^5}{9627.6} \approx 51.93 \approx 52 \text{ resolvable spots}$$
>
> *(2 marks)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat energy-band diagram, explain the mechanism of injection electroluminescence in a forward-biased p–n junction LED. Derive the expression $\lambda = h c / E_g$ for the peak emission wavelength. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the construction and working of an AC powder electroluminescent (ACPEL) display cell. Discuss the role of the dielectric barrier layers and explain why a high-frequency AC drive (typically 50 Hz to 1 kHz) is essential. State two advantages and two limitations of ACPEL displays. **(7 marks)**

---

> **Model Solution to Question B:**

> **(a) Injection EL in a p–n junction (7 marks):**
> - **Energy band diagram:** Draw the conduction band $E_c$, valence band $E_v$, Fermi level $E_f$ on both p- and n-sides. Under forward bias, the n-side $E_c$ is lowered relative to the p-side $E_v$, allowing electrons to diffuse to the p-side and holes to the n-side. The injected minority carriers recombine radiatively across the depletion region, emitting photons of energy $E_g = E_c - E_v$.
> - **Derivation:** At the moment of recombination, an electron at $E_c$ transitions to an empty state at $E_v$. By energy conservation, $E_{photon} = E_c - E_v = E_g$. Since $E_{photon} = h\nu = h c / \lambda$, equate and solve:
> $$\lambda = \frac{h c}{E_g}$$
> Substituting $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s and $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s, with $E_g$ in electron-volts (1 eV = $1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ J):
> $$\lambda(\mu\text{m}) = \frac{1.24}{E_g(\text{eV})}$$
> *(Distribution: 2 marks for diagram, 2 marks for mechanism explanation, 1.5 marks for derivation, 1.5 marks for numerical formula)*
>
> **(b) ACPEL cell construction and working (7 marks):**
> - **Construction:** A thin phosphor layer (typically ZnS doped with Mn and co-doped with Cu for ease of excitation) is sandwiched between two dielectric layers (e.g., $\text{BaTiO}_3$, $\text{Y}_2\text{O}_3$). Above and below this sandwich are two electrodes — the front one is transparent (ITO) and the back is metallic (Al or Ag).
> - **Working:** When a high AC voltage (100–220 Vrms at 50 Hz–1 kHz) is applied, a strong electric field ($\sim 10^6$ V/m) develops across the phosphor. Electrons trapped at defect sites are released into the conduction band of ZnS by field ionization or tunneling. These electrons are accelerated by the field and impact-excite the $\text{Mn}^{2+}$ luminescent centers. The excited Mn centers then de-excite by emitting photons — typically yellow-orange at 585 nm. When the AC field reverses, electrons move in the opposite direction and the process repeats, so light is emitted twice per AC cycle.
> - **Role of dielectric layers:** They limit the current through the cell to a safe capacitive value, prevent dielectric breakdown of the phosphor, and localize the high field across the phosphor grains. Without them, the device would draw destructive currents and quickly fail.
> - **Why AC is essential:** DC operation would cause ionic migration of the activator (e.g., Cu ions) and lead to rapid degradation of the phosphor. AC operation continuously reverses the field, sweeping ions back and forth, ensuring uniform activator distribution and long life (often > 50,000 hours).
> - **Advantages:** (1) Cold light — no heat, no infrared, ideal for night-vision-compatible displays; (2) rugged, solid-state, no vacuum required, wide viewing angle.
> - **Limitations:** (1) Low luminous efficiency compared to LEDs; (2) requires high-voltage AC driver, complicating circuitry.
> *(Distribution: 2 marks for construction, 2 marks for mechanism, 1 mark for dielectric role, 1 mark for AC necessity, 1 mark for advantages/limitations)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Wrong units in numerical problems:** Forgetting to convert MHz to Hz, nm to m, or mm to m before substitution. This is a **silent killer** — even if the formula is correct, the numerical answer will be off by 6–12 orders of magnitude, and you get **zero marks for the numerical part**.
> 2. **Mixing up Klein-Cook inequalities:** KTU examiners specifically test whether you can correctly state $Q \ll 1$ for Raman–Nath and $Q \gg 1$ for Bragg. Writing them backwards costs 1 full mark.
> 3. **Omitting the index $n$ in the Bragg equation:** A very common error is to write $2\Lambda \sin\theta_B = \lambda$ when the question is in a medium of index $n$. The correct form is $2 n \Lambda \sin\theta_B = \lambda$ (optical path), or equivalently $2\Lambda \sin\theta_B = \lambda/n$ when $\lambda$ is the free-space wavelength.
> 4. **Confusing EL with PL:** In Part A, students often describe photoluminescence (light-induced light) instead of electroluminescence (current-induced light). Examiners notice immediately.
> 5. **Skipping the energy-band diagram in injection-EL questions:** A question that says "with the help of a neat energy-band diagram" without the diagram loses **2 marks minimum** — even if the text explanation is perfect.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ The **acousto-optic effect** is light–sound interaction; the sound wave acts as a moving phase grating.
- ✅ The **Bragg condition** is $2 n \Lambda \sin\theta_B = \lambda$ (or $2\Lambda \sin\theta_B = \lambda/n$ with $\lambda$ free-space).
- ✅ The **Bragg angle** for small angles: $\theta_B = \lambda f_s / (2 n v_s)$.
- ✅ The **deflection angle** of the first-order beam: $\Delta\theta = \lambda f_s / (n v_s)$.
- ✅ The **Klein–Cook parameter** $Q = 2\pi \lambda L / (n \Lambda^2)$: $Q \ll 1$ → Raman–Nath; $Q \gg 1$ → Bragg.
- ✅ The **number of resolvable spots** in an AO deflector: $N = D \Delta f_s / (n v_s)$.
- ✅ The **piezoelectric transducer** (often $\text{LiNbO}_3$) is the heart of any AO device — it converts RF into acoustic waves.
- ✅ **Electroluminescence** = direct conversion of electrical energy into light without thermal intermediates.
- ✅ **Two main types of EL:** (i) Injection EL (LEDs) — bandgap-driven; (ii) Intrinsic/High-field EL (AC powder panels) — impact-excitation-driven.
- ✅ **LED peak emission wavelength:** $\lambda = 1.24 / E_g$ (μm, with $E_g$ in eV).
- ✅ **ACPEL cell** = transparent electrode / dielectric / ZnS:Mn,Cu phosphor / dielectric / metal electrode.
- ✅ **Why AC drive in ACPEL:** Reverses field every half-cycle, prevents ionic migration, doubles emission per cycle, gives long lifetime.
- ✅ **Dielectric layers in ACPEL** limit current, localize field, prevent breakdown — they are **not optional**.
- ✅ **Engineering winners:** $\text{TeO}_2$ for AO (high $M_2$); ZnS:Mn for yellow-orange EL; GaAs for IR LEDs; GaAsP for red LEDs; GaN for blue/green/white LEDs.
- ✅ **AO applications:** AODs, AOMs, AOTFs, Q-switches, spectrum analyzers.
- ✅ **EL display applications:** Night-vision-compatible cockpit displays, instrument panels, OLED TVs, smartphone displays, indicator lamps.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Injection Luminescence
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT861 - OPTOELECTRONICS AND INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Injection Luminescence

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Injection Luminescence** is the radiative recombination process in which excess minority charge carriers are *electrically injected* across the depletion region of a forward-biased semiconductor p-n junction (or heterostructure), subsequently recombining with majority carriers to emit photons whose energy is approximately equal to the bandgap energy of the active region.

In the context of KTU 2024 Scheme Module 2, injection luminescence is the **fundamental operating principle of the Light Emitting Diode (LED)**. The word *luminescence* refers to any non-thermal light emission, and the qualifier *injection* specifically denotes the **electrical injection** mechanism (as opposed to optical pumping or electron-beam excitation).

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PEICT861 – Module 2):** Injection luminescence is introduced as a direct application of carrier recombination in direct bandgap semiconductors. Students must clearly distinguish it from photoluminescence (optical pumping) and cathodoluminescence (electron beam pumping).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine a **two-story building (energy bands)**:
- The **upper floor (Conduction Band)** is full of restless "kids" (electrons) sliding down a slide.
- The **lower floor (Valence Band)** is full of pillows (holes — empty seats).
- Between the floors is a **"staircase" called the Band Gap (Eg)**.

Now, suppose a **blower (forward bias voltage)** pushes more kids from the lower floor up to the upper floor, where they slide around for a while. Eventually, every kid on the upper floor must come back down to fill a pillow on the lower floor. Each "slide-down" releases a small packet of energy — in semiconductors, this packet is a **photon of light**.

This is exactly what happens in an LED under forward bias:
1. The forward-bias voltage lowers the built-in potential barrier.
2. Electrons are **injected** from the n-side into the p-side.
3. Holes are **injected** from the p-side into the n-side.
4. These injected minority carriers recombine radiatively with majority carriers across the bandgap, emitting photons of energy **ℏω ≈ Eg**.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following are the standard metrics and constants used throughout injection-luminescence analysis:

- **Planck's constant:** **h = 6.626 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s**
- **Reduced Planck's constant:** **ℏ = 1.054 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s**
- **Electron charge:** **q = 1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ C**
- **Bandgap Energy:** **Eg (in eV)**
- **Emission wavelength:** **λ (in nm or μm)**
- **Speed of light in vacuum:** **c = 3 × 10⁸ m/s**
- **Standard reference temperature:** **T = 300 K (room temperature)**
- **Boltzmann constant:** **kB = 1.381 × 10⁻²³ J/K**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physics Behind Injection Luminescence — Step-by-Step

The complete process of injection luminescence can be broken down into **five sequential physical stages**, each of which is critical for KTU examination answers.

### Step 1 — Application of Forward Bias
When a forward bias voltage **V** is applied across the p-n junction, the depletion width **W** shrinks, and the built-in potential barrier **Vbi** is reduced by an amount equal to the applied voltage. The new barrier height becomes **(Vbi − V)**.

### Step 2 — Minority Carrier Injection
Once the barrier is reduced, the diffusion current dominates. Electrons from the n-side diffuse (inject) into the p-side, and holes from the p-side diffuse into the n-side. The **minority carrier injection** is governed by the injected excess carrier density:
$$\Delta n_p (x) = \Delta n_p (0)\, e^{-x/L_n}$$

where **Ln** is the electron diffusion length in the p-region, and **Δnp(0)** is the injected carrier density at the depletion edge.

### Step 3 — Radiative Recombination
The injected minority carriers recombine with majority carriers. For *radiative* recombination, energy is released as a photon. The **radiative recombination rate** is given by:
$$R_{rad} = B \cdot n \cdot p$$

where **B** is the radiative recombination coefficient (cm³/s), and **n, p** are electron and hole concentrations.

### Step 4 — Non-Radiative Losses (Competing Process)
Some carriers recombine via **non-radiative** channels such as:
- **Shockley–Read–Hall (SRH) recombination** at defect states
- **Auger recombination** (energy given to a third carrier)
- **Surface recombination** at the device boundaries

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** The ratio of radiative to total recombination determines the **internal quantum efficiency (ηi)**, which is the single most important LED figure of merit.

### Step 5 — Photon Emission & Escape
The generated photons must escape the semiconductor without being reabsorbed. The probability of escape defines the **external quantum efficiency (ηext)**.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Quantity | Mathematical Expression | Unit / Value | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Emission wavelength (peak) | $\lambda = \dfrac{h c}{E_g} = \dfrac{1.24}{E_g\ (\text{eV})}\ \mu\text{m}$ | nm or μm | Peak photon wavelength |
| 2 | Photon energy | $E_g = h\nu = \dfrac{h c}{\lambda}$ | eV or J | Band-to-band transition energy |
| 3 | Injected minority carrier density | $\Delta n_p (x) = \Delta n_p (0)\, e^{-x/L_n}$ | cm⁻³ | Spatial decay of injected carriers |
| 4 | Minority carrier diffusion length | $L_n = \sqrt{D_n \tau_n}$ | μm | Distance carrier travels before recombining |
| 5 | Radiative recombination rate | $R_{rad} = B\, n\, p$ | cm⁻³ s⁻¹ | Rate of photon-generating events |
| 6 | Non-radiative SRH rate | $R_{SRH} = \dfrac{n\, p - n_i^2}{\tau_p (n + n_1) + \tau_n (p + p_1)}$ | cm⁻³ s⁻¹ | Defect-mediated recombination |
| 7 | Carrier lifetime | $\dfrac{1}{\tau} = \dfrac{1}{\tau_{rad}} + \dfrac{1}{\tau_{nr}}$ | s | Total = radiative + non-radiative |
| 8 | Internal quantum efficiency | $\eta_i = \dfrac{\tau_{nr}}{\tau_{rad} + \tau_{nr}} = \dfrac{R_{rad}}{R_{rad} + R_{nr}}$ | dimensionless (0–1) | Fraction of carrier recombinations that produce photons |
| 9 | External quantum efficiency | $\eta_{ext} = \eta_i \cdot \eta_{extraction}$ | dimensionless (0–1) | Fraction of photons that escape the device |
| 10 | Light extraction efficiency | $\eta_{extraction} \approx \dfrac{1}{4 n^2}$ (Fresnel limit) | dimensionless | For high-index semiconductors (e.g., GaAs, n≈3.5 → ~2%) |
| 11 | Output optical power | $P_{opt} = \eta_{ext} \cdot \dfrac{h c}{q\lambda} \cdot I$ | W | Optical power from injected current |
| 12 | Forward-bias diode current | $I = I_S \left(e^{qV/k_B T} - 1\right)$ | A | Standard Shockley diode equation |
| 13 | Built-in potential | $V_{bi} = \dfrac{k_B T}{q} \ln\left(\dfrac{N_A N_D}{n_i^2}\right)$ | V | Junction barrier at equilibrium |
| 14 | Direct vs Indirect bandgap condition | Direct: $\Delta k = 0$ (momentum-conserving) | — | Required for efficient injection luminescence |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL KTU Formula:** The single most-tested equation is the **peak emission wavelength**:
> $$\lambda_{\text{peak}}\ (\mu m) = \dfrac{1.24}{E_g\ (\text{eV})}$$
> For GaAs (Eg = 1.42 eV) → λ ≈ 0.87 μm. For GaP (Eg = 2.26 eV) → λ ≈ 0.55 μm (green).

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Injection luminescence is the foundational physics behind:

- **Light Emitting Diodes (LEDs):** Indicator lamps, traffic signals, displays, automotive lighting, general illumination.
- **Laser Diodes (LDs):** Optical fiber communication, CD/DVD/Blu-ray readers, LIDAR, laser pointers, surgical lasers.
- **Optical Interconnects:** Chip-to-chip and board-to-board high-speed data links.
- **Solid-State Lighting (SSL):** Replacing incandescent and fluorescent lamps with high-efficiency GaN/InGaN LEDs.
- **Visible Light Communication (VLC):** Li-Fi systems where LEDs serve as data transmitters.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For efficient injection luminescence, the semiconductor **MUST be a direct bandgap material** (e.g., GaAs, InP, GaN, InGaAs). Indirect materials like Si and Ge have very low radiative efficiency because the recombination requires a phonon to conserve momentum — this is why silicon cannot be used for efficient LEDs.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Peak Emission Wavelength from Bandgap Energy

### Statement to Prove
> The peak wavelength of photons emitted by injection luminescence is given by $\lambda = \dfrac{hc}{E_g}$.

### Derivation (Every Step Shown)

**Step 1 — Identify the energy released in radiative recombination.**
When an electron in the conduction band (energy Ec) recombines with a hole in the valence band (energy Ev), the energy released is exactly equal to the bandgap energy Eg:

$$E_{\text{released}} = E_C - E_V = E_g$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Planck–Einstein relation for photon energy.**
A photon of frequency ν carries energy:

$$E_{\text{photon}} = h \nu = \dfrac{h c}{\lambda}$$

**Step 3 — Equate the released energy to the photon energy.**
By conservation of energy, the bandgap energy must equal the photon energy:

$$E_g = \dfrac{h c}{\lambda}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for λ.**

$$\lambda = \dfrac{h c}{E_g}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute numerical values to obtain a useful practical form.**
Using h = 6.626 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s, c = 3 × 10⁸ m/s, and converting Eg from eV to joules via 1 eV = 1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ J:

$$\lambda = \dfrac{(6.626 \times 10^{-34})(3 \times 10^8)}{E_g \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19}}$$

$$\lambda = \dfrac{1.988 \times 10^{-25}}{E_g \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19}}\ \text{m}$$

$$\lambda = \dfrac{1.2408 \times 10^{-6}}{E_g}\ \text{m}$$

**Step 6 — Convert to micrometers for convenience.**

$$\boxed{\lambda\ (\mu m) = \dfrac{1.24}{E_g\ (\text{eV})}}$$

This is the **canonical KTU formula** for emission wavelength.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Internal Quantum Efficiency (ηᵢ)

### Statement to Prove
> The internal quantum efficiency of an LED is the ratio of radiative recombination rate to total recombination rate:
> $$\eta_i = \dfrac{1}{1 + \tau_{rad}/\tau_{nr}}$$

### Derivation (Every Step Shown)

**Step 1 — Express the total recombination rate.**
The total recombination rate is the sum of the radiative and non-radiative contributions. Each pathway has an associated lifetime:

$$R_{\text{total}} = R_{rad} + R_{nr} = \dfrac{\Delta n}{\tau_{rad}} + \dfrac{\Delta n}{\tau_{nr}} = \Delta n \left(\dfrac{1}{\tau_{rad}} + \dfrac{1}{\tau_{nr}}\right)$$

where Δn is the excess carrier density.

**Step 2 — Express the effective total lifetime τ.**

$$R_{\text{total}} = \dfrac{\Delta n}{\tau}$$

Equating with Step 1:

$$\dfrac{1}{\tau} = \dfrac{1}{\tau_{rad}} + \dfrac{1}{\tau_{nr}}$$

**Step 3 — Define internal quantum efficiency.**
By definition, ηᵢ is the probability that a recombination event produces a photon, i.e., the fraction of events that are radiative:

$$\eta_i = \dfrac{R_{rad}}{R_{total}} = \dfrac{\Delta n / \tau_{rad}}{\Delta n / \tau} = \dfrac{\tau}{\tau_{rad}}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the expression for τ from Step 2.**

$$\tau = \dfrac{1}{\dfrac{1}{\tau_{rad}} + \dfrac{1}{\tau_{nr}}} = \dfrac{\tau_{rad} \tau_{nr}}{\tau_{rad} + \tau_{nr}}$$

**Step 5 — Combine to get ηᵢ in its final form.**

$$\eta_i = \dfrac{\tau}{\tau_{rad}} = \dfrac{\tau_{nr}}{\tau_{rad} + \tau_{nr}} = \dfrac{1}{1 + \tau_{rad}/\tau_{nr}}$$

$$\boxed{\eta_i = \dfrac{1}{1 + \tau_{rad}/\tau_{nr}}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** To maximize ηᵢ, we need **τnr ≫ τrad**, i.e., the non-radiative lifetime must be much longer than the radiative lifetime. This is achieved by:
> 1. Using high-purity, defect-free direct-bandgap semiconductors.
> 2. Reducing dislocation density (especially in GaN-on-sapphire).
> 3. Passivating surface states.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: External Quantum Efficiency and Output Power

### Statement to Prove
> The optical output power of an LED is given by:
> $$P_{opt} = \eta_{ext} \cdot \dfrac{h c}{q \lambda} \cdot I$$

### Derivation (Every Step Shown)

**Step 1 — Relate the number of photons emitted to the number of carriers injected.**
Every electron passing through the diode carries charge q. If the current is I, the rate of carrier injection is:

$$\text{Injection rate} = \dfrac{I}{q}\ \text{(electrons per second)}$$

**Step 2 — Apply external quantum efficiency.**
Only a fraction ηext of the injected carriers result in photons that escape the device:

$$\text{Photon emission rate} = \eta_{ext} \cdot \dfrac{I}{q}$$

**Step 3 — Multiply by the energy per photon.**
Each photon carries energy hc/λ:

$$P_{opt} = \left(\eta_{ext} \cdot \dfrac{I}{q}\right) \cdot \dfrac{h c}{\lambda}$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange to the standard form.**

$$\boxed{P_{opt} = \eta_{ext} \cdot \dfrac{h c}{q \lambda} \cdot I}$$

### Numerical Example (Worked)

For a GaAs LED with Eg = 1.42 eV, λ ≈ 0.873 μm, and ηext = 2% (= 0.02), driven at I = 20 mA:

**Step A — Compute hc/(qλ):**

$$\dfrac{h c}{q \lambda} = \dfrac{(6.626 \times 10^{-34})(3 \times 10^8)}{(1.602 \times 10^{-19})(0.873 \times 10^{-6})}$$

**Step B — Evaluate numerator:**

$$h c = 6.626 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^8 = 1.9878 \times 10^{-25}\ \text{J·m}$$

**Step C — Evaluate denominator:**

$$q \lambda = 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \times 0.873 \times 10^{-6} = 1.3986 \times 10^{-25}\ \text{J·m/eV-eq}$$

**Step D — Divide:**

$$\dfrac{h c}{q \lambda} = \dfrac{1.9878 \times 10^{-25}}{1.3986 \times 10^{-25}} = 1.4212\ \text{V}$$

This is consistent with Eg/q = 1.42 eV, as expected (since hc/qλ = Eg/q = Eg in eV).

**Step E — Compute output power:**

$$P_{opt} = 0.02 \times 1.42 \times 0.020\ \text{W} = 5.68 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{W} = 0.568\ \text{mW}$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Minority Carrier Injection Profile

### Statement to Prove
> Under low-level forward-bias injection into a uniformly doped p-region, the steady-state excess minority carrier (electron) profile is:
> $$\Delta n_p (x) = \Delta n_p (0)\, e^{-x/L_n}$$
> where Ln = √(Dn τn).

### Derivation (Every Step Shown)

**Step 1 — Write the ambipolar minority-carrier diffusion equation in 1-D steady state:**

$$D_n \dfrac{d^2 \Delta n_p}{dx^2} - \dfrac{\Delta n_p}{\tau_n} = 0$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange to standard form:**

$$\dfrac{d^2 \Delta n_p}{dx^2} = \dfrac{\Delta n_p}{D_n \tau_n}$$

**Step 3 — Define the diffusion length:**

$$L_n \equiv \sqrt{D_n \tau_n}$$

Then:

$$\dfrac{d^2 \Delta n_p}{dx^2} = \dfrac{\Delta n_p}{L_n^2}$$

**Step 4 — Solve the second-order ODE.**
The general solution is:

$$\Delta n_p (x) = A\, e^{x/L_n} + B\, e^{-x/L_n}$$

**Step 5 — Apply boundary conditions.**
At x → ∞, Δnp must remain finite (no sources at infinity), so A = 0.
At x = 0 (depletion edge on p-side), the injected density is Δnp(0), giving B = Δnp(0).

**Step 6 — Final solution.**

$$\boxed{\Delta n_p (x) = \Delta n_p (0)\, e^{-x/L_n}}$$

**Step 7 — Relate Δnp(0) to the diode current.**
The diffusion current of injected electrons at x = 0 is:

$$J_n (0) = q D_n \left.\dfrac{d \Delta n_p}{dx}\right|_{x=0} = -\dfrac{q D_n \Delta n_p(0)}{L_n}$$

Therefore:

$$\Delta n_p (0) = \dfrac{J_n (0) L_n}{q D_n} = \dfrac{J_n (0) \tau_n}{q L_n}$$

This completes the derivation.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Injection Luminescence (Mermaid)

The following diagram illustrates the **block-level functional flow** of injection luminescence in a forward-biased p-n junction LED. It is rendered as a Mermaid flow-chart for clarity and exam-readiness.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Forward Bias Voltage V Applied Across p-n Junction] --> B[Built-in Potential Barrier Vbi Reduced to Vbi - V]
    B --> C[Depletion Region Width W Shrinks]
    C --> D[Electrons Injected from n-side into p-side]
    C --> E[Holes Injected from p-side into n-side]
    D --> F[Excess Minority Carriers Delta n in p-region]
    E --> G[Excess Minority Carriers Delta p in n-region]
    F --> H[Diffusion of Injected Carriers over Diffusion Length Ln]
    H --> I{Radiative or Non-Radiative Recombination?}
    I -- Radiative Path --> J[Band-to-Band Transition Releases Photon of Energy hc/lambda approx Eg]
    I -- Non-Radiative Path --> K[Energy Dissipated as Heat via SRH or Auger]
    J --> L[Photon Travels Toward Semiconductor Surface]
    L --> M{Critical Angle Condition Satisfied?}
    M -- Yes --> N[Photon Escapes Semiconductor - Contributes to Output Power Popt]
    M -- No --> O[Total Internal Reflection - Photon Trapped and Reabsorbed]
    O --> P[Energy Lost - Reduces External Efficiency]
    N --> Q[Final Output: Coherent or Incoherent Light Emission from LED]
    P --> Q
    K --> Q
```

## 4.2 Band Diagram Schematic (Mermaid Block Representation)

A physical energy-band diagram of a forward-biased p-n junction is rendered below as a Mermaid block-level schematic showing the **relative positions of conduction band (Ec), valence band (Ev), and Fermi level (Ef) on both sides** under forward bias.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph N_SIDE["N-SIDE (n-type)"]
        EC_N["Ec_n: Conduction Band Edge (high)"]
        EF_N["Ef_n: Fermi Level (near Ec)"]
        EV_N["Ev_n: Valence Band Edge (low)"]
    end
    subgraph DEPLETION["DEPLETION REGION (Narrowed under Forward Bias)"]
        BAND_BEND["Bands bend downward from n to p"]
        EFIELD["Built-in Electric Field Reduced"]
    end
    subgraph P_SIDE["P-SIDE (p-type)"]
        EC_P["Ec_p: Conduction Band Edge (lower than n-side)"]
        EF_P["Ef_p: Fermi Level (near Ev)"]
        EV_P["Ev_p: Valence Band Edge (high)"]
    end
    EF_N -.->|"qV shift (forward bias)"| EF_P
    EC_N === BAND_BEND
    BAND_BEND === EC_P
    EV_N === BAND_BEND
    BAND_BEND === EV_P
    note1["Forward bias tilts bands: electrons slide down Ec slope into p-region and recombine with holes in Ev, emitting photon of energy ~Eg"]
    BAND_BEND -.-> note1
```

## 4.3 Recombination Process Flow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Injected Electron in Conduction Band of p-side] --> Drift[Thermalizes to Band Edge via Phonon Emission]
    Drift --> Recomb{J type of Recombination?}
    Recomb -- Radiative Direct --> BB[Band-to-Band: h nu approx Eg]
    Recomb -- Radiative Indirect --> DA[Donor-Acceptor Pair: h nu slightly less than Eg]
    Recomb -- Non-Radiative --> SRH[SRH via Defect Level in Gap]
    Recomb -- Non-Radiative --> AUG[Auger: Energy to Third Carrier]
    BB --> Photon[Photon Emission]
    DA --> Photon
    SRH --> Heat[Heat Dissipation]
    AUG --> Heat
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** When asked to "draw the energy band diagram" in a KTU exam, always label:
> 1. Ec and Ev on both sides.
> 2. Ef and its relative position (near Ec on n-side, near Ev on p-side).
> 3. The built-in potential Vbi.
> 4. The applied forward bias V.
> 5. The direction of band bending.
> 6. The recombination event as an arrow from Ec to Ev.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A.1 — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Define injection luminescence. Why is it preferred in direct bandgap semiconductors?**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):

**Definition (1.5 marks):**
Injection luminescence is the process in which excess minority charge carriers are electrically injected across the depletion region of a forward-biased p-n junction, and these carriers recombine radiatively with majority carriers, emitting photons of energy approximately equal to the bandgap energy Eg of the active region.

**Reason for direct bandgap preference (1.5 marks):**
In a direct bandgap semiconductor, the conduction band minimum and the valence band maximum occur at the **same value of crystal momentum k**. Therefore, radiative recombination can occur without the participation of a phonon (no momentum change is required, Δk = 0). This makes the radiative recombination probability very high. In contrast, indirect bandgap semiconductors require a phonon for momentum conservation, making radiative recombination very inefficient. Hence, injection luminescence is practical only in direct bandgap materials such as GaAs, InP, and GaN.

---

### Question A.2 — `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**Derive the relationship between peak emission wavelength and bandgap energy of an LED.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):

In a radiative band-to-band transition, the energy released when an electron drops from the conduction band edge (Ec) to the valence band edge (Ev) is the bandgap energy Eg:

$$E_g = E_C - E_V$$

The energy of the emitted photon is given by the Planck relation:

$$E_{\text{photon}} = h \nu = \dfrac{h c}{\lambda}$$

By conservation of energy, equating both expressions:

$$E_g = \dfrac{h c}{\lambda}$$

Solving for λ:

$$\boxed{\lambda = \dfrac{h c}{E_g} = \dfrac{1.24}{E_g\ (\text{eV})}\ \mu m}$$

This shows that **higher bandgap energy produces shorter (bluer) wavelength emission**.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme ESE Module Examinations allow **internal choice** at the module level. Students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

### Question A — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2 Choice 1]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the mechanism of injection luminescence in a forward-biased p-n junction LED, with the help of an energy band diagram. Discuss the role of direct and indirect bandgap semiconductors. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for the peak emission wavelength of an LED. A GaAs LED has a bandgap of 1.42 eV. Calculate the peak emission wavelength. Compare it with a GaN LED (Eg = 3.4 eV). Comment on the color of emission in each case. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Mechanism of Injection Luminescence (7 marks):**

**1. Construction of the p-n junction (1 mark):**
A p-n junction is formed by joining a p-type semiconductor (excess holes) and an n-type semiconductor (excess electrons). At thermal equilibrium, a depletion region forms with a built-in electric field and built-in potential Vbi that opposes further diffusion of carriers. The Fermi level Ef is constant across the junction at equilibrium.

**2. Application of forward bias (1 mark):**
When an external voltage V is applied with the p-side positive and the n-side negative (forward bias), the external field opposes the built-in field. The potential barrier is reduced from Vbi to (Vbi − V). The depletion width W decreases, and the bands on the p-side are shifted downward relative to the n-side by an amount qV.

**3. Carrier injection (2 marks):**
The reduced barrier enables the diffusion of majority carriers across the junction:
- **Electrons** from the conduction band of the n-side are injected into the p-side, where they become **excess minority carriers** (Δnp).
- **Holes** from the valence band of the p-side are injected into the n-side, where they become **excess minority carriers** (Δpn).

The injected density profile in the p-region decays exponentially:
$$\Delta n_p (x) = \Delta n_p (0)\, e^{-x/L_n}$$

**4. Radiative recombination and photon emission (2 marks):**
The injected electrons in the p-region recombine with the abundant holes. In a **direct bandgap** material (e.g., GaAs, GaN), this is a vertical transition in k-space (no phonon needed), releasing a photon of energy:
$$h\nu \approx E_g = E_C - E_V$$

In an **indirect bandgap** material (e.g., Si, Ge), the conduction band minimum and valence band maximum occur at different k-values. Recombination requires a simultaneous phonon to conserve momentum, making photon emission extremely inefficient. Hence, **only direct bandgap semiconductors are used for practical LEDs**.

**5. Photon escape and output (1 mark):**
The emitted photons must reach the semiconductor-air interface at an angle below the critical angle θc = sin⁻¹(1/n) to escape. Otherwise, they undergo total internal reflection and are reabsorbed, limiting the **external quantum efficiency**.

**Energy Band Diagram (to be drawn by student):**
Show: n-side Ec, Ef near Ec, Ev; p-side Ec, Ef near Ev, Ev; forward bias tilts bands; arrow from Ec (n) to Ev (p) representing the recombination event and photon emission.

**(b) Derivation and Calculation (7 marks):**

**Derivation of peak emission wavelength (3 marks):**

From conservation of energy in a band-to-band transition:
$$E_g = h\nu = \dfrac{h c}{\lambda}$$

Solving for λ:
$$\lambda = \dfrac{h c}{E_g}$$

Substituting h = 6.626 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s, c = 3 × 10⁸ m/s, and 1 eV = 1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ J:

$$\lambda\ (\mu m) = \dfrac{1.24}{E_g\ (\text{eV})}$$

**For GaAs LED (Eg = 1.42 eV) — 2 marks:**

$$\lambda_{GaAs} = \dfrac{1.24}{1.42} = 0.873\ \mu m = 873\ \text{nm}$$

This lies in the **near-infrared (NIR)** region, just beyond the visible red. It is **invisible to the human eye**, but ideal for optical-fiber communication (since silica fibers have low loss near 850 nm and 1310 nm).

**For GaN LED (Eg = 3.4 eV) — 2 marks:**

$$\lambda_{GaN} = \dfrac{1.24}{3.4} = 0.365\ \mu m = 365\ \text{nm}$$

This lies in the **ultraviolet (UV)** region. Pure GaN is not used directly for visible LEDs; instead, **InGaN alloys** (with lower Eg) are used to access blue (~470 nm), green (~530 nm), and white (using phosphors).

**Comparison and Color Comment:**
- GaAs emits at 873 nm — **invisible (NIR)**, used in remote controls, optical-fiber links, and infrared illumination.
- GaN emits at 365 nm — **ultraviolet**, used in UV sterilization, water purification, and (with In alloying) blue/white LEDs.

---

### Question B — `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 2 Choice 2]`

**(a)** What is internal quantum efficiency? Derive an expression for it in terms of carrier lifetimes. Discuss the factors affecting the external quantum efficiency of an LED. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat sketch, explain the I–V characteristics of an LED. A GaAs LED has internal quantum efficiency of 60% and extraction efficiency of 30%. If it is driven by a forward current of 25 mA at a peak emission wavelength of 873 nm, calculate the optical output power. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Internal Quantum Efficiency and Factors (7 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
**Internal quantum efficiency (ηᵢ)** is defined as the ratio of the number of photons generated inside the semiconductor (via radiative recombination) to the number of electrons injected into the device (carriers crossing the junction):

$$\eta_i = \dfrac{\text{Number of photons generated internally per second}}{\text{Number of electrons injected per second}}$$

**Derivation (3 marks):**
The total recombination rate per unit volume is the sum of radiative and non-radiative contributions:

$$R_{total} = R_{rad} + R_{nr} = \dfrac{\Delta n}{\tau_{rad}} + \dfrac{\Delta n}{\tau_{nr}}$$

Define the effective lifetime τ such that $R_{total} = \Delta n / \tau$, giving:

$$\dfrac{1}{\tau} = \dfrac{1}{\tau_{rad}} + \dfrac{1}{\tau_{nr}}$$

The internal quantum efficiency is the fraction of recombinations that produce photons:

$$\eta_i = \dfrac{R_{rad}}{R_{total}} = \dfrac{\Delta n/\tau_{rad}}{\Delta n/\tau} = \dfrac{\tau}{\tau_{rad}}$$

Substituting the expression for τ:

$$\eta_i = \dfrac{1/\tau_{rad}}{1/\tau_{rad} + 1/\tau_{nr}} = \dfrac{\tau_{nr}}{\tau_{rad} + \tau_{nr}} = \dfrac{1}{1 + \tau_{rad}/\tau_{nr}}$$

To maximize ηᵢ, we require τnr ≫ τrad (long non-radiative lifetime, short radiative lifetime).

**Factors affecting external quantum efficiency (3 marks):**
The external quantum efficiency is given by $\eta_{ext} = \eta_i \times \eta_{extraction}$, where ηextraction is the fraction of internally generated photons that escape the device. The factors are:

1. **Refractive index mismatch (Fresnel loss):** The high refractive index of semiconductors (e.g., n ≈ 3.5 for GaAs) causes a small critical angle θc ≈ sin⁻¹(1/n) ≈ 16.6°. Only ~2% of photons satisfy the escape cone condition; the rest undergo total internal reflection and are reabsorbed.

2. **Reabsorption:** Photons emitted downward into the substrate may be reabsorbed, especially in direct bandgap materials where the absorption edge is sharp.

3. **Surface roughness and dome shaping:** Texturing the LED surface or shaping the chip into a hemisphere or truncated pyramid increases the escape probability.

4. **Non-radiative recombination centers:** Defects, dislocations, and surface states reduce ηᵢ.

5. **Auger recombination:** Significant at high injection levels and in narrow-bandgap materials; reduces the radiative fraction.

6. **Doping concentration:** Heavy doping can introduce non-radiative Auger channels and free-carrier absorption.

7. **Temperature:** Higher temperature increases non-radiative recombination rates, reducing ηᵢ.

**(b) I–V Characteristics and Output Power Calculation (7 marks):**

**I–V Characteristics (3 marks):**
The current-voltage relationship of an LED follows the Shockley diode equation:

$$I = I_S \left( e^{qV / k_B T} - 1 \right)$$

For forward bias (V > 0) and V >> kBT/q:
$$I \approx I_S e^{qV / k_B T}$$

A **sketch** to be drawn by student showing:
- For V < Vthreshold (cut-in voltage, ~1.2–3 V depending on material): very small reverse saturation current IS.
- For V > Vthreshold: current rises exponentially.
- The turn-on voltage Vthreshold is approximately equal to Eg/q (i.e., the bandgap voltage).
- For GaAs (Eg = 1.42 eV), Vthreshold ≈ 1.4 V.
- For GaN (Eg = 3.4 eV), Vthreshold ≈ 3.0 V.

**Calculation of Output Power (4 marks):**

**Given:**
- Internal quantum efficiency: ηᵢ = 60% = 0.60
- Extraction efficiency: ηextraction = 30% = 0.30
- Forward current: I = 25 mA = 25 × 10⁻³ A
- Peak emission wavelength: λ = 873 nm = 0.873 × 10⁻⁶ m

**Step 1 — External quantum efficiency:**
$$\eta_{ext} = \eta_i \times \eta_{extraction} = 0.60 \times 0.30 = 0.18$$

**Step 2 — Photon energy:**
$$E_{photon} = \dfrac{h c}{\lambda} = \dfrac{(6.626 \times 10^{-34})(3 \times 10^8)}{0.873 \times 10^{-6}}$$
$$E_{photon} = \dfrac{1.9878 \times 10^{-25}}{0.873 \times 10^{-6}} = 2.277 \times 10^{-19}\ \text{J}$$

In electron-volts: $E_{photon} = 2.277 \times 10^{-19} / 1.602 \times 10^{-19} = 1.42\ \text{eV}$ ✓ (matches Eg)

**Step 3 — Output power formula:**
$$P_{opt} = \eta_{ext} \cdot \dfrac{h c}{q \lambda} \cdot I = \eta_{ext} \cdot \dfrac{E_g}{q} \cdot I$$

**Step 4 — Substitute values:**
$$P_{opt} = 0.18 \times 1.42 \times 25 \times 10^{-3}$$
$$P_{opt} = 0.18 \times 0.0355\ \text{W}$$
$$P_{opt} = 6.39 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{W} = 6.39\ \text{mW}$$

**Final Answer:** The optical output power of the LED is **6.39 mW**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Common Mark-Deduction Zones):**
> 1. **Forgetting units:** The wavelength formula λ (μm) = 1.24/Eg (eV) requires Eg in **eV** and gives λ in **μm**. Mixing units is the #1 error.
> 2. **Confusing internal vs external quantum efficiency:** ηᵢ is the *material property* (radiative vs non-radiative), while ηext includes the *geometric light extraction*. KTU specifically tests this distinction.
> 3. **Drawing band diagrams without labels:** A band diagram without labeled Ec, Ev, Ef, Vbi, and the applied bias V will lose at least 2 marks.
> 4. **Skipping the sign of bias:** Always state explicitly that injection luminescence requires **forward bias**. Under reverse bias, the device does not emit light (it may detect light — that is a photodiode).
> 5. **Using indirect bandgap materials in design examples:** Si and Ge LEDs are theoretical curiosities only; always use direct bandgap examples (GaAs, InP, GaN, InGaN) in numerical problems.

---

## 5.X Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Use this section as your last-minute revision sheet before the exam.**

- ✅ **Injection luminescence** = Electrical injection of minority carriers + radiative recombination in a forward-biased p-n junction.
- ✅ It is the **operating principle of LEDs and laser diodes**.
- ✅ **Direct bandgap semiconductors are mandatory** (GaAs, InP, GaN, InGaN). Indirect materials (Si, Ge) are unsuitable due to low radiative efficiency.
- ✅ **Peak emission wavelength:** $\lambda (\mu m) = \dfrac{1.24}{E_g\ (\text{eV})}$
- ✅ **Photons are emitted** with energy $\approx E_g$ via band-to-band recombination.
- ✅ **Carrier injection profile:** $\Delta n_p (x) = \Delta n_p (0)\, e^{-x/L_n}$, where $L_n = \sqrt{D_n \tau_n}$.
- ✅ **Internal quantum efficiency:** $\eta_i = \dfrac{\tau_{nr}}{\tau_{rad} + \tau_{nr}}$. Maximized when τnr ≫ τrad.
- ✅ **External quantum efficiency:** $\eta_{ext} = \eta_i \times \eta_{extraction}$.
- ✅ **Output power:** $P_{opt} = \eta_{ext} \cdot \dfrac{E_g}{q} \cdot I$.
- ✅ **I–V relation:** $I = I_S (e^{qV/k_B T} - 1)$; turn-on voltage ≈ Eg/q.
- ✅ **Critical angle for escape:** $\theta_c = \sin^{-1}(1/n)$. For high-index semiconductors, only ~2% of photons escape without special shaping.
- ✅ **Forward bias required:** Without forward bias, no injection, no luminescence. Reverse bias may be used for photodetection.
- ✅ **Common materials and emission:**
  - GaAs (1.42 eV) → 873 nm (NIR)
  - AlGaAs (1.8–2.2 eV) → 560–690 nm (red)
  - InGaN (2.5–3.0 eV) → 410–500 nm (blue/green)
  - GaP (2.26 eV) → 550 nm (green, with N doping)
- ✅ **Two key recombination losses:** SRH (defect-mediated) and Auger (three-carrier process) — both non-radiative.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Light Emitting Diode
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT861 - OPTOELECTRONICS AND INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Light Emitting Diode

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Light Emitting Diode (LED)** is a two-terminal, forward-biased **p–n junction optoelectronic device** fabricated from a **direct bandgap semiconductor** that converts **electrical energy into incoherent optical radiation** through the mechanism of **spontaneous radiative recombination** of injected electrons and holes across the depletion region.

In formal KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, an LED belongs to the family of **injection electroluminescent devices** and is governed by the emission principle:

$$h\nu \;\approx\; E_g \;\approx\; qV_f$$

where $E_g$ is the semiconductor bandgap energy, $V_f$ is the forward bias voltage, $q$ is the elementary charge, and $h\nu$ is the energy of the emitted photon.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **two-storey building** with people crowded on the upper floor and an empty lower floor. If you provide a controlled staircase connecting them, the people naturally walk down — and every time someone "drops" from the upper floor to the lower floor, they release energy (sound, heat, vibration). 

Now imagine that instead of losing the energy as heat, every person who drops releases it as a **single packet of light** (a photon). That staircase is the **p–n junction**, the upper floor is the **conduction band**, the lower floor is the **valence band**, and the energy gap between floors is the **bandgap $E_g$**. The color of the light depends purely on the height of the drop (the bandgap).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key takeaway:** LEDs *only* work efficiently with **direct bandgap** semiconductors (e.g., GaAs, InP, GaN) because in such materials electrons can drop directly from conduction to valence band without needing a phonon to conserve momentum — making photon emission highly probable.

### 1.3 Standard Physical Constants

| Symbol | Quantity | Value |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **$h$** | Planck's constant | **$6.626 \times 10^{-34}\ \text{J·s}$** |
| **$c$** | Speed of light in vacuum | **$2.998 \times 10^{8}\ \text{m/s}$** |
| **$q$** | Elementary charge | **$1.602 \times 10^{-19}\ \text{C}$** |
| **$hc$** | Photon energy constant | **$1.2398\ \mu\text{m·eV}$** |
| **$k_B$** | Boltzmann constant | **$1.381 \times 10^{-23}\ \text{J/K}$** |

---

### 1.4 Classification of LEDs (KTU Syllabus View)

> [!NOTE]
> Per KTU 2024 syllabus categorization, LEDs are classified by:
> - **Emission wavelength:** Infrared LED (IRED), Visible LED (Red → Violet)
> - **Junction type:** Homojunction LED, Heterojunction LED (DH — Double Heterojunction)
> - **Output geometry:** Surface-emitting LED (SLED / Burrus-type), Edge-emitting LED (ELED)
> - **Material system:** III-V (GaAs, GaP, AlGaAs, InGaN), IV-IV (SiC), and II-VI based

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

The operation of an LED follows a strictly ordered physical sequence. Understanding each step is critical for both conceptual questions and numerical derivations in KTU exams.

**Step 1 — Forward Bias Injection**
When an external voltage $V_f \ge E_g/q$ is applied across the p–n junction with the **p-side positive**:
- Electrons from the n-region are injected into the p-region.
- Holes from the p-region are injected into the n-region.

**Step 2 — Minority Carrier Diffusion**
The injected minority carriers diffuse across the **depletion region** (or the active region in DH-LEDs) and form a high concentration of excess carriers near the junction.

**Step 3 — Radiative Recombination**
An electron in the conduction band recombines with a hole in the valence band. In a **direct bandgap semiconductor**, the momentum is conserved without phonon assistance, and the energy is released as a **photon** with energy:

$$h\nu \;=\; E_c - E_v \;=\; E_g$$

**Step 4 — Photon Escape**
The photon travels through the bulk semiconductor. Due to the **high refractive index** of semiconductors ($n \approx 2.5$ to $3.5$), only photons incident within a narrow **escape cone** (critical angle $\theta_c = \sin^{-1}(1/n)$) can exit the device — the rest undergo total internal reflection (TIR) and are eventually absorbed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the *Why* matters:** The escape cone limit is the single biggest reason why **external quantum efficiency ($\eta_{ext}$)** is so much smaller than **internal quantum efficiency ($\eta_i$)**. KTU examiners love testing this!

---

### 2.2 Energy Band Diagram (Textual Description)

Under forward bias, the bands on the p-side bend downward, and electrons from the n-side conduction band tunnel/diffuse across the junction into the **p-side conduction band**. They then "fall" through the bandgap to the valence band, emitting photons.

- In a **homojunction LED**, recombination occurs over a wide region (≈ diffusion length, $\sim 1$ to $10\ \mu\text{m}$).
- In a **double heterojunction (DH) LED**, the active region is sandwiched between wider-bandgap materials, **confining both carriers and photons** to a thin active layer ($\sim 0.1\ \mu\text{m}$) — drastically improving $\eta_i$.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is a **high-density cheat sheet** that covers every equation the KTU 2024 examiner can possibly ask from this topic.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units / Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $h\nu = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda} = qV \approx E_g$ | Photon energy equals bandgap | $E_g$ in eV |
| 2 | $\lambda(\mu m) = \dfrac{1.24}{E_g(\text{eV})}$ | Peak emission wavelength | $\mu m$ |
| 3 | $E_g(\text{eV}) = \dfrac{1.24}{\lambda(\mu m)}$ | Bandgap from peak wavelength | eV |
| 4 | $\eta_i = \dfrac{\text{radiative recomb. rate}}{\text{total recomb. rate}} = \dfrac{R_r}{R_r + R_{nr}}$ | Internal Quantum Efficiency | Dimensionless |
| 5 | $\eta_i = \dfrac{\tau_{nr}}{\tau_r + \tau_{nr}}$ | IQE in terms of carrier lifetimes | $\tau_r, \tau_{nr}$ |
| 6 | $\eta_{ext} = \eta_i \times \eta_{\text{extraction}}$ | External Quantum Efficiency | $\eta_{\text{extraction}} \le 2\% / n^2$ |
| 7 | $\eta_{\text{extraction}} = \dfrac{1}{2}\left(1 - \cos\theta_c\right)$ | Snell's escape cone fraction | $\theta_c = \sin^{-1}(1/n)$ |
| 8 | $\eta_{power} = \dfrac{\text{optical power out}}{\text{electrical power in}} = \dfrac{P_o}{I_f V_f}$ | Power conversion efficiency | Dimensionless |
| 9 | $\eta_L = \dfrac{\Phi_V}{P_{elec}} = \dfrac{F(\lambda)\cdot \eta_{ext}}{V_f}$ | Luminous efficiency | lumens/Watt |
| 10 | $P_o = \eta_{ext}\cdot (h\nu/q)\cdot I_f$ | Optical output power | Watts |
| 11 | $B = \dfrac{R_{sp}}{\Delta n}$ | Radiative recombination coefficient | $\text{cm}^3/\text{s}$ |
| 12 | $\theta_c = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{n_2}{n_1}\right)$ | Critical angle for total internal reflection | radians/degrees |

> **Engineering Utility:**
> - **$\lambda$ vs $E_g$ relation** → Material selection for desired color.
> - **$\eta_{extraction}$** → Drives packaging innovations (domes, lenses, textured surfaces).
> - **$\eta_L$ (lm/W)** → Industry figure-of-merit for visible LEDs (modern white LEDs reach $\eta_L > 300\ \text{lm/W}$).
> - **DH structures** → Underpin all modern high-brightness LEDs (GaN/InGaN blue LEDs, Nobel Prize 2014).

---

### 2.4 Material–Wavelength Mapping (Frequently Asked)

| Material | Bandgap (eV) | Peak Emission | Color / Spectral Region |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **GaAs** | 1.42 | **$\lambda \approx 870\ \text{nm}$** | Near-Infrared (NIR) |
| **$\text{Al}_{0.3}\text{Ga}_{0.7}\text{As}$** | 1.81 | **$\approx 685\ \text{nm}$** | Red |
| **GaAs$_{0.6}$P$_{0.4}$** | 1.91 | **$\approx 650\ \text{nm}$** | Red |
| **GaAs$_{0.35}$P$_{0.65}$** | 2.10 | **$\approx 590\ \text{nm}$** | Yellow |
| **GaP (Zn,O doped)** | 2.26 | **$\approx 565\ \text{nm}$** | Green |
| **SiC** | 2.96 | **$\approx 420\ \text{nm}$** | Blue |
| **InGaN / GaN** | 2.6 – 3.4 | **$\approx 450$ – $530\ \text{nm}$** | Blue / Green |
| **AlGaInP** | tunable | **$\approx 590$ – $630\ \text{nm}$** | Amber / Red |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I — Relation Between Bandgap Energy and Peak Emission Wavelength

This is the **most-asked derivation** in KTU ESE for the Optoelectronics module.

**Starting Statement:**
The energy of an emitted photon is equal to the semiconductor bandgap energy.

$$h\nu \;=\; E_g$$

**Step 1:** Write photon frequency in terms of wavelength using $\nu = c / \lambda$:

$$h \cdot \frac{c}{\lambda} \;=\; E_g$$

**Step 2:** Solve explicitly for wavelength $\lambda$:

$$\lambda \;=\; \frac{hc}{E_g}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute the numerical values of the constants ($h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}\ \text{J·s}$, $c = 2.998 \times 10^{8}\ \text{m/s}$, $1\ \text{eV} = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}\ \text{J}$):

$$\lambda \;=\; \frac{(6.626 \times 10^{-34})(2.998 \times 10^{8})}{E_g \,[\text{J}]}$$

**Step 4:** Convert $E_g$ from joules to electron-volts to obtain the standard form used by every engineer:

$$\lambda(\mu m) \;=\; \frac{1.24}{E_g(\text{eV})}$$

> **Conversion logic check:**
> $hc = 6.626 \times 10^{-34} \times 2.998 \times 10^{8} = 1.986 \times 10^{-25}\ \text{J·m}$
> Dividing by $1.602 \times 10^{-19}\ \text{J/eV}$ gives $1.2398 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{eV·m} = 1.24\ \mu\text{m·eV}$ ∎

---

### 3.2 Derivation II — Internal Quantum Efficiency in Terms of Carrier Lifetimes

**Starting Statement:**
The internal quantum efficiency $\eta_i$ is the ratio of radiative recombination rate to total recombination rate:

$$\eta_i \;=\; \frac{R_r}{R_r + R_{nr}}$$

**Step 1:** Write each recombination rate as the excess carrier density $\Delta n$ divided by the respective lifetime:

$$R_r \;=\; \frac{\Delta n}{\tau_r} \qquad \text{and} \qquad R_{nr} \;=\; \frac{\Delta n}{\tau_{nr}}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute into the definition:

$$\eta_i \;=\; \frac{\dfrac{\Delta n}{\tau_r}}{\dfrac{\Delta n}{\tau_r} + \dfrac{\Delta n}{\tau_{nr}}}$$

**Step 3:** Cancel the common $\Delta n$ from numerator and denominator:

$$\eta_i \;=\; \frac{\dfrac{1}{\tau_r}}{\dfrac{1}{\tau_r} + \dfrac{1}{\tau_{nr}}}$$

**Step 4:** Multiply numerator and denominator by $\tau_r \cdot \tau_{nr}$ to simplify:

$$\eta_i \;=\; \frac{\tau_{nr}}{\tau_r + \tau_{nr}}$$

> **Interpretation:** For high $\eta_i$, the **non-radiative lifetime $\tau_{nr}$ must be very large** (i.e., non-radiative recombination centers must be minimized). This is why LED-grade semiconductors have extremely low dislocation densities.

---

### 3.3 Derivation III — External Quantum Efficiency from Escape Cone Geometry

**Step 1:** When a photon reaches the semiconductor–air interface, it escapes only if its angle of incidence $\theta_i$ is less than the critical angle $\theta_c$ given by:

$$\theta_c \;=\; \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{n_{\text{air}}}{n_{semi}}\right) \;=\; \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{1}{n}\right)$$

**Step 2:** The fraction of photons emitted isotropically that fall within this escape cone is the ratio of the spherical cap area to the full sphere:

$$\eta_{\text{extraction}} \;=\; \frac{2\pi R^2 (1 - \cos\theta_c)}{4\pi R^2} \;=\; \frac{1 - \cos\theta_c}{2}$$

**Step 3:** Combine with internal quantum efficiency:

$$\eta_{ext} \;=\; \eta_i \cdot \frac{1 - \cos\theta_c}{2}$$

**Step 4:** Numerical example: For GaAs, $n \approx 3.6$, so $\theta_c \approx 16.1°$, and:

$$\eta_{\text{extraction}} \;=\; \frac{1 - \cos(16.1°)}{2} \;=\; \frac{1 - 0.961}{2} \;\approx\; 1.97\%$$

> **This is the famous "2 percent problem" of bare semiconductor LEDs.** It is the primary engineering motivation for dome lenses, photon-recycling cavities, and surface texturing in modern packaged LEDs.

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Model Q&A Style)

**Problem:** An LED is fabricated from $\text{Al}_{x}\text{Ga}_{1-x}\text{As}$ with $E_g = 1.81\ \text{eV}$. Calculate:
(i) the peak emission wavelength,
(ii) the photon energy in joules,
(iii) the photon momentum.

**Solution:**

**(i) Peak wavelength:**

$$\lambda(\mu m) \;=\; \frac{1.24}{E_g(\text{eV})} \;=\; \frac{1.24}{1.81} \;\approx\; 0.685\ \mu m \;=\; 685\ \text{nm}$$

**(ii) Photon energy in joules:**

$$E \;=\; h\nu \;=\; \frac{hc}{\lambda} \;=\; \frac{(6.626 \times 10^{-34})(2.998 \times 10^{8})}{685 \times 10^{-9}}$$

$$E \;\approx\; 2.90 \times 10^{-19}\ \text{J} \;\approx\; 1.81\ \text{eV}$$

**(iii) Photon momentum:**

$$p \;=\; \frac{h}{\lambda} \;=\; \frac{6.626 \times 10^{-34}}{685 \times 10^{-9}} \;\approx\; 9.67 \times 10^{-28}\ \text{kg·m/s}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 LED Construction — Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

The following Mermaid diagram captures the **functional architecture flow** of a typical double-heterojunction (DH) surface-emitting LED — including carrier injection, recombination, photon generation, and the escape pathway.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["DC Forward Bias Source"] -->|"Vf supplied"| B["Metal Contact P-Side"]
    B --> C["P-Type Wide Bandgap Cladding Layer"]
    C -->|"Holes injected"| D["Active Region InGaAs Active Layer"]
    E["N-Type Wide Bandgap Cladding Layer"] -->|"Electrons injected"| D
    D -->|"Radiative recombination"| F["Photons Generated h nu equal to Eg"]
    F --> G["Photon Propagation"]
    G -->|"Within escape cone"| H["Photon Escapes to Air"]
    G -->|"Outside escape cone Total Internal Reflection"| I["Photon Reabsorbed or Lost"]
    H --> J["Output Light Beam"]
    I --> K["Heat Dissipation Substrate"]
    A -.->|"Current path"| E
```

> **How to read this diagram (for valuation purposes):**
> - Cladding layers (wide bandgap) confine both carriers and photons to the active region — this is the *heterojunction advantage*.
> - The escape cone branch shows why $\eta_{ext}$ is small; the lost photons contribute to heating.

---

### 4.2 LED Types Comparison — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    LED["Light Emitting Diode"] --> H["Homojunction LED"]
    LED --> DH["Double Heterojunction DH LED"]
    LED --> S["Surface Emitter SLED"]
    LED --> E["Edge Emitter ELED"]

    H --> H1["Single material pn junction"]
    H1 --> H2["Wide recombination zone"]
    H2 --> H3["Lower eta_i"]

    DH --> D1["Two wider Eg cladding layers"]
    D1 --> D2["Thin active region less than 0.1 micron"]
    D2 --> D3["Carrier confinement plus Photon confinement"]
    D3 --> D4["Higher eta_i Higher brightness"]

    S --> S1["Emission perpendicular to junction plane"]
    S1 --> S2["Lambertian pattern"]
    S2 --> S3["Used in displays indicators"]

    E --> E1["Emission parallel to junction plane"]
    E1 --> E2["Smaller beam divergence"]
    E2 --> E3["Used in fiber optic coupling"]
```

---

### 4.3 LED Characteristic Curves — I–V and L–I Behavior

The electrical and optical characteristics of an LED can be summarized as two curves:

| Curve | x-axis | y-axis | Equation (ideal) | Behaviour |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **I–V** | Forward voltage $V_f$ | Current $I_f$ | $I_f = I_s\left(e^{qV_f/\eta kT} - 1\right)$ | Exponential turn-on at $V_f \approx E_g/q$ |
| **L–I** | Forward current $I_f$ | Optical output $L$ | $L \propto I_f$ (linear region) | Linear until thermal roll-off |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU favourite question:** *"Sketch the I–V and L–I characteristics of an LED and identify the threshold voltage."* The threshold is at $V_f \approx E_g/q$ — for GaAs this is about **1.4 V**, for GaN blue about **2.7 V**.

---

### 4.4 Spectral Output Characteristics

A real LED does **not** emit a single wavelength. Due to thermal broadening of carrier energies around the band edge, the output spectrum follows a roughly **Gaussian profile** centred at $\lambda_p = 1.24/E_g$ with a spectral half-width $\Delta\lambda$ typically:

$$\Delta\lambda \;\approx\; 1.8\, k_B T \, \frac{\lambda_p^2}{hc}$$

- At room temperature ($T = 300\ \text{K}$, $k_BT \approx 0.026\ \text{eV}$), $\Delta\lambda$ is typically **30 – 100 nm** depending on $E_g$.
- Narrower spectra occur at low temperature or in quantum-well (QW) LEDs.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *Define an LED. Why are direct bandgap semiconductors preferred for LED fabrication?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A Light Emitting Diode (LED) is a forward-biased p–n junction device that emits incoherent light through spontaneous radiative recombination when electrons transition from the conduction band to the valence band, releasing a photon of energy $h\nu \approx E_g$.

**Why direct bandgap (1 mark each):**
1. In direct bandgap materials (e.g., GaAs, InP, GaN), the conduction band minimum and valence band maximum occur at the **same crystal momentum $k$**. Therefore, electron-hole recombination does not require phonon assistance to conserve momentum, and photon emission is highly probable.
2. In indirect bandgap materials (e.g., Si, Ge), the band edges occur at different $k$ values. The transition must involve a phonon, which makes radiative recombination extremely inefficient — making these materials unsuitable for light emission.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *State the relation between peak emission wavelength and bandgap energy of an LED material. A GaAs LED has $E_g = 1.42\ \text{eV}$. Find its peak emission wavelength.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The peak emission wavelength is given by:

$$\lambda_p(\mu m) = \frac{1.24}{E_g(\text{eV})}$$

Substituting $E_g = 1.42\ \text{eV}$:

$$\lambda_p = \frac{1.24}{1.42} \approx 0.873\ \mu m = 873\ \text{nm}$$

This lies in the **near-infrared region**.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Derive the relation between the peak emission wavelength $\lambda$ and the bandgap energy $E_g$ of an LED material. Explain clearly why the colour of emission depends on the semiconductor used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat energy band diagram, explain the working of a **Double Heterojunction (DH) LED**. Mention two advantages over a homojunction LED. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

**Derivation (5 marks):**
The energy of the photon emitted during electron-hole recombination equals the semiconductor bandgap:

$$h\nu = E_g$$

Since $\nu = c/\lambda$:

$$h\cdot \frac{c}{\lambda} = E_g$$

Rearranging for $\lambda$:

$$\lambda = \frac{hc}{E_g}$$

Substituting $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}\ \text{J·s}$, $c = 3 \times 10^8\ \text{m/s}$, and converting $E_g$ from joules to eV:

$$\boxed{\lambda(\mu m) = \frac{1.24}{E_g(\text{eV})}}$$

**Colour dependence (2 marks):**
Since $\lambda \propto 1/E_g$, materials with larger bandgaps emit shorter (bluer) wavelengths, and smaller bandgaps emit longer (redder) wavelengths. By choosing a semiconductor with the appropriate $E_g$, the engineer controls the emitted colour — e.g., GaAs ($E_g = 1.42\ \text{eV}$) → IR, GaP ($E_g = 2.26\ \text{eV}$) → green, GaN ($E_g \approx 3.4\ \text{eV}$) → UV/blue.

---

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

**Structure (2 marks):**
A DH LED sandwiches a **narrow bandgap active layer** (e.g., InGaAs, $E_{g1}$) between a **p-type wide bandgap layer** ($E_{g2}$) and an **n-type wide bandgap layer** ($E_{g3}$), with $E_{g2}, E_{g3} > E_{g1}$.

**Working — band diagram narrative (3 marks):**

Under forward bias:
- Electrons from the n-side and holes from the p-side are **injected into the narrow-gap active layer**.
- Because the cladding layers have higher bandgaps, they form **potential barriers** that confine both carriers within the thin active layer (carrier confinement).
- Radiative recombination occurs entirely within this thin region, producing high photon density.
- The refractive index difference between the active layer and claddings also creates an **optical waveguide**, confining photons until they exit (photon confinement).

**Two advantages over homojunction LED (2 marks):**
1. **Higher internal quantum efficiency** due to carrier confinement in the active region — recombination outside the active layer is suppressed.
2. **Higher radiant intensity** and narrower spectral output due to thin active region.
3. *(Optional 3rd point)* Lower threshold current and better high-frequency modulation response.

---

#### **Question B — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Alternative]**

**(a)** Define **internal quantum efficiency** and **external quantum efficiency** of an LED. Derive an expression for the internal quantum efficiency in terms of carrier lifetimes. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **escape cone limitation** of LEDs. A GaAs LED has refractive index $n = 3.6$. Calculate the critical angle and the fraction of photons that can escape. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

**Definitions (2 marks):**
- **Internal Quantum Efficiency ($\eta_i$):** Ratio of the rate of radiative recombination to the total recombination rate inside the semiconductor.
- **External Quantum Efficiency ($\eta_{ext}$):** Ratio of the number of photons emitted into free space per second to the number of electrons injected per second (i.e., number of photons escaping the device per injected carrier).

**Derivation (5 marks):**

$$\eta_i = \frac{R_r}{R_r + R_{nr}}$$

With $R_r = \Delta n/\tau_r$ and $R_{nr} = \Delta n/\tau_{nr}$:

$$\eta_i = \frac{\Delta n/\tau_r}{\Delta n/\tau_r + \Delta n/\tau_{nr}} = \frac{1/\tau_r}{1/\tau_r + 1/\tau_{nr}}$$

Multiplying numerator and denominator by $\tau_r \tau_{nr}$:

$$\boxed{\eta_i = \frac{\tau_{nr}}{\tau_r + \tau_{nr}}}$$

---

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

**Concept (3 marks):**
The semiconductor–air interface has a large refractive index mismatch. Photons striking the interface at angles greater than the **critical angle $\theta_c$** undergo total internal reflection (TIR) and are trapped inside the device, where they are eventually absorbed and contribute to heat.

**Calculations (4 marks):**

Critical angle:

$$\theta_c = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{1}{n}\right) = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{1}{3.6}\right) \approx \sin^{-1}(0.2778) \approx 16.14°$$

Fraction of photons escaping (assuming isotropic emission):

$$\eta_{\text{extraction}} = \frac{1 - \cos\theta_c}{2} = \frac{1 - \cos(16.14°)}{2} = \frac{1 - 0.9607}{2} \approx 1.97\%$$

Hence only about **2%** of internally generated photons escape — the rest are lost to TIR and re-absorption.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students commonly lose marks on LED questions:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch in $\lambda$–$E_g$ formula:** Always write $\lambda(\mu m) = 1.24/E_g(\text{eV})$. Using SI units directly will cost **1 mark**.
> 2. **Confusing internal vs external efficiency:** $\eta_i$ concerns carrier recombination physics; $\eta_{ext}$ includes optical escape losses. Examiners **deduct 2 marks** if these are interchanged.
> 3. **Forgetting the recombination rate expression** $R_r = \Delta n/\tau_r$ — this is the foundation of the lifetime derivation; omitting it loses marks even if final answer is correct.
> 4. **Writing $\theta_c = \sin^{-1}(n)$ instead of $\sin^{-1}(1/n)$** — a classic sign-error. Always verify which medium has the higher refractive index.
> 5. **Skipping band-diagram sketch in DH-LED questions** — even a hand-drawn schematic showing the active layer between two wider bandgap cladding layers scores full marks. A textual answer alone loses **2–3 marks**.
> 6. **Wrong $\eta_{extraction}$ formula:** Some students write $(1-\cos\theta_c)/4\pi$ — correct formula is **$(1-\cos\theta_c)/2$**.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — LED (KTU 2024 Scheme)**

- ✅ LED = forward-biased p–n junction **direct bandgap** semiconductor → spontaneous **electroluminescence**.
- ✅ Peak wavelength relation: **$\lambda(\mu m) = 1.24 / E_g(\text{eV})$**.
- ✅ Forward voltage threshold: **$V_f \approx E_g / q$**.
- ✅ Internal QE: **$\eta_i = \tau_{nr} / (\tau_r + \tau_{nr})$**.
- ✅ External QE: **$\eta_{ext} = \eta_i \cdot \eta_{\text{extraction}}$**.
- ✅ Escape cone fraction: **$\eta_{\text{extraction}} = (1 - \cos\theta_c)/2$**.
- ✅ Critical angle: **$\theta_c = \sin^{-1}(1/n)$**.
- ✅ **DH LED** > Homojunction LED (carrier + photon confinement → higher $\eta_i$).
- ✅ I–V curve: exponential turn-on; L–I curve: linear (until thermal saturation).
- ✅ Spectral half-width: **$\Delta\lambda \propto k_B T \cdot \lambda_p^2 / (hc)$**.
- ✅ Material–colour map: **GaAs → IR; AlGaAs → red; GaP → green; GaN/InGaN → blue/UV**.
- ✅ Luminous efficiency $\eta_L$ (lm/W) is the **industry figure-of-merit** for visible LEDs.
- ✅ Surface emitters give **Lambertian pattern**; edge emitters are used for **fiber coupling**.
- ✅ LEDs are **incoherent** sources (unlike LASER diodes).
- ✅ Modern high-brightness LEDs use **dome lenses** to overcome the ~2% escape cone loss.
- ✅ **Nobel Prize in Physics 2014** was awarded for the **blue GaN LED**, enabling white LEDs.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Plasma Displays
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEICT861 - OPTOELECTRONICS AND INSTRUMENTATION > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Plasma Displays # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition A **Plasma Display Panel (PDP)** is a flat-panel emissive display technology in which each picture element (pixel) is composed of minute gas-filled cells (typically a mixture of **Neon (Ne) and Xenon (Xe)**) sandwiched between two glass substrates. When an alternating high-frequency voltage is applied across the cell electrodes, the inert gas ionizes into a **plasma** state, releasing **ultraviolet (UV) photons** at characteristic wavelengths of **147 nm** and **173 nm**. These invisible UV photons strike a thin layer of red, green, and blue phosphors deposited inside the cell, which then re-emit the energy as visible light through photoluminescence. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Plasma displays belong to the family of **self-emissive** (or "emissive") displays, unlike LCDs which require a separate backlight. Every sub-pixel in a PDP is essentially a miniature fluorescent lamp controlled electronically. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Mini Neon Sign" Imagine a wall made up of millions of tiny, individually controlled **neon tubes**, each smaller than a grain of sand. Each tiny tube is coated inside with red, green, or blue phosphor paint. When you "switch on" one of these micro-tubes via an electrical signal, the noble gas inside sparks up, bathes the phosphor with invisible UV light, and the phosphor instantly glows in the desired color. By combining red, green, and blue glowing tubes side-by-side, you can build any color you want. By switching millions of them on and off rapidly, you get a full-color moving picture on a TV screen. **That is exactly how a Plasma TV works.** ## 1.3 The Plasma State — A Brief Reminder The word **"Plasma"** here does **NOT** refer to the fluid in blood.
