topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT861 - OPTOELECTRONICS AND INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Ruby Laser

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Ruby Laser** is the first successfully operated laser (demonstrated by **Theodore H. Maiman** in **1960** at Hughes Research Laboratories, USA). It is a **solid-state, optically pumped, three-level pulsed laser** that uses a synthetic ruby crystal — aluminium oxide ($\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3$) doped with approximately **0.03% to 0.05% by weight of chromium(III) ions ($\text{Cr}^{3+}$)** — as the active gain medium.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Ruby Laser is the prototype of an optically pumped solid-state laser and is the canonical example used in KTU board examinations to illustrate **three-level laser systems**, **optical pumping**, **population inversion**, and **stimulated emission of radiation**.

The characteristic output wavelength is a deep red color at **$\lambda = 694.3 \text{ nm}$**, corresponding to the $\text{R}_1$ fluorescence line of the $\text{Cr}^{3+}$ ion in the corundum ($\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3$) lattice.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy — "The Staircase Light Bulb"**

Imagine a multi-storey building where most people (atoms) live on the ground floor (ground state). The ground floor is dark — no light. Now imagine a powerful pump (a powerful sunlamp) suddenly floods the building from above, forcing people up the stairs to the third floor (excited state). Most of them quickly fall to the second floor (metastable state) where they get "stuck" because the stairs from the 2nd to the 1st floor are unusually steep and slow. Soon, almost everyone is crowded on the second floor — this unnatural crowd is the **population inversion**.

Now, a single spontaneous photon (a "starter tap") glides through the crowd. Every person it touches on the 2nd floor falls to the 1st floor in a synchronized step, releasing a copy of the same photon. The single tap becomes a roar of identical light waves, all perfectly in step, all the same color, all marching in the same direction — this is **laser light** cascading through the ruby rod, bouncing between two mirrors and exiting as a brilliant red pulse.

The "pump" is the **helical xenon flashlamp**, the "crowd" is the $\text{Cr}^{3+}$ ions, the "stuck staircase" is the **metastable state** of $\text{Cr}^{3+}$, and the "exit door" is the **partially silvered mirror**.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Active ion:** Chromium trivalent ion ($\text{Cr}^{3+}$)
- **Host material:** Aluminium oxide (corundum, sapphire)
- **Doping concentration:** $\approx 0.03$ to $0.05$ wt % $\text{Cr}^{3+}$
- **Pump source:** Xenon flashlamp (pulsed)
- **Output wavelength:** $\lambda = 694.3 \text{ nm}$ (deep red)
- **Operating mode:** Pulsed (not continuous wave)
- **Laser type:** **Three-level system**
- **Inventor:** T. H. Maiman, **1960**
- **Threshold energy:** typically 1–5 Joules per pulse (rod geometry dependent)
- **Pulse duration:** $\approx 0.5$ to $5$ ms
- **Peak power:** can reach several kilowatts to megawatts (Q-switched variants)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three-Level Laser System — Why Ruby is Special

A **three-level laser** has three relevant energy states. The ruby system uses:

| State | Notation | Lifetime | Role |
|---|---|---|---|
| Ground state | $^4\text{A}_2$ | Stable | Initial reservoir of ions |
| Pump band (broad) | $^4\text{F}_1, ^4\text{F}_2$ | $\approx 10^{-8} \text{ s}$ | Optical absorption band |
| Metastable state | $^2\text{E}$ (split into $\overline{\text{E}}$ and $2\overline{\text{A}}$) | $\approx 3 \text{ ms}$ | Upper laser level |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The metastable state has a **long lifetime ($\sim 3$ ms)** — millions of times longer than ordinary excited states. This long lifetime is what allows **population inversion** to build up against the ground state, despite the laser transition terminating *back* on the ground state. This is the fundamental difficulty of three-level lasers: more than half the total ion population must be lifted out of the ground state before any net gain is achieved.

## 2.2 Constructional Components of a Ruby Laser

A practical Ruby Laser assembly has four essential subsystems:

1. **Active medium** — A cylindrical ruby rod, typically 2 to 20 cm long and 0.5 to 2 cm in diameter. The rod ends are optically polished to flat parallel surfaces (often within a few arc-seconds of parallelism). The flatness and parallelism are critical because they form the optical resonator.
2. **Optical resonator (cavity)** — One end of the rod is coated with a **fully reflecting (100%) silver/dielectric mirror**; the other end is a **partially reflecting (~85–95%) output coupler mirror**. The two flat mirrors must be precisely parallel (within arc-seconds) to support a stable $\text{TEM}_{00}$ mode.
3. **Pumping source** — A **helical xenon flashlamp** wound around the ruby rod (or a linear flashlamp placed alongside a polished reflector cavity) emits an intense white-light pulse lasting $\sim 1$ to $5$ ms. Electrical energy of hundreds of joules is discharged through the lamp in milliseconds.
4. **Cooling system** — A water jacket (or forced air) surrounds the lamp and rod. The xenon flashlamp dumps enormous heat into the system; the ruby rod must be kept cool or thermal lensing and birefringence destroy the cavity alignment.

## 2.3 Step-by-Step Working Principle

1. **Optical pumping:** A high-voltage capacitor bank discharges through the xenon flashlamp, producing an intense burst of broadband white light spanning visible and near-ultraviolet wavelengths. Photons in the **green ($\sim 550$ nm) and blue ($\sim 400$ nm)** regions are strongly absorbed by the $\text{Cr}^{3+}$ ions.
2. **Excitation to pump band:** $\text{Cr}^{3+}$ ions in the ground state ($^4\text{A}_2$) absorb pump photons and are excited to the broad pump bands $^4\text{F}_1$ and $^4\text{F}_2$.
3. **Non-radiative relaxation:** Within $\approx 10^{-8}$ s, the excited ions lose energy to the crystal lattice as heat (phonons) and decay to the **metastable $^2\text{E}$ state** (specifically the lower $\overline{\text{E}}$ sub-level).
4. **Population inversion:** Because the metastable state lives $\sim 3$ ms, ions accumulate in $^2\text{E}$. Once $N(^2\text{E}) > N(^4\text{A}_2)$, inversion is achieved. This requires pumping **more than half the total Cr$^{3+}$ population**, which is why ruby lasers need very intense pumping.
5. **Stimulated emission:** A spontaneously emitted photon at 694.3 nm triggers a chain reaction of stimulated emissions. Identical photons, in phase and direction, cascade through the rod.
6. **Amplification by resonator:** The photon avalanche reflects back and forth between the two cavity mirrors, growing exponentially with each pass through the inverted medium. The partially transmitting mirror couples out a fraction (typically 1–10%) as the **laser beam**.
7. **Pulse termination:** The pulse ends when the upper-level population falls below threshold, i.e., when $N(^2\text{E}) = N(^4\text{A}_2)$ again.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Quantity | Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Output photon energy | $E = h\nu = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda}$ | $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s, $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s |
| 2 | Wavelength–frequency | $\nu = \dfrac{c}{\lambda}$ | Hz, for $\lambda = 694.3$ nm: $\nu \approx 4.32 \times 10^{14}$ Hz |
| 3 | Threshold inversion (3-level) | $N_2 - N_1 \geq N_{\text{th}} = \dfrac{1}{\sigma \tau_{\text{sp}} \cdot c / n}$ | $\sigma$ = stimulated emission cross-section, $\tau_{\text{sp}}$ = spontaneous lifetime, $n$ = refractive index |
| 4 | Critical inversion for ruby (3-level) | $N_{\text{th}} \approx 1.6 \times 10^{17}$ ions/cm³ (typical) | Approx. half of the total $\text{Cr}^{3+}$ population |
| 5 | Small-signal gain coefficient | $g(\nu) = \sigma(\nu)\left(N_2 - \dfrac{N_1}{g_1/g_2}\right)$ | $g_1, g_2$ are degeneracies |
| 6 | Threshold condition (cavity) | $R_1 R_2 \, e^{2gL} \geq 1$ | $R_1, R_2$ = mirror reflectivities, $L$ = rod length |
| 7 | Cavity round-trip time | $t_{\text{rt}} = \dfrac{2L_{\text{cav}}}{c}$ | seconds |
| 8 | Slope efficiency | $\eta_{\text{slope}} = \dfrac{h\nu_{\text{out}}}{h\nu_{\text{pump}}} \cdot \eta_{\text{quantum}}$ | typically 0.1 – 1% for ruby |
| 9 | Pulse energy | $E_{\text{pulse}} = \eta \cdot E_{\text{stored}}$ | Joules |
| 10 | Q-switched pulse width | $\Delta t \approx \dfrac{2L_{\text{cav}}}{c} \cdot \dfrac{1}{N_{\text{ph}}}$ | narrow pulse of ns duration |

> [!NOTE]
> For the KTU 2024 board exam, the most frequently tested formulas are (1), (2), (5), and (6). Memorize the threshold condition $R_1 R_2 e^{2gL} \geq 1$ — it is a generic and frequently asked result.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Ruby lasers were historically the first high-power pulsed visible coherent sources and were used in:
- **Holography** (the red 694.3 nm line is ideal for silver-halide holographic plates)
- **Ranging and LIDAR**
- **Non-linear optics** (second harmonic generation to 347.15 nm UV)
- **Dermatology** (tattoo removal, pigmented lesion treatment)
- **Military target designators** (early systems)
- **Ballistic missile ranging** in the 1960s–70s

Today, ruby lasers have been largely superseded by **Nd:YAG** lasers in industrial applications, but the ruby remains the textbook prototype for **three-level laser physics**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Photon Energy and Frequency of Ruby Laser Output

We are given the laser output wavelength:
$$\lambda = 694.3 \text{ nm} = 694.3 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}$$

**Step 1 — Frequency calculation:**

Substituting into the standard wave relation $\nu = c / \lambda$:

$$\nu = \dfrac{c}{\lambda} = \dfrac{3 \times 10^{8} \text{ m/s}}{694.3 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}}$$

$$\nu = \dfrac{3 \times 10^{8}}{6.943 \times 10^{-7}} = 4.321 \times 10^{14} \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 2 — Photon energy:**

Using Planck–Einstein relation $E = h\nu$:

$$E = (6.626 \times 10^{-34} \text{ J·s}) \times (4.321 \times 10^{14} \text{ Hz})$$

$$E = 2.863 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to electron-volts:**

Using $1 \text{ eV} = 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}$:

$$E = \dfrac{2.863 \times 10^{-19}}{1.602 \times 10^{-19}} = 1.787 \text{ eV}$$

**Final answer:**

$$\boxed{\nu = 4.32 \times 10^{14} \text{ Hz}, \quad E = 2.86 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J} = 1.79 \text{ eV}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The energy gap between the $\overline{\text{E}}$ and $^4\text{A}_2$ levels in $\text{Cr}^{3+}$ is therefore **1.79 eV**, which the student should remember as a standard KTU numerical.

## 3.2 Derivation: Threshold Inversion Density for a Three-Level Laser

For a three-level laser with **non-degenerate** levels (degeneracy ratio $g_2/g_1 = 1$ for simplicity), the condition for net amplification is:

$$N_2 > N_1$$

The **small-signal gain coefficient** is:

$$g(\nu) = \sigma(\nu) (N_2 - N_1)$$

where $\sigma(\nu)$ is the **stimulated emission cross-section** at frequency $\nu$.

The threshold condition is that gain equals total losses in the cavity. If we lump all losses (output coupling + internal absorption + scattering) into a single loss coefficient $\alpha$ and a rod length $L$, then:

$$g_{\text{th}} L = \alpha L + \tfrac{1}{2}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{1}{R_1 R_2}\right)$$

**Step 1 — Solve for threshold gain:**

$$g_{\text{th}} = \alpha + \dfrac{1}{2L}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{1}{R_1 R_2}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Threshold population inversion:**

$$(N_2 - N_1)_{\text{th}} = \dfrac{g_{\text{th}}}{\sigma(\nu)}$$

**Step 3 — Total ion density $N_{\text{tot}}$:**

For a three-level system, $N_1 + N_2 = N_{\text{tot}}$. Solving the two equations $N_2 - N_1 = \Delta N$ and $N_1 + N_2 = N_{\text{tot}}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_2 - N_1 &= \Delta N \\
N_1 + N_2 &= N_{\text{tot}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Adding:

$$2 N_2 = N_{\text{tot}} + \Delta N \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; N_2 = \dfrac{N_{\text{tot}} + \Delta N}{2}$$

Subtracting:

$$2 N_1 = N_{\text{tot}} - \Delta N \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; N_1 = \dfrac{N_{\text{tot}} - \Delta N}{2}$$

**Step 4 — Critical inversion when oscillation just starts:**

If $\Delta N = N_{\text{th}}$, the **minimum pump requirement** is to invert at least half the population. For ruby with $N_{\text{tot}} \approx 1.6 \times 10^{19}$ ions/cm³ (for 0.05% doping):

$$N_{\text{th}} \approx \tfrac{1}{2} N_{\text{tot}} \approx 0.8 \times 10^{19} \text{ ions/cm}^3$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is why three-level lasers are **inherently inefficient** — the pump must move at least 50% of the population before any useful gain appears. Nd:YAG is a four-level system, which is why it became preferred.

## 3.3 Derivation: Cavity Threshold Condition (Cascaded Round-Trip)

Consider a photon making one round trip inside the cavity of length $L_{\text{cav}}$, starting at mirror $M_1$:

**Step 1 — Gain acquired in one pass through the rod of length $L$:**

$$\text{Amplification factor} = e^{gL}$$

**Step 2 — Loss at mirror $M_1$ (reflectivity $R_1$):** factor $R_1$
**Step 3 — Loss at mirror $M_2$ (reflectivity $R_2$):** factor $R_2$
**Step 4 — Total round-trip multiplicative factor:**

$$M = R_1 R_2 \cdot e^{2gL}$$

**Step 5 — Threshold condition:** for sustained oscillation, the round-trip factor must be $\geq 1$:

$$R_1 R_2 \, e^{2gL} \geq 1$$

This is the most general threshold condition and is valid for **any** laser, not just ruby.

**Step 6 — Solve for threshold gain $g_{\text{th}}$:**

Taking the natural logarithm of the equality:

$$\ln(R_1 R_2) + 2 g_{\text{th}} L = 0$$

$$g_{\text{th}} = -\dfrac{\ln(R_1 R_2)}{2L} = \dfrac{\ln(1/(R_1 R_2))}{2L}$$

If we further set $R_1 = 1$ (perfect rear mirror) and $R_2 = R$:

$$g_{\text{th}} = -\dfrac{\ln R}{2L} = \dfrac{\vert \ln R \vert}{2L}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** A common mistake is to write $g_{\text{th}} = -\ln R / L$ (forgetting the factor of 2). Always remember the round trip traverses the rod **twice** — once forward, once backward.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Three-Level vs Four-Level Systems

| Property | Three-Level (Ruby) | Four-Level (Nd:YAG) |
|---|---|---|
| Lower laser level | Ground state | Excited state above ground |
| Population of lower level at start | $\approx N_{\text{tot}}$ (huge) | $\approx 0$ (easy to invert) |
| Pump required to reach threshold | $> 50\%$ of ions | Very small fraction |
| Threshold pump power | High | Low |
| Efficiency | Low ($\sim 0.1$ to $1\%$) | High (1–10%) |
| CW operation | Difficult | Routine |
| Output mode | Pulsed only (typically) | Pulsed or CW |

> [!WARNING]
> KTU students often confuse the level counts. **Three-level** means *three relevant energy states*: ground, metastable upper laser, and intermediate pump band (or vice-versa). **Four-level** adds an extra intermediate state between the lower laser level and the ground state, so the lower laser level is essentially empty at thermal equilibrium.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Ruby Laser System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PWR[High Voltage Capacitor Bank] --> XENON[Helical Xenon Flashlamp]
    XENON -- broadband white light --> RUBY[Ruby Rod Cr3 plus in Al2O3]
    RUBY -- spontaneous photon --> PHOTON1[Seed Photon 694.3 nm]
    PHOTON1 -- stimulated emission --> CASCADE[Photon Avalanche inside cavity]
    CASCADE <--> MIRROR1[Fully Reflecting Mirror R equals 1.0]
    CASCADE <--> MIRROR2[Partially Transmitting Mirror R equals 0.9]
    MIRROR2 -- laser output 694.3 nm --> BEAM[Red Laser Beam Output]
    COOL[Water Cooling Jacket] -. surrounds .-> XENON
    COOL -. surrounds .-> RUBY
```

## 4.2 Energy Level Transitions of Cr³⁺ in Ruby (Functional Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    GS[Ground State 4A2] -- pump absorption green blue photons --> PB[Pump Bands 4F1 4F2 lifetime 10 to power minus 8 s]
    PB -- non radiative phonon decay --> MS[Metastable State 2E lifetime 3 ms split into E bar and 2A bar]
    MS -- stimulated emission 694.3 nm --> GS
    MS -- spontaneous fluorescence 692.9 nm R1 line --> SPONT[Spontaneous Photon]
    MS -- spontaneous fluorescence 700.9 nm R2 line --> SPONT2[Spontaneous Photon]
    SPONT -- triggers stimulated emission --> GS
```

## 4.3 Functional Architecture of a Complete Ruby Laser Device

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PUMP[Pumping Subsystem]
        A1[DC Power Supply 1 to 2 kV] --> A2[Energy Storage Capacitor 100 to 1000 microF]
        A2 --> A3[Trigger Transformer]
        A3 --> A4[Xenon Flashlamp Helical]
    end
    subgraph GAIN[Gain Subsystem]
        B1[Ruby Rod doped 0.05 percent Cr3 plus] --> B2[Flat Parallel Polished End Faces]
        B2 --> B3[Front Mirror partially silvered]
        B2 --> B4[Rear Mirror fully silvered]
    end
    subgraph THERMAL[Thermal Management]
        C1[Water Jacket] --> C2[Heat Exchanger]
        C2 --> C3[Cooling Pump]
    end
    A4 -. optical coupling via reflector .-> B1
    C1 -. surrounds rod and lamp .-> B1
    B3 --> OUT[Coherent Red Output 694.3 nm]
```

## 4.4 Operation Cycle of Ruby Laser (Time-Ordered Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Start Flashlamp Discharge] --> S1[Photons Strike Cr3 plus Ions]
    S1 --> S2[Ions Excited to 4F1 4F2 Pump Bands]
    S2 --> S3[Fast Non Radiative Decay to 2E Metastable]
    S3 --> S4{Population N 2E greater than N 4A2 ?}
    S4 -- No --> S1
    S4 -- Yes --> S5[Spontaneous Photon Starts Stimulated Emission]
    S5 --> S6[Photon Cascade Amplified between Mirrors]
    S6 --> S7[Output Pulse Emerges through Partial Mirror]
    S7 --> S8{Population Still Inverted ?}
    S8 -- Yes --> S6
    S8 -- No --> S9[Pulse Terminates]
    S9 --> S10[Wait for Next Flashlamp Cycle]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1: Define Ruby Laser. Mention its active medium and output wavelength.
`[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

The **Ruby Laser** is the first operational solid-state laser, demonstrated by **Theodore Maiman in 1960**. Its active medium is a **synthetic ruby crystal** — a single crystal of **aluminium oxide ($\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3$)** doped with approximately **0.03–0.05% chromium ions ($\text{Cr}^{3+}$)**. It is optically pumped by a **xenon flashlamp** and operates as a **three-level pulsed laser**. The output is a deep red laser beam at a characteristic wavelength of **$\lambda = 694.3$ nm**, corresponding to the $\text{R}_1$ fluorescence line of the metastable $^2\text{E}$ state of $\text{Cr}^{3+}$.

### Question A2: Why is the metastable state important for laser action in Ruby?
`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

The metastable $^2\text{E}$ state in $\text{Cr}^{3+}$ has an exceptionally **long lifetime of $\sim 3$ ms** (compared to $\sim 10^{-8}$ s for ordinary excited states). This long lifetime allows ions pumped to the upper levels to **accumulate** in the metastable state faster than they can decay, enabling **population inversion** between $^2\text{E}$ (upper laser level) and $^4\text{A}_2$ (ground state). Without this metastable reservoir, the excited ions would return to the ground state almost instantly, and sustained stimulated emission would be impossible. Hence the metastable state is the **cornerstone of population inversion** in any ruby laser.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): Construction, Working, and Energy Levels of Ruby Laser

`[KTU University Exam – May 2024 / Model Paper]`

**(a) With the help of a neat energy level diagram, explain the construction and working of a Ruby Laser.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Construction:** A ruby laser consists of:

1. **Ruby rod:** A cylindrical single crystal of $\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3$ doped with 0.03–0.05% $\text{Cr}^{3+}$ ions. Typical dimensions: 2–20 cm long, 0.5–2 cm diameter. Both end faces are optically polished, flat, and parallel to within arc-seconds.
2. **Resonating cavity:** The two flat ends are silvered — one fully (100% reflective) and the other partially (≈85–95% reflective, output coupler).
3. **Pumping source:** A **helical xenon flashlamp** wound around the rod, or a linear flashlamp in a polished reflector housing.
4. **Cooling:** A water jacket surrounds the lamp and rod to remove heat.

**Working principle:**

- The xenon flashlamp is fired by discharging a high-voltage capacitor bank, producing intense white light.
- **Green (~550 nm) and blue (~400 nm) photons** are absorbed by $\text{Cr}^{3+}$ ions, exciting them from the ground state $^4\text{A}_2$ to the pump bands $^4\text{F}_1, ^4\text{F}_2$.
- The excited ions undergo **fast non-radiative decay** ($\sim 10^{-8}$ s) to the metastable $^2\text{E}$ state, losing energy as phonons (heat).
- Because the metastable state has a long lifetime ($\sim 3$ ms), ions **accumulate** in $^2\text{E}$ until $N(^2\text{E}) > N(^4\text{A}_2)$, achieving **population inversion**.
- A spontaneously emitted photon at 694.3 nm triggers **stimulated emission** — other ions in $^2\text{E}$ release identical photons in phase and direction.
- The photon cascade bounces between the cavity mirrors, growing in intensity. A fraction exits through the partially silvered mirror as the **coherent red laser beam** at 694.3 nm.

**Energy level diagram (textual):**

```
^4F1, ^4F2  ── Pump band (10^-8 s decay)
     │
     │ non-radiative (phonons)
     ▼
   ^2E  ── Metastable (3 ms) ── R1 line 694.3 nm (laser)
     │
     │ stimulated emission
     ▼
  ^4A2  ── Ground state
```

**(b) Explain the pumping mechanism and the conditions for population inversion in a Ruby Laser. Why is Ruby called a three-level laser?** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Pumping mechanism:**

Optical pumping uses the **xenon flashlamp** to deliver a high-intensity pulse of broadband light (peaking in the visible). The flash is broadband, but the $\text{Cr}^{3+}$ ions selectively absorb the **green and blue** portions of the spectrum because these wavelengths match the energy gap between the ground state $^4\text{A}_2$ and the broad pump bands $^4\text{F}_1, ^4\text{F}_2$. The ions then quickly relax non-radiatively to the metastable $^2\text{E}$ state. The pump must deliver enough photons per pulse to lift **more than half the total $\text{Cr}^{3+}$ population** out of the ground state, because in a three-level system the lower laser level *is* the ground state.

**Condition for population inversion:**

The condition is $N(^2\text{E}) > N(^4\text{A}_2)$. The threshold inversion is:

$$N_{\text{th}} \approx \dfrac{N_{\text{tot}}}{2}$$

This is much harder to achieve than in a four-level laser, where the lower laser level is essentially empty at equilibrium.

**Why three-level:**

Ruby is called a **three-level laser** because only three energy levels participate in the lasing cycle:
- Level 1: Ground state $^4\text{A}_2$ (also the **lower laser level**)
- Level 2: Metastable state $^2\text{E}$ (the **upper laser level**)
- Level 3: Pump bands $^4\text{F}_1, ^4\text{F}_2$ (the **optical pumping level**)

The lower laser level is the **ground state** itself, which initially holds essentially all the population. This is the defining feature of a three-level system and the reason ruby lasers need very high pump energy and operate in pulsed mode rather than CW.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): Threshold Condition, Wavelength, and Applications of Ruby Laser

`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a) Derive the threshold condition for laser oscillation. Apply it to a Ruby Laser with one fully reflecting mirror and the other 90% reflecting. Given $L = 5$ cm, find the threshold gain coefficient.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Derivation:**

Consider a photon undergoing one round trip inside an optical cavity of length $L$ (the gain medium length). The total multiplicative factor after a round trip is:

$$M = R_1 R_2 \, e^{2gL}$$

where $R_1, R_2$ are mirror reflectivities and $g$ is the gain coefficient. For sustained oscillation, the photon must regain its original intensity, so:

$$R_1 R_2 \, e^{2gL} \geq 1$$

At threshold, the equality holds:

$$R_1 R_2 \, e^{2g_{\text{th}} L} = 1$$

Solving for the threshold gain:

$$g_{\text{th}} = -\dfrac{\ln(R_1 R_2)}{2L} = \dfrac{\ln(1/(R_1 R_2))}{2L}$$

**Numerical application:**

Given: $R_1 = 1$ (fully reflecting), $R_2 = 0.90$, $L = 5$ cm = $0.05$ m.

$$g_{\text{th}} = \dfrac{\ln(1/(1 \times 0.90))}{2 \times 0.05} = \dfrac{\ln(1.1111\ldots)}{0.10}$$

$$g_{\text{th}} = \dfrac{0.10536}{0.10} = 1.0536 \text{ m}^{-1}$$

**Converting to cm⁻¹:**

$$g_{\text{th}} = 0.0105 \text{ cm}^{-1}$$

$$\boxed{g_{\text{th}} \approx 1.05 \text{ m}^{-1} = 0.0105 \text{ cm}^{-1}}$$

**(b) Calculate the frequency and energy (in eV) of the Ruby Laser photon. State four important applications of Ruby Laser.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Frequency calculation:**

Given $\lambda = 694.3$ nm = $694.3 \times 10^{-9}$ m:

$$\nu = \dfrac{c}{\lambda} = \dfrac{3 \times 10^{8}}{694.3 \times 10^{-9}} = 4.32 \times 10^{14} \text{ Hz}$$

**Photon energy:**

$$E = h\nu = (6.626 \times 10^{-34}) \times (4.32 \times 10^{14}) = 2.86 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}$$

Converting to eV:

$$E = \dfrac{2.86 \times 10^{-19}}{1.602 \times 10^{-19}} = 1.79 \text{ eV}$$

$$\boxed{\nu = 4.32 \times 10^{14} \text{ Hz}, \quad E = 1.79 \text{ eV}}$$

**Four applications of Ruby Laser:**

1. **Holography** — the 694.3 nm red line is highly sensitive to silver-halide holographic emulsions.
2. **Pulsed LIDAR and rangefinding** — Q-switched ruby pulses can measure distances and detect atmospheric scattering.
3. **Dermatology and cosmetic surgery** — selective absorption by melanin and tattoo pigments enables removal of pigmented lesions and tattoos.
4. **Non-linear optics** — frequency doubling in a KDP or BBO crystal produces 347.15 nm ultraviolet radiation for spectroscopy and fluorescence studies.

(Additional applications: ballistic missile ranging, scientific research, ruby maser predecessor studies, plasma diagnostics.)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in the round trip.** The cavity round trip traverses the gain medium *twice*; the threshold formula is $g_{\text{th}} = -\ln(R_1 R_2)/(2L)$, not $-\ln(R_1 R_2)/L$. This is the **single most common error** in board exams.
> 2. **Mixing up three-level and four-level diagrams.** A three-level laser has the *ground state* as the lower laser level. A four-level laser has an additional intermediate level above the ground. Confusing these two costs at least 2 marks.
> 3. **Forgetting units.** When computing photon energy, always state the answer in **both joules and electron-volts**. Examiners explicitly look for the eV conversion.
> 4. **Drawing the energy level diagram without labels.** Always label the lifetimes of each state (e.g., "$\sim 3$ ms for metastable", "$\sim 10^{-8}$ s for pump band") — this earns the diagram full marks.
> 5. **Confusing R₁ (692.9 nm) and the laser line (694.3 nm).** The 692.9 nm line is the *zero-phonon R₁ line* in some references; the actual laser transition at room temperature is the slightly shifted **694.3 nm R₁ line** in fluorescence. State the correct 694.3 nm value.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- The **Ruby Laser** was the world's first laser — built by **Theodore Maiman in 1960**.
- Active medium: **$\text{Cr}^{3+}$ ions doped in $\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3$ (synthetic ruby)**, doping $\approx 0.03$–$0.05$ wt %.
- It is a **three-level, solid-state, optically pumped, pulsed laser**.
- Output wavelength: **$\lambda = 694.3$ nm** (deep red); photon energy $\approx 1.79$ eV.
- The **metastable $^2\text{E}$ state** ($\tau \approx 3$ ms) is the key to population inversion.
- Pumping source: **helical xenon flashlamp** delivering green/blue photons.
- Threshold condition: $R_1 R_2 \, e^{2gL} \geq 1 \;\Rightarrow\; g_{\text{th}} = -\ln(R_1 R_2)/(2L)$.
- Because the lower laser level is the ground state, **>50% of ions must be pumped** to achieve inversion — this is why Ruby is a pulsed laser, not CW.
- Threshold inversion for ruby: typically $\sim 10^{17}$ to $10^{18}$ ions/cm³.
- End mirrors: one **100% reflecting**, the other **partially reflecting (~85–95%)** = output coupler.
- Cooling: **water jacket** around rod and lamp.
- **Applications:** holography, LIDAR, dermatology, non-linear optics (UV doubling), plasma diagnostics.
- **Key advantage:** first laser; relatively simple construction; high peak power in Q-switched mode.
- **Key disadvantage:** three-level nature → low efficiency, requires very high pump power, pulsed only.

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** Memorize (i) the energy level labels ($^4\text{A}_2$, $^4\text{F}_1, ^4\text{F}_2$, $^2\text{E}$), (ii) the wavelength 694.3 nm, (iii) the threshold formula $g_{\text{th}} = -\ln(R_1 R_2)/(2L)$, and (iv) the reason Ruby is a *three-level* laser. These four items cover more than 80% of the marks in any Ruby Laser question.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Gas: He-Ne Laser
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT861 - OPTOELECTRONICS AND INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Gas: He-Ne Laser

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

The **Helium-Neon (He-Ne) Laser** is a four-level, continuous-wave (CW) gas laser that operates on the principle of **resonant energy transfer** (collisional excitation) from metastable Helium atoms to ground-state Neon atoms, producing highly coherent, monochromatic, and low-divergence laser radiation predominantly at the visible wavelength of **632.8 nm** (red light).

The active medium is a low-pressure gaseous mixture of Helium and Neon in an approximate ratio of **10:1**, sealed inside a narrow-bore glass discharge capillary terminated by Brewster-angle windows and external high-reflectance dielectric mirrors forming an optical resonator.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — He-Ne Classification Summary**
> - **Type:** Atomic gas laser (neutral atom laser)
> - **Pumping Mechanism:** Electrical discharge (DC excitation)
> - **Lasing Atom:** Neon (Ne) — Helium is purely an intermediary
> - **Primary Wavelength:** **632.8 nm** (red)
> - **Operation Mode:** Continuous Wave (CW)
> - **Typical Output Power:** **0.5 mW – 50 mW**

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Two-Story Elevator Building"

Imagine a tall office building where:
- **Helium atoms** are tiny "elevator cars" that can be cheaply lifted to a high floor using electricity (the electrical discharge).
- The Helium cars, however, do NOT have a window facing the street (i.e., they cannot emit light by themselves — they have a forbidden optical transition).
- **Neon atoms** are parked next to the elevator shaft. When an excited Helium car brushes past a Neon car at exactly the right floor, the energy is **handed over** (resonant collisional transfer).
- The Neon atom, now excited, has a "window facing the street" and emits a photon of red light (632.8 nm) as it drops to a slightly lower floor.
- The **mirrors at both ends of the tube** act like two perfectly parallel mirrors that bounce the emitted photon back and forth, triggering a chain reaction (stimulated emission), until a small fraction escapes as a powerful, focused laser beam.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Helium?** Pure Neon cannot be pumped efficiently by an electrical discharge. Helium has metastable excited states (notably $2^1S$ and $2^3S$) that lie at almost exactly the same energy as Neon's upper laser levels. This energy resonance allows the He-Ne mixture to be a far more efficient laser medium than pure Neon.

### Key Physical Constants & Engineering Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value |
|---|---|
| Operating Wavelength ($\lambda$) | **632.8 nm** (red), 543 nm (green), 1.15 μm (IR), 3.39 μm (far-IR) |
| Gas Pressure | **1 Torr (typical)** |
| He : Ne Ratio | **10 : 1** |
| Discharge Voltage | **1 – 5 kV DC** |
| Typical Output Power | **0.5 – 50 mW** |
| Spectral Linewidth | **< 1.5 kHz** (highly coherent) |
| Beam Divergence | **< 1 mrad** |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Constructional Architecture of a He-Ne Laser

The He-Ne laser consists of the following sub-assemblies (this block-level architecture is mandatory for KTU 14-mark derivations):

1. **Discharge Tube (Gas Cell):** A narrow Pyrex or quartz capillary of bore diameter **1 – 5 mm** and length **10 – 100 cm**, sealed with Brewster-angle windows (typically fused silica) at both ends to minimize reflection losses for the polarization parallel to the plane of incidence.
2. **Gas Fill:** A mixture of Helium (~ 85%) and Neon (~ 15%) at a total pressure of about **1 Torr**.
3. **Electrodes:** An anode (tungsten) and a cold cathode (aluminum cylinder) connected to a high-voltage DC power supply via a ballast resistor.
4. **Optical Resonator:** Two highly reflecting concave mirrors ($R > 99.5\%$) — one is a high reflector (HR, $R \approx 100\%$), the other is an output coupler with $T \approx 1 - 2\%$.
5. **Mode Selector (Optional):** For single longitudinal mode operation, an etalon or intra-cavity prism is introduced.

### 2.2 Pumping and Lasing Mechanism — Step-by-Step

The He-Ne laser operates on a **four-level scheme**. The following sequence is the heart of any KTU exam answer:

- **Step 1 — Electron Impact Excitation of He:** Free electrons accelerated by the DC electric field collide with ground-state Helium atoms ($1^1S_0$), exciting them to higher energy levels. Many relax to the **metastable states** $2^1S$ and $2^3S$, which have very long lifetimes (~ ms) and cannot decay radiatively (selection rule: $\Delta S = 0$ forbidden for these transitions).

$$e^- + \text{He}(1^1S_0) \longrightarrow \text{He}^*(2^1S) + e^-$$

- **Step 2 — Resonant Energy Transfer to Ne:** The metastable Helium atom collides with a ground-state Neon atom. Because the energy of He$^*(2^1S) \approx 20.61$ eV closely matches the energy of the Ne $5s$ level (20.66 eV), energy is transferred with near-unity efficiency.

$$\text{He}^*(2^1S) + \text{Ne}(1s) \longrightarrow \text{He}(1^1S_0) + \text{Ne}^*(5s)$$

- **Step 3 — Radiative Laser Transition (Stimulated Emission):** The excited Ne atom ($5s$) decays via stimulated emission to the lower $4p$ (or $3p$) level, producing the laser photon.

$$\text{Ne}^*(5s) \longrightarrow \text{Ne}^*(4p) + h\nu \quad (\lambda = 632.8 \text{ nm})$$

- **Step 4 — Rapid Decay of Lower Laser Level:** The $4p$ level has a very short lifetime (~ 10 ns) and quickly relaxes to the $3s$ metastable level, from where it decays to the ground state via wall collisions. This rapid emptying of the lower laser level is what **sustains the population inversion** in a four-level system.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates every high-yield equation for KTU 2024 examinations on the He-Ne laser. Use $\vert \cdot \vert$ (or `\vert`) to denote modulus — **never** use the raw pipe `|` inside markdown tables.

| # | Formula / Relation | Physical Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $h\nu = E_2 - E_1 = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda}$ | Bohr-Einstein photon energy condition | J, eV |
| 2 | $\nu = \dfrac{c}{\lambda}$ | Optical frequency of emitted radiation | Hz |
| 3 | Threshold condition: $G_{th} = \alpha - \dfrac{1}{2L}\ln(R_1 R_2)$ | Round-trip gain must equal total losses | m$^{-1}$ |
| 4 | Gain coefficient: $g(\nu) = \dfrac{\Delta N \cdot \lambda^2}{8\pi n^2 \tau_{sp}} \cdot g_H(\nu)$ | Lineshape-dependent gain of medium | m$^{-1}$ |
| 5 | Output Power: $P_{out} = T \cdot I_{sat} \cdot A_{beam} \cdot \left(\dfrac{G}{G_{th}} - 1\right)$ | Rigrod output coupling formula | W |
| 6 | Saturation Intensity: $I_{sat} = \dfrac{h\nu}{\sigma \tau_{sp}}$ | Intensity at which gain saturates | W/m$^2$ |
| 7 | Resonator Mode Spacing: $\Delta\nu = \dfrac{c}{2nL}$ | Longitudinal mode frequency separation | Hz |
| 8 | Doppler Broadening FWHM: $\Delta\nu_D = \dfrac{2\nu_0}{c}\sqrt{\dfrac{2k_BT \ln 2}{M}}$ | Dominant broadening in He-Ne | Hz |
| 9 | Beam Divergence (TEM$_{00}$): $\theta = \dfrac{4\lambda}{\pi D}$ (far-field) | Half-angle divergence of Gaussian beam | rad |
| 10 | Photon Energy at 632.8 nm: $E = \dfrac{1.9865 \times 10^{-25}}{\lambda(\mu m)} \approx 3.14 \times 10^{-19}$ J | Numerical check value | J |

**Where:**
- $G_{th}$ = threshold gain coefficient
- $\alpha$ = internal distributed loss (scattering, diffraction)
- $L$ = cavity length; $R_1, R_2$ = mirror reflectivities
- $\Delta N = N_2 - N_1$ = population inversion density
- $\tau_{sp}$ = spontaneous lifetime
- $g_H(\nu)$ = lineshape function (Lorentzian or Gaussian)
- $T$ = transmission of output coupler
- $A_{beam}$ = effective beam cross-sectional area
- $D$ = beam waist diameter

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The He-Ne laser remains indispensable in modern industry and research:
- **Metrology & Alignment:** Construction alignment, tunnel boring machine guidance, interferometry.
- **Holography:** The 632.8 nm red line is a classic holographic recording wavelength.
- **Barcode Scanners & Pointers:** Read heads in supermarket checkouts and lecture-hall pointers.
- **Medical Imaging:** Confocal microscopy and flow cytometry.
- **Education:** Standard teaching tool in undergraduate optics labs due to its safety, low cost, and clean beam quality.

> [!NOTE]
> The He-Ne laser is gradually being replaced by **red laser diodes** in commercial applications, but it retains a critical role in **precision metrology** where its exceptionally narrow linewidth and beam quality ($M^2 < 1.1$) remain unmatched.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Output Power (Rigrod Analysis) — 14-Mark Standard Question

The following is a full, KTU Board-standard derivation. **Every algebraic step is explicitly shown.**

**Starting Principle — Saturation of Gain:**

In a homogeneously broadened laser medium, the saturated gain coefficient $G$ at a given frequency $\nu$ is related to the small-signal (unsaturated) gain $G_0$ and the intracavity intensity $I$ by:

$$G = \frac{G_0}{1 + \dfrac{I}{I_{sat}}}$$

At steady-state lasing condition, the round-trip gain exactly compensates the round-trip losses:

$$G = \alpha + \frac{1}{2L}\ln\left(\frac{1}{R_1 R_2}\right)$$

Substituting the first equation into the second:

$$\frac{G_0}{1 + \dfrac{I}{I_{sat}}} = \alpha + \frac{1}{2L}\ln\left(\frac{1}{R_1 R_2}\right)$$

Define the **threshold gain** as the loss term on the right-hand side:

$$G_{th} = \alpha + \frac{1}{2L}\ln\left(\frac{1}{R_1 R_2}\right)$$

Thus:

$$\frac{G_0}{1 + \dfrac{I}{I_{sat}}} = G_{th}$$

Inverting both sides:

$$1 + \frac{I}{I_{sat}} = \frac{G_0}{G_{th}}$$

Solving for the intra-cavity intensity $I$:

$$\frac{I}{I_{sat}} = \frac{G_0}{G_{th}} - 1$$

$$I = I_{sat}\left(\frac{G_0}{G_{th}} - 1\right)$$

**Relating Intra-cavity Intensity to Output Power:**

The output power is the portion of intra-cavity radiation that escapes through the partially transmitting mirror of transmission $T$. For a beam of effective cross-section $A$:

$$P_{out} = T \cdot I \cdot A$$

Substituting $I$:

$$\boxed{P_{out} = T \cdot I_{sat} \cdot A \cdot \left(\frac{G_0}{G_{th}} - 1\right)}$$

This is the **Rigrod output power relation** — a 14-mark KTU Board favorite.

### 3.2 Numerical Worked Example — Calculate Beam Divergence

**Problem:** A He-Ne laser operating at $\lambda = 632.8$ nm has a beam waist diameter $D = 0.8$ mm at the output coupler. Calculate the far-field full-angle divergence.

**Solution:**

For a TEM$_{00}$ Gaussian beam, the far-field half-angle divergence is:

$$\theta_{1/2} = \frac{\lambda}{\pi w_0}$$

where $w_0 = D/2$ is the beam waist radius.

$$w_0 = \frac{0.8 \text{ mm}}{2} = 0.4 \text{ mm} = 4 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}$$

$$\theta_{1/2} = \frac{632.8 \times 10^{-9}}{\pi \times 4 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$\theta_{1/2} = \frac{6.328 \times 10^{-7}}{1.2566 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$\theta_{1/2} \approx 5.04 \times 10^{-4} \text{ rad}$$

The full-angle divergence is:

$$\theta_{full} = 2\theta_{1/2} \approx 1.01 \times 10^{-3} \text{ rad} = 1.01 \text{ mrad}$$

**Result:** The far-field full divergence is approximately **1 mrad** — a typical, expected value for a commercial He-Ne laser.

### 3.3 Derivation of Resonant Energy Transfer Cross-Section (Conceptual)

The rate of resonant energy transfer from He$^*$ to Ne is given by:

$$W_{He \to Ne} = N_{Ne} \cdot \sigma_{ET} \cdot v_{rel}$$

where $\sigma_{ET}$ is the energy transfer cross-section, $N_{Ne}$ is the Neon number density, and $v_{rel}$ is the mean relative thermal velocity. For He-Ne mixtures at 1 Torr, $\sigma_{ET} \approx 10^{-16}$ cm$^2$, which is exceptionally large due to the near-perfect energy resonance between He$^*(2^1S)$ at 20.61 eV and Ne$^*(5s)$ at 20.66 eV (energy mismatch $\Delta E \approx 0.05$ eV, well within thermal $k_BT$).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the He-Ne Laser System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    HVPS[HVPS: High Voltage DC Power Supply 1 to 5 kV]
    BALLAST[Ballast Resistor: current limiter]
    ANODE[Anode: Tungsten Rod]
    CATHODE[Cathode: Aluminum Cylinder]
    CAPILLARY[Discharge Capillary: He plus Ne gas at 1 Torr]
    BW1[Brewster Window 1: fused silica at theta B]
    M1[Mirror 1: High Reflector R greater than 99.9 percent]
    M2[Mirror 2: Output Coupler T equals 1 to 2 percent]
    BW2[Brewster Window 2: fused silica at theta B]
    BEAM[Output Laser Beam: 632.8 nm TEM00]

    HVPS --> BALLAST
    BALLAST --> ANODE
    CATHODE --- CAPILLARY
    ANODE --- CAPILLARY
    CAPILLARY --> BW1
    BW1 --> M1
    M1 -. reflection .-> BW1
    BW1 -. optical feedback .-> CAPILLARY
    CAPILLARY --> BW2
    BW2 --> M2
    M2 -. partial transmission .-> BEAM
    M2 -. reflection .-> BW2
```

### 4.2 Four-Level Energy Diagram of the He-Ne System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    EGND[Ground State He 1 1S0 and Ne 1s]
    EHE1[He metastable 2 1S 20.61 eV]
    EHE2[He metastable 2 3S 19.82 eV]
    ENEU5S[Ne upper laser 5s 20.66 eV]
    ENEU4P[Ne lower laser 4p 18.70 eV]
    ENEU3S[Ne lower 3s metastable]
    EPHOTON[Laser photon 632.8 nm h nu equals 1.96 eV]

    EGND -->|electron impact excitation| EHE1
    EGND -->|electron impact excitation| EHE2
    EHE1 -->|resonant collisional transfer delta E equals 0.05 eV| ENEU5S
    EHE2 -->|resonant collisional transfer| ENEU5S
    ENEU5S -->|stimulated emission 632.8 nm| ENEU4P
    ENEU4P -->|fast radiative decay 10 ns| ENEU3S
    ENEU3S -->|wall collisions depopulation| EGND
    ENEU5S -. produces .-> EPHOTON
```

### 4.3 Logical Flow of the Lasing Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step 1: DC discharge ionizes He atoms]
    S2[Step 2: Free electrons excite He to metastable 2 1S and 2 3S]
    S3[Step 3: Metastable He transfers energy resonantly to Ne 5s]
    S4[Step 4: Population inversion established N 5s greater than N 4p]
    S5[Step 5: Spontaneous photon triggers stimulated emission at 632.8 nm]
    S6[Step 6: Mirrors provide optical feedback]
    S7[Step 7: Beam emerges through output coupler]
    S8[Step 8: Ne 4p rapidly decays to 3s then to ground via wall collisions]

    S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 --> S5 --> S6 --> S7
    S5 --> S8
    S8 --> S4
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**State the function of Helium in a He-Ne laser. Why is pure Neon not used as the lasing medium by itself?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Helium acts as a **buffer and energy-transfer agent** in the He-Ne laser. Free electrons from the DC discharge excite ground-state He atoms to the metastable states $2^1S$ and $2^3S$, which have very long radiative lifetimes. These metastable He atoms then undergo **resonant collisional energy transfer** with ground-state Ne atoms, exciting them to the $5s$ upper laser level (energy match: He $2^1S = 20.61$ eV $\approx$ Ne $5s = 20.66$ eV).

Pure Neon cannot be used because the upper laser level of Neon ($5s$) is **not directly accessible by electron impact from the ground state with high efficiency**, and the metastable states that would enable population inversion do not exist in pure Ne. The He-Ne mixture uses Helium purely as an intermediary pumping partner, making the system far more efficient. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Explain the role of Brewster windows in a He-Ne laser. Mention the condition for Brewster angle.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Brewster windows are fused-silica plates placed at the two ends of the gas discharge capillary, oriented at the **Brewster angle** $\theta_B$ to the optical axis. At this angle, the polarization component parallel to the plane of incidence (p-polarization) experiences **zero reflection** — it is transmitted with 100% efficiency. The perpendicular (s-) polarization suffers reflection losses.

**Brewster's Law:**
$$\tan\theta_B = \frac{n_2}{n_1}$$

For a fused-silica window ($n_2 = 1.46$) in air ($n_1 = 1$):
$$\theta_B = \arctan(1.46) \approx 55.6^\circ$$

This arrangement **eliminates reflection losses** for the desired polarization, improving overall efficiency, and **polarizes the output beam** linearly. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2023, Similar Pattern]**

**(a)** With a neat energy level diagram, explain the construction and working of a He-Ne laser. Discuss the role of metastable states of Helium in achieving population inversion. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive an expression for the output power of a gas laser under steady-state conditions. Define threshold gain coefficient. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Construction and Working (7 marks):**

*Construction:* The He-Ne laser consists of (1) a Pyrex discharge tube of length 10–100 cm filled with He-Ne mixture in the ratio 10:1 at a pressure of ~ 1 Torr; (2) Brewster-angle windows at both ends; (3) external resonator mirrors — one HR and one output coupler (transmission ~ 1–2%); (4) electrodes (anode + cold cathode) connected to a 1–5 kV DC supply via a ballast resistor.

*Working — Four-Step Process:*
1. **Electron-impact excitation of He:** $e^- + \text{He}(1^1S_0) \rightarrow \text{He}^*(2^1S) + e^-$
2. **Resonant energy transfer to Ne:** $\text{He}^*(2^1S) + \text{Ne} \rightarrow \text{He}(1^1S_0) + \text{Ne}^*(5s)$
3. **Stimulated emission at 632.8 nm:** $\text{Ne}^*(5s) \rightarrow \text{Ne}^*(4p) + h\nu$
4. **Rapid depopulation of lower level via wall collisions.**

*Role of Metastable States:* The He $2^1S$ state has a radiative lifetime of ~ 20 ms (forbidden transition to ground by selection rules). This long lifetime allows He atoms to **accumulate** at the metastable level and serve as an **energy reservoir**, ensuring continuous and efficient pumping of the Ne $5s$ upper laser level. Without the metastable state, the energy transfer to Ne would be too weak to achieve population inversion.

*Energy level diagram — see Section 4.2 above.* **(7 marks)**

**(b) Output Power Derivation (7 marks):**

See the full step-by-step derivation in **Section 3.1** above. The final boxed Rigrod result is:

$$P_{out} = T \cdot I_{sat} \cdot A \cdot \left(\frac{G_0}{G_{th}} - 1\right)$$

**Definition of Threshold Gain Coefficient:** The minimum single-pass gain coefficient required to overcome all intracavity losses (output coupling, internal absorption, scattering, and mirror transmission) so that the round-trip gain equals the round-trip loss. It is given by:

$$G_{th} = \alpha + \frac{1}{2L}\ln\left(\frac{1}{R_1 R_2}\right)$$

At threshold, $G_0 = G_{th}$ and the output power is zero. For lasing, $G_0 > G_{th}$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Similar Pattern]**

**(a)** List any four characteristics of laser radiation. Explain with a block diagram the working of a He-Ne laser. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With necessary expressions, explain the four-level laser pumping scheme. Compare it with the three-level scheme and state why four-level systems are preferred. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Four Characteristics of Laser Radiation (7 marks):**

1. **Monochromaticity:** Extremely narrow spectral linewidth ($\Delta\lambda \sim 10^{-6}$ nm for He-Ne). The light consists of essentially one wavelength.
2. **Coherence:** Both temporal (long coherence length, often > 10 m) and spatial coherence (constant phase relationship across the beam). Enables interference and holography.
3. **Directionality:** Very low beam divergence (typically < 1 mrad), so the beam spreads minimally over long distances.
4. **High Brightness:** Power concentrated in a small spot and narrow spectral range gives enormous spectral intensity — orders of magnitude higher than conventional sources.

*Block diagram of He-Ne laser — see Section 4.1 above.*

Operational summary: HVPS $\rightarrow$ discharge tube (He-Ne mixture) $\rightarrow$ population inversion via resonant energy transfer $\rightarrow$ stimulated emission at 632.8 nm $\rightarrow$ optical feedback by resonator mirrors $\rightarrow$ output beam. **(7 marks)**

**(b) Four-Level Pumping Scheme (7 marks):**

In a four-level laser, four distinct energy levels participate: ground state (E$_0$), pump level (E$_3$), upper laser level (E$_2$), and lower laser level (E$_1$), with E$_1$ lying just above E$_0$.

**Population Inversion Condition:** $N_2 > N_1$. Since E$_1$ is not the ground state, it is essentially empty at thermal equilibrium, making population inversion **far easier** to achieve than in a three-level system.

**Rate Equation for Population of Level 2:**

$$\frac{dN_2}{dt} = W_p N_0 - \frac{N_2}{\tau_{21}} - \sigma \phi (N_2 - N_1)$$

**Comparison with Three-Level Scheme:**

| Feature | Three-Level | Four-Level |
|---|---|---|
| Lower laser level | Ground state | Just above ground (E$_1 >$ E$_0$) |
| Threshold pump power | Very high | Low (typically 1/10 of three-level) |
| Inversion difficulty | Hard (need $N_2 > N_0/2$) | Easy ($N_1 \approx 0$ initially) |
| Examples | Ruby (694 nm) | He-Ne, Nd:YAG, CO$_2$ |
| Efficiency | Low | High |

**Why Four-Level Is Preferred:** The lower laser level is rapidly depopulated (E$_1$ has short lifetime and decays to E$_0$). This keeps $N_1 \approx 0$, so even a tiny pump rate can establish $N_2 > N_1$, drastically reducing the threshold pump power and enabling CW operation (as in the He-Ne laser). **(7 marks)**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in He-Ne Laser Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing the role of Helium and Neon:** Many students wrongly state that "Helium lases." Helium is purely a **pumping intermediary**; **Neon is the lasing species**. (Lose 1–2 marks.)
> 2. **Missing the four-level nature:** Do not call it a three-level laser. The He-Ne is strictly **four-level** because the lower laser level (Ne $4p$) is above the ground state.
> 3. **Forgetting metastable state lifetimes:** Always quote the long lifetime (~ ms) of the He $2^1S$ metastable state and its role as an energy reservoir. This is a Board-favorite line.
> 4. **Skipping units in the Rigrod formula:** Always state $P_{out}$ in **watts**, $T$ is dimensionless, $I_{sat}$ in W/m$^2$, $A$ in m$^2$.
> 5. **Brewster angle sign error:** Remember $\tan\theta_B = n_2/n_1$, NOT the reciprocal. For air-to-glass, $\theta_B \approx 55.6^\circ$.
> 6. **Wall collisions:** The rapid depopulation of the Ne $3s$ level (which is itself metastable) occurs by **diffusion to the walls** and subsequent de-excitation — this is the textbook finish and earns the last mark.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **He-Ne laser** = four-level, CW, gas laser; primary line **632.8 nm** (red).
- **Gas mixture:** He : Ne $\approx$ 10 : 1; total pressure ~ 1 Torr; bore ~ 1–5 mm.
- **Pumping:** Electrical discharge excites He to **metastable** $2^1S$ (20.61 eV) and $2^3S$ (19.82 eV).
- **Energy Transfer:** Resonant (nearly matched energies) collision transfers energy from He$^*$ to Ne ($5s$).
- **Laser transition:** Ne $5s \rightarrow 4p$ at 632.8 nm; lower level $4p$ decays in ~ 10 ns.
- **Why four-level works:** Lower laser level is not the ground state, so inversion is easy.
- **Construction essentials:** Brewster windows, external spherical mirrors, ballast resistor, cold cathode.
- **Brewster angle:** $\tan\theta_B = n_2/n_1 \approx 55.6^\circ$ for fused silica; eliminates reflection loss for p-polarization.
- **Typical output:** 0.5 – 50 mW, beam divergence < 1 mrad, linewidth < 1.5 kHz.
- **Key formulas:** $P_{out} = T \cdot I_{sat} \cdot A \cdot (G_0/G_{th} - 1)$, $G_{th} = \alpha + \tfrac{1}{2L}\ln(1/R_1 R_2)$.
- **Real-world uses:** Metrology, alignment, holography, bar-code scanners, education, bio-imaging.
- **Comparison point:** He-Ne $\to$ four-level, low power, high coherence. **Ruby** $\to$ three-level, pulsed, high power.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Argon Ion Laser
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT861 - OPTOELECTRONICS AND INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Argon Ion Laser

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard)

The **Argon Ion Laser** is a continuous-wave (CW), gas-discharge laser in which **singly-ionized argon atoms (Ar⁺)** serve as the active laser medium. Lasing is achieved via **electronic transitions between the $3s^2 3p^4 4p$ and $3s^2 3p^4 4s$ excited states** of the Ar⁺ ion, producing a characteristic multi-line emission spectrum dominated by the blue line at **488.0 nm** and the green line at **514.5 nm**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PEICT861 – Module 3):** The Argon Ion Laser is treated as the *canonical example of an ion laser*, demonstrating how **stepwise electron-impact ionization + excitation** creates the necessary population inversion in a *four-level ionic system* that cannot be lased in its neutral atomic state.

---

### Conceptual Analogy — "The High-Pressure Fire Hose"

Imagine a garden hose spraying water. Now imagine squeezing the nozzle so hard that the water becomes a *blade* sharp enough to cut steel — but to get that pressure, you must first *ionize* the water molecules. The Argon Ion Laser is conceptually the same:

1. You cannot just push argon atoms and expect them to lase — they are *too stable* in their neutral ground state.
2. You must first **ionize** the argon (strip away an electron using a high-energy electron collision) — this is like pressurizing the hose.
3. Then you must **further excite** the resulting ion using a second electron collision.
4. Once the excited ion relaxes, it emits a photon with just the right wavelength to bounce between two mirrors and amplify.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction from He-Ne Laser:**
> - He-Ne uses **neutral atoms**; Ar⁺ uses **singly-ionized atoms**.
> - He-Ne transitions occur between *metastable levels of metastable He*.
> - Ar⁺ requires **~15.76 eV** of energy per atom just to ionize argon, hence the **high current density discharge** and water cooling.

---

### Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Active medium | Singly-ionized Argon (Ar⁺) |
| Primary wavelengths | **488.0 nm (blue)**, **514.5 nm (green)** |
| Other strong lines | 351.1 nm, 363.8 nm, 457.9 nm, 476.5 nm, 496.5 nm, 501.7 nm, 528.7 nm |
| Spectral region | UV to Green |
| Output power (CW) | 1 mW – 20 W (commercial); up to 100 W (laboratory) |
| Wall-plug efficiency | $\eta \approx 0.05\% - 0.1\%$ (very low) |
| Ionization energy of Ar | **15.76 eV** |
| Excitation energy to 4p level | $\approx 35$ eV (from neutral Ar ground) |
| Operating gas pressure | 0.1 – 1.0 Torr (inside plasma tube) |
| Discharge current | 10 A – 50 A (typical) |
| Discharge voltage | 50 V – 200 V |
| Plasma tube length | 0.3 m – 2.0 m |
| Cooling | **Water cooling** (mandatory) |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Ionized Atoms? — The Physics Justification

Neutral argon has its **valence electrons tightly bound in the closed $3s^2 3p^6$ sub-shell**. The corresponding electronic transitions lie in the vacuum-ultraviolet (VUV), which is impractical to mirror and use. However, **once ionized**:

- The Ar⁺ ion has configuration $3s^2 3p^5$ (one "hole" in the p-subshell).
- The excited configurations $3s^2 3p^4 4s$ and $3s^2 3p^4 4p$ are energetically accessible.
- Transitions between these excited configurations produce *visible* photons.
- The "hole" in the p-shell acts like a single valence particle, simplifying the energy level structure.

> [!NOTE]
> This is why **all ion lasers (Ar⁺, Kr⁺)** lase in the **visible-to-UV** range — the ionization reconfigures the electron cloud to mimic a one-electron atom, just like hydrogen.

---

### 2.2 Energy Level Structure of Ar⁺

The Ar⁺ ion is **isoelectronic with Cl** (17 electrons). Using Paschen notation:

| State (Paschen) | Configuration | Lifetime | Role |
|---|---|---|---|
| $1s$ | $3s^2 3p^5$ (ground of Ar⁺) | Stable | Resets after ion recombines |
| $2p$ (lower laser) | $3s^2 3p^4 4s$ | $\tau_{2p} \approx 1$ ns | Rapidly decays → ideal lower level |
| $3p$ (upper laser) | $3s^2 3p^4 4p$ | $\tau_{3p} \approx 8$–$10$ ns | Holds population → ideal upper level |
| $4p, 4s, 5s, ...$ | Higher excited states | Short | Used in stepwise pumping |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Population Inversion Mechanism:**
> Because $\tau_{3p} > \tau_{2p}$, the **upper level (3p) is metastable-like**, while the **lower level (2p) is rapidly emptied** by radiative decay to the $1s$ ground state. This is a *natural* four-level laser system — **no special depopulation tricks needed**.

---

### 2.3 Pumping Mechanism — Stepwise Electron-Impact Excitation

The Ar⁺ laser uses **two successive electron collisions**:

**Step 1: Ionization**
$$\text{Ar} + e^{-}(E_1 \geq 15.76 \text{ eV}) \longrightarrow \text{Ar}^{+} + 2e^{-}$$

**Step 2: Excitation of the Ion**
$$\text{Ar}^{+} + e^{-}(E_2 \geq 19.6 \text{ eV}) \longrightarrow (\text{Ar}^{+})^{*} + e^{-}$$

**Step 3: Lasing Transition (3p → 2p)**
$$(\text{Ar}^{+})^{*} \longrightarrow \text{Ar}^{+} + h\nu \quad (\lambda = 488.0 \text{ nm or } 514.5 \text{ nm})$$

**Step 4: Repopulation of Ground State (3p⁵ → 3p⁴4s → 3p⁵)**
The lower laser level (2p) decays rapidly to the $1s$ ground of Ar⁺ with $\tau \approx 1$ ns, ensuring a *constantly empty* lower level.

---

### 2.4 Construction of an Argon Ion Laser

```
   [HR Mirror] ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ [OC Mirror]
                  ↑                 ↑
            Brewster            Brewster
            Window              Window
                  │                 │
        ┌─────────┴─────────────────┴─────────┐
        │        PLASMA TUBE (BeO or Graphite) │
        │   ┌─── Ar gas @ ~0.5 Torr ───┐      │
        │   │   with axial magnetic     │      │
        │   │   field B (~1000 G)       │      │
        │   └───────────────────────────┘      │
        └────┬───────────────────────────┬─────┘
             │                           │
          [CATHODE]                  [ANODE]
          (BaO coated,              (Water-cooled,
          heated)                    collects ions)
             │                           │
             └────── [Gas Return Bore] ──┘
                     (recirculates gas)
```

**Key Components Explained:**

1. **Plasma Tube (Boron Nitride or Beryllium Oxide):** Withstands high temperature and ion bombardment. Insulating material to confine the discharge.
2. **Brewster Windows:** Quartz windows angled at the Brewster angle $\theta_B = \tan^{-1}(n_2/n_1)$ to **minimize reflection loss** for the preferred polarization.
3. **Cathode:** Thermionic (oxide-coated) emitting copious electrons to sustain the discharge.
4. **Anode:** Water-cooled copper; collects the current and the Ar⁺ ions.
5. **Magnetic Field Coil:** A solenoid producing $\sim$1000 G along the tube axis **confines the plasma** to the central axis, increasing current density by 2–3× and improving efficiency.
6. **Gas Return Path:** A bypass tube from anode to cathode maintains gas pressure equilibrium and allows continuous operation.
7. **Optical Resonator:** Two highly reflecting mirrors — High Reflector (HR, R > 99.9%) and Output Coupler (OC, T ≈ 3–10%).
8. **Water Jacket:** Surrounds the plasma tube; **removes ~kW of waste heat**.

---

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize this table — these formulas are tested directly in KTU exams.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Photon energy | $E = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda}$ | $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s |
| 2 | Frequency of emitted photon | $\nu = \dfrac{c}{\lambda}$ | Hz |
| 3 | Wavenumber | $\tilde{\nu} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | cm⁻¹ |
| 4 | Power output (CW) | $P_{out} = \eta \cdot P_{in}$ | $\eta \approx 0.05\% - 0.1\%$ |
| 5 | Brewster angle | $\theta_B = \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{n_2}{n_1}\right)$ | No reflection for p-polarization |
| 6 | Threshold gain | $g_{th} = \dfrac{1}{2L}\ln\left(\dfrac{1}{R_1 R_2}\right) + \alpha$ | $L$ = tube length, $R_1, R_2$ = mirror reflectivities |
| 7 | Threshold population inversion | $\Delta N_{th} = \dfrac{8\pi n^2 \nu^2 \tau_{sp}}{c^2} \cdot g_{th}$ | Steady-state condition |
| 8 | FWHM of Doppler-broadened line | $\Delta \nu_D = \dfrac{2\nu_0}{c}\sqrt{\dfrac{2k_BT \ln 2}{M}}$ | $M$ = Ar⁺ ion mass |
| 9 | Small-signal gain coefficient | $g(\nu) = \dfrac{\lambda^2}{8\pi n^2 \tau_{sp}} \cdot g_N(\nu) \cdot \Delta N$ | $g_N(\nu)$ = lineshape function |
| 10 | Magnetic confinement condition | Plasma radius $r \leq \dfrac{1}{B}\sqrt{\dfrac{2m_e V}{e}}$ | Higher $B$ → tighter plasma |
| 11 | Electron impact ionization rate | $R_{ion} = n_e n_{Ar} \langle \sigma v \rangle$ | Function of $T_e$ |
| 12 | Stimulated emission cross-section | $\sigma_{21}(\nu) = \dfrac{\lambda^2}{4\pi n^2 \tau_{sp}} g_N(\nu)$ | For Ar⁺ 488 nm: $\sigma \approx 2.5 \times 10^{-16}$ m² |

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL NOTATION RULE (KTU Board Exam):** When writing *absolute value* in derivations (e.g., $\vert x \vert$ or $\vert \Delta N \vert$), **use \vert or \mid in LaTeX, never the pipe `|` character**, or it will break the markdown table rendering.

---

### 2.6 Why a Magnetic Field?

Without a magnetic field, electrons diffuse radially, striking the tube walls and losing energy. The axial magnetic field forces electrons into **helical (spiral) paths** around the field lines, effectively **trapping the plasma in the central core** of the tube. This:

- Increases the **electron density** in the lasing region by a factor of 2–3.
- **Reduces the required discharge current** for threshold.
- **Improves the beam quality** (TEM₀₀ mode selection).
- **Reduces erosion of the tube walls** (longer plasma tube life).

> [!NOTE]
> The axial magnetic field in an Ar⁺ laser is analogous to the *confinement field* in a tokamak — it keeps the hot particles where the action is.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Photon Energy of the 488.0 nm Blue Line

**Given:** $\lambda = 488.0 \text{ nm} = 488.0 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}$.

**Step 1 — Substitute into Planck–Einstein relation:**
$$E = \frac{hc}{\lambda}$$

**Step 2 — Plug in numerical values:**
$$E = \frac{(6.626 \times 10^{-34} \text{ J·s}) \times (3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s})}{488.0 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}}$$

**Step 3 — Compute numerator:**
$$hc = 6.626 \times 3 \times 10^{-26} = 19.878 \times 10^{-26} \text{ J·m}$$

**Step 4 — Divide by $\lambda$:**
$$E = \frac{19.878 \times 10^{-26}}{488.0 \times 10^{-9}} = \frac{19.878}{488.0} \times 10^{-17} \text{ J}$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate numerically:**
$$E = 0.04073 \times 10^{-17} \text{ J} = 4.073 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}$$

**Step 6 — Convert to electron-volts** (using $1 \text{ eV} = 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}$):
$$E = \frac{4.073 \times 10^{-19}}{1.602 \times 10^{-19}} \text{ eV} \approx 2.542 \text{ eV}$$

$$\boxed{E_{488} \approx 2.542 \text{ eV} \quad \text{and} \quad E_{515} \approx 2.409 \text{ eV}}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Doppler Linewidth of Ar⁺ at T = 2000 K

The Doppler FWHM (full width at half maximum) of a spectral line is:
$$\Delta \nu_D = \frac{2 \nu_0}{c} \sqrt{\frac{2 k_B T \ln 2}{M}}$$

**Step 1 — Compute the most probable speed of Ar⁺ ions at T = 2000 K:**
$$v_p = \sqrt{\frac{2 k_B T}{M}}$$

with $k_B = 1.381 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K, $T = 2000$ K, $M_{Ar^+} = 39.95 \times 1.66 \times 10^{-27} = 6.63 \times 10^{-26}$ kg.

$$v_p = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 1.381 \times 10^{-23} \times 2000}{6.63 \times 10^{-26}}}$$

$$v_p = \sqrt{\frac{5.524 \times 10^{-20}}{6.63 \times 10^{-26}}} = \sqrt{8.33 \times 10^{5}} = 913 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the central frequency for $\lambda = 488$ nm:**
$$\nu_0 = \frac{c}{\lambda} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{488 \times 10^{-9}} = 6.148 \times 10^{14} \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 3 — Assemble the Doppler FWHM formula:**
$$\Delta \nu_D = \frac{2 \nu_0}{c} \cdot v_p \cdot \sqrt{2 \ln 2}$$

Note: $\sqrt{2 \ln 2} \approx 1.177$.

$$\Delta \nu_D = \frac{2 \times 6.148 \times 10^{14}}{3 \times 10^8} \times 913 \times 1.177$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate term by term:**
$$\frac{2 \nu_0}{c} = \frac{1.2296 \times 10^{15}}{3 \times 10^8} = 4.099 \times 10^{6} \text{ m}^{-1}$$

$$\Delta \nu_D = 4.099 \times 10^{6} \times 913 \times 1.177$$

$$\Delta \nu_D = 4.099 \times 10^{6} \times 1074.6$$

$$\boxed{\Delta \nu_D \approx 4.40 \times 10^{9} \text{ Hz} \;\;(\text{i.e., } 4.4 \text{ GHz})}$$

**Interpretation:** The Ar⁺ gain linewidth is **~4.4 GHz**, which is *much narrower* than solid-state lasers (Nd:YAG is ~120 GHz). This is why Ar⁺ lasers are excellent sources for **high-resolution spectroscopy**.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Threshold Population Inversion

The threshold population inversion per unit volume is given by:
$$\Delta N_{th} = \frac{8 \pi n^2 \nu^2 \tau_{sp}}{c^2} \cdot g_{th}$$

**Step 1 — Define each variable for Ar⁺ 488 nm:**
- Refractive index of plasma: $n \approx 1.000$ (negligible correction)
- Central frequency: $\nu_0 = 6.148 \times 10^{14}$ Hz
- Spontaneous emission lifetime: $\tau_{sp} = 10 \text{ ns} = 10^{-8}$ s
- Threshold gain: assume $g_{th} = 0.5$ m⁻¹ (typical for 1 m tube, R₁R₂ = 0.99)

**Step 2 — Substitute:**
$$\Delta N_{th} = \frac{8 \pi (1)^2 (6.148 \times 10^{14})^2 (10^{-8})}{(3 \times 10^8)^2} \cdot 0.5$$

**Step 3 — Compute $\nu^2$:**
$$\nu^2 = (6.148 \times 10^{14})^2 = 3.780 \times 10^{29} \text{ Hz}^2$$

**Step 4 — Compute numerator:**
$$8 \pi \cdot \nu^2 \cdot \tau_{sp} = 8 \pi \cdot 3.780 \times 10^{29} \cdot 10^{-8} = 9.499 \times 10^{22}$$

**Step 5 — Compute denominator $c^2$:**
$$c^2 = 9 \times 10^{16}$$

**Step 6 — Divide:**
$$\frac{9.499 \times 10^{22}}{9 \times 10^{16}} = 1.055 \times 10^{6} \text{ m}^{-1}$$

**Step 7 — Multiply by $g_{th}$:**
$$\Delta N_{th} = 1.055 \times 10^{6} \times 0.5$$

$$\boxed{\Delta N_{th} \approx 5.28 \times 10^{5} \text{ ions/cm}^3}$$

**Interpretation:** Only about **half a million Ar⁺ ions per cm³** must be in the upper state more than the lower state. With a typical electron density of $\sim 10^{14}$ cm⁻³, the *fractional* inversion needed is only $\sim 5 \times 10^{-9}$ — extremely small, which is why the Ar⁺ laser reaches threshold easily.

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Output Power from Wall-Plug Efficiency

**Given:**
- Input electrical power: $P_{in} = V \cdot I = 100 \text{ V} \times 30 \text{ A} = 3000$ W
- Wall-plug efficiency: $\eta = 0.1\% = 10^{-3}$

**Step 1 — Apply efficiency definition:**
$$P_{out} = \eta \cdot P_{in}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute:**
$$P_{out} = 10^{-3} \times 3000$$

$$\boxed{P_{out} = 3 \text{ W}}$$

**Why such low efficiency?** Because the Ar⁺ laser dissipates the *vast majority* of input power as:
- Heat in the plasma (radiative + collisional)
- Heat at the anode (ion bombardment)
- Heat at the tube walls
- Spontaneous emission in *all directions*

This is why **water cooling is not optional** — without it, the tube would melt in seconds.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    ArGround[Ar Neutral Ground 3s2 3p6]
    ArIonGround[Ar+ Ion Ground 3s2 3p5 Level 1s]
    LowerLaser[Ar+ 4s Level 2p Lower Laser]
    UpperLaser[Ar+ 4p Level 3p Upper Laser]
    HigherLevels[Ar+ 5p 5s etc Higher Excited]
    ArGround -->|e- impact 15.76 eV| ArIonGround
    ArIonGround -->|e- impact excitation 19.6 eV| UpperLaser

```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block — Working Cycle Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1 Electron Impact Ionization of Ar] --> B[Step 2 Excitation of Ar+ to 4p Level]
    B --> C[Step 3 Population Inversion N4p greater than N4s]
    C --> D[Step 4 Stimulated Emission 488 or 514 nm]
    D --> E[Step 5 Photon Amplification in Resonator]
    E --> F[Step 6 Lower Level 4s Decays in 1 ns to Ground]
    F --> G[Step 7 New Electron Impact Re-excites]
    G --> A
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model Paper PEICT861)]**

**Q1. List any *six* prominent lasing wavelengths of the Argon Ion Laser and identify the strongest two.**

**Model Answer:**
The Argon Ion Laser produces lasing action at multiple wavelengths in the UV–visible range. The prominent lines are:
1. **351.1 nm** (UV)
2. **363.8 nm** (UV)
3. **457.9 nm** (blue-violet)
4. **476.5 nm** (blue)
5. **488.0 nm** (blue) — **strongest single line**
6. **496.5 nm** (blue-green)
7. **501.7 nm** (green)
8. **514.5 nm** (green) — **second strongest line**
9. **528.7 nm** (green)

The two strongest transitions are at **488.0 nm (blue)** and **514.5 nm (green)**, corresponding to $4p \to 4s$ transitions of the Ar⁺ ion. [3 marks]

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model Paper PEICT861)]**

**Q2. Why is the wall-plug efficiency of an Argon Ion Laser very low (≈ 0.1%)? Justify.**

**Model Answer:**
The wall-plug efficiency $\eta$ of an Ar⁺ laser is very low (typically $0.05\% - 0.1\%$) because:

1. **High ionization cost:** Each Ar atom must first be ionized (costing 15.76 eV) *before* it can be excited. This energy is mostly lost as heat when Ar⁺ ions recombine at the walls.
2. **Anode dissipation:** The anode intercepts energetic ions and electrons, dissipating power as heat.
3. **Spontaneous emission loss:** Photons emitted in directions other than along the cavity axis do not contribute to lasing.
4. **Wide spectral output:** Energy is spread over many wavelengths, not concentrated at a single useful one.
5. **Plasma radiation:** The hot plasma radiates bremsstrahlung and line radiation, which is wasted.

Hence only ~0.1% of input electrical power is converted to coherent laser output, while ~99.9% must be removed by water cooling. [3 marks]

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**
> **Module Internal Choice: Answer ANY ONE of the two alternatives**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** With a neat energy level diagram, explain the **construction and working principle of an Argon Ion Laser**. Discuss the role of the magnetic field and the Brewster windows. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Derive the **threshold population inversion density** for the 488.0 nm line of the Ar⁺ laser, given that the cavity length is 1 m, mirror reflectivities are R₁ = 1.0 and R₂ = 0.98, and the spontaneous lifetime is 10 ns. Assume a Lorentzian lineshape. **[7 marks]**

##### Model Solution

**(a) Construction and Working:**

The Argon Ion Laser consists of the following components:

1. **Plasma tube** (BeO or graphite, ~30 cm to 2 m long) containing low-pressure Ar gas (~0.5 Torr).
2. **Brewster windows** (quartz) at both ends, angled at $\theta_B = \tan^{-1}(n)$ to transmit p-polarized light with zero reflection loss.
3. **Optical resonator** formed by two mirrors — HR (R = 100%) and OC (R = 98%).
4. **Thermionic cathode** (BaO-coated) emitting electrons.
5. **Water-cooled anode** collecting the current and ions.
6. **Axial magnetic solenoid** (~1000 G) confining the plasma.
7. **Gas return path** from anode to cathode.
8. **Water cooling jacket** around the tube.
9. **High-current power supply** (50–200 V, 10–50 A).

**Working Principle:**

- A high voltage applied across the electrodes creates a dense discharge.
- Free electrons collide with Ar atoms:
  $$\text{Ar} + e^- \to \text{Ar}^+ + 2e^-$$
- The resulting Ar⁺ ions are further excited by electron impact:
  $$\text{Ar}^+ + e^- \to (\text{Ar}^+)^* + e^-$$
- The excited Ar⁺ decays radiatively from the metastable-like 4p level (τ ≈ 10 ns) to the 4s level (τ ≈ 1 ns), emitting **488.0 nm or 514.5 nm photons**.
- Because the lower level (4s) decays in 1 ns while the upper (4p) holds for 10 ns, **population inversion is automatic** — a *natural four-level system*.
- Photons traveling along the axis are reflected back and forth by the mirrors, triggering stimulated emission. The output is taken through the partially transmitting OC.

**Role of the magnetic field:** Confines the plasma to the tube axis (helical electron motion), increasing electron density by 2–3×, lowering threshold current, and improving efficiency and beam quality. [7 marks]

**(b) Threshold Population Inversion Derivation:**

**Step 1 — Threshold gain condition:**
$$g_{th} = \frac{1}{2L} \ln \left( \frac{1}{R_1 R_2} \right)$$

**Step 2 — Substitute L = 1 m, R₁ = 1.0, R₂ = 0.98:**
$$g_{th} = \frac{1}{2 \times 1} \ln \left( \frac{1}{1.0 \times 0.98} \right) = \frac{1}{2} \ln(1.0204)$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate ln:**
$$g_{th} = \frac{1}{2} \times 0.0202 = 0.0101 \text{ m}^{-1}$$

**Step 4 — Apply threshold inversion formula:**
$$\Delta N_{th} = \frac{8 \pi n^2 \nu^2 \tau_{sp}}{c^2} g_{th}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute values:**
- $\nu = 6.148 \times 10^{14}$ Hz
- $\tau_{sp} = 10^{-8}$ s
- $n = 1.0$
- $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s
- $g_{th} = 0.0101$ m⁻¹

$$\Delta N_{th} = \frac{8 \pi (1)^2 (6.148 \times 10^{14})^2 (10^{-8})}{(3 \times 10^8)^2} \times 0.0101$$

**Step 6 — Evaluate the constant factor (from earlier derivation, Sec 3.3):**
$$\frac{8 \pi \nu^2 \tau_{sp}}{c^2} \approx 1.055 \times 10^{6} \text{ m}^{-1}$$

**Step 7 — Multiply by $g_{th}$:**
$$\Delta N_{th} = 1.055 \times 10^{6} \times 0.0101 \approx 1.066 \times 10^{4} \text{ m}^{-3}$$

**Step 8 — Convert to per cm³:**
$$\boxed{\Delta N_{th} \approx 1.07 \times 10^{-2} \text{ ions/cm}^3}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Note: The very small numerical value arises from the *low mirror loss* (R₂ = 0.98 is high). In practice, OC mirror transmission (2%) and other cavity losses (T = 2–5%) give $g_{th} \approx 0.5$ m⁻¹, yielding $\Delta N_{th} \approx 5 \times 10^5$ cm⁻³. [7 marks]

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **pumping mechanism** of the Argon Ion Laser. Compare it briefly with the pumping in the He-Ne laser. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A laboratory Ar⁺ laser operates at 488.0 nm with an output power of 5 W. If the input power is 4 kW, calculate (i) the wall-plug efficiency, (ii) the number of photons emitted per second, and (iii) the energy of a single photon in eV. **[7 marks]**

##### Model Solution

**(a) Pumping Mechanism:**

The Ar⁺ laser is pumped by **electrical discharge** (not optical pumping as in dye or solid-state lasers). The mechanism is **stepwise electron-impact excitation**:

**Step 1 — Ionization by electron impact (requires E ≥ 15.76 eV):**
$$Ar + e^{-}(\text{fast}) \to Ar^{+} + 2e^{-}$$

**Step 2 — Excitation of the Ar⁺ ion (requires E ≥ 19.6 eV):**
$$Ar^{+} + e^{-}(\text{fast}) \to (Ar^{+})^{*}_{4p} + e^{-}(\text{slow})$$

These are **inelastic collisions** where the electron loses energy equal to the excitation energy of the ion.

**Step 3 — Upper-level lifetime and inversion:** The 4p level is metastable-like (τ ≈ 10 ns). Continuous electron bombardment keeps the upper level populated.

**Step 4 — Lower level drain:** The 4s level (τ ≈ 1 ns) decays rapidly to the Ar⁺ ground state, ensuring inversion is maintained.

**Comparison with He-Ne laser:**
- **He-Ne** uses **resonant energy transfer** from metastable He (2¹S, 2³S) to Ne atoms; the He atoms are excited by electron impact, then transfer energy by collision.
- **Ar⁺** uses **direct electron-impact excitation** of the ion itself (no transfer step).
- **He-Ne** operates at low current (mA range); **Ar⁺** requires high current (tens of A) because of the additional ionization step.
- **He-Ne** is a 4-level system; **Ar⁺** is also 4-level but with ionic levels. [7 marks]

**(b) Numerical Problem:**

**Given:** $\lambda = 488.0$ nm, $P_{out} = 5$ W, $P_{in} = 4000$ W.

**(i) Wall-plug efficiency:**
$$\eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = \frac{5}{4000} = 0.00125$$
$$\boxed{\eta = 0.125\%}$$

**(ii) Number of photons per second:**
Energy of one photon:
$$E = \frac{hc}{\lambda} = \frac{6.626 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^{8}}{488 \times 10^{-9}} = 4.073 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}$$

Number of photons emitted per second:
$$\dot{N} = \frac{P_{out}}{E} = \frac{5}{4.073 \times 10^{-19}}$$
$$\boxed{\dot{N} \approx 1.23 \times 10^{19} \text{ photons/second}}$$

**(iii) Energy of a single photon in eV:**
$$E = \frac{4.073 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}}{1.602 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J/eV}} = 2.542 \text{ eV}$$
$$\boxed{E \approx 2.54 \text{ eV}}$$ [7 marks]

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls for Ar⁺ Laser Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing Ar (neutral) with Ar⁺ (ion):** *Many students write "argon atoms" when the active medium is ionized argon ions.* The laser is an **ion laser**, not a neutral gas laser. **[-2 marks]**
> 2. **Writing "population inversion is hard" — WRONG:** The Ar⁺ laser has a *natural* 4-level system (τ_upper > τ_lower). **Do not** say it requires special techniques. **[-1 mark]**
> 3. **Forgetting to mention the magnetic field:** A diagram of an Ar⁺ laser without the axial solenoid is incomplete. **[-2 marks]**
> 4. **Confusing wall-plug efficiency with quantum efficiency:** Quantum efficiency (488 nm) is $\approx 7\%$, but wall-plug is $\approx 0.1\%$. Examiners will test this distinction. **[-2 marks]**
> 5. **Forgetting Brewster windows:** These are *essential* to avoid reflection losses and to polarize the output. **[-1 mark]**
> 6. **Wrong wavelength identification:** 488.0 nm is **blue** (not violet), 514.5 nm is **green** (not yellow). **[-1 mark]**
