<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Power Required to Heat a Material from $T_1$ to $T_2$

**Statement:** Find the average laser power needed to raise the temperature of a mass $m$ from $T_1$ to $T_2$ in time $t$.

**Step 1 — Write the first law of thermodynamics for the workpiece:**
The heat absorbed equals the temperature rise times the heat capacity:
$$Q = m c (T_2 - T_1)$$

**Step 2 — Express the rate of heat delivery (power):**
Average power is energy per unit time:
$$P = \dfrac{Q}{t}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $Q$ into $P$:**
$$P = \dfrac{m c (T_2 - T_1)}{t}$$

**Step 4 — Apply reflectivity correction:**
Only the absorbed fraction contributes to heating:
$$P_{incident} = \dfrac{m c (T_2 - T_1)}{t \cdot (1 - R)}$$

This is the **fundamental heating formula** in laser material processing.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Power Required to Melt a Material Completely

**Statement:** Find the laser power required to raise mass $m$ from $T_0$ to its melting point $T_m$, then fully convert it to liquid.

**Step 1 — Sensible heat to reach melting point:**
$$Q_1 = m c (T_m - T_0)$$

**Step 2 — Latent heat absorbed during phase change at $T_m$:**
$$Q_2 = m L_f$$

**Step 3 — Total heat required:**
$$Q_{total} = Q_1 + Q_2 = m c (T_m - T_0) + m L_f$$

**Step 4 — Convert to required power over time $t$:**
$$P = \dfrac{m c (T_m - T_0) + m L_f}{t \cdot (1 - R)}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Power Required to Vaporize a Cylindrical Volume (Drilling)

**Statement:** A pulsed laser drills a cylindrical hole of diameter $d$ and depth $h$ in a metal sheet. Find the average power required per pulse, given pulse duration $\tau$.

**Step 1 — Volume of the cylindrical hole:**
$$V = \dfrac{\pi d^2 h}{4}$$

**Step 2 — Mass removed:**
$$m = \rho V = \dfrac{\rho \pi d^2 h}{4}$$

**Step 3 — Total energy required for complete vaporization (from $T_0$):**
$$Q_{total} = m \left[ c (T_b - T_0) + L_f + L_v \right]$$

where $T_b$ is the boiling point of the material.

**Step 4 — Substituting $m$:**
$$Q_{total} = \dfrac{\rho \pi d^2 h}{4} \left[ c (T_b - T_0) + L_f + L_v \right]$$

**Step 5 — Pulse energy required:**
$$E_p = \dfrac{Q_{total}}{(1 - R)}$$

**Step 6 — Average power during pulse:**
$$\boxed{P_{avg} = \dfrac{E_p}{\tau} = \dfrac{\rho \pi d^2 h \left[ c (T_b - T_0) + L_f + L_v \right]}{4 \tau (1 - R)}}$$

**Step 7 — Drilling rate (depth per second if continuous):**
$$v = \dfrac{h}{t_{drill}}$$

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU 14-Mark Problem)

**Problem:** A Nd:YAG laser ($\lambda = 1.06\ \mu\text{m}$) is used to drill a 1 mm diameter, 2 mm deep hole in a steel plate. Given:

- Density of steel: $\rho = 7850 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- Specific heat: $c = 450 \text{ J/kg·K}$
- Initial temperature: $T_0 = 25°\text{C}$
- Boiling point: $T_b = 2900°\text{C}$
- Latent heat of fusion: $L_f = 2.7 \times 10^5 \text{ J/kg}$
- Latent heat of vaporization: $L_v = 6.1 \times 10^6 \text{ J/kg}$
- Reflectivity at 1.06 µm: $R = 0.35$
- Pulse duration: $\tau = 0.5 \text{ ms}$

**Find:** Average power per pulse.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Volume:**
$$V = \dfrac{\pi (1 \times 10^{-3})^2 (2 \times 10^{-3})}{4} = 1.571 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 2 — Mass:**
$$m = \rho V = 7850 \times 1.571 \times 10^{-9} = 1.233 \times 10^{-5} \text{ kg}$$

**Step 3 — Sensible heat:**
$$Q_s = m c \Delta T = 1.233 \times 10^{-5} \times 450 \times (2900 - 25)$$
$$Q_s = 1.233 \times 10^{-5} \times 450 \times 2875 = 15.96 \text{ J}$$

**Step 4 — Latent heat of fusion:**
$$Q_f = m L_f = 1.233 \times 10^{-5} \times 2.7 \times 10^5 = 3.33 \text{ J}$$

**Step 5 — Latent heat of vaporization:**
$$Q_v = m L_v = 1.233 \times 10^{-5} \times 6.1 \times 10^6 = 75.21 \text{ J}$$

**Step 6 — Total heat:**
$$Q_{total} = 15.96 + 3.33 + 75.21 = 94.50 \text{ J}$$

**Step 7 — Absorbed energy (after reflectivity loss):**
$$E_p = \dfrac{Q_{total}}{1 - R} = \dfrac{94.50}{0.65} = 145.38 \text{ J}$$

**Step 8 — Average power:**
$$\boxed{P_{avg} = \dfrac{E_p}{\tau} = \dfrac{145.38}{0.5 \times 10^{-3}} = 2.91 \times 10^5 \text{ W} \approx 291 \text{ kW}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This high peak power is typical for **Q-switched pulsed Nd:YAG lasers** used in industrial drilling.

## 3.5 Derivation 4: Power for Laser Trimming

For trimming a thin-film resistor of length $\ell$, width $w$, thickness $t_f$, the laser removes a kerf of width $w_k$ and depth $t_f$ at velocity $v_t$. The volume removal rate is:
$$\dot{V} = w_k \cdot t_f \cdot v_t$$

The power required:
$$P_{trim} = \rho \dot{V} \left[ c (T_m - T_0) + L_f \right] \cdot \dfrac{1}{(1 - R)}$$

Trim resolution (smallest resistance change):
$$\dfrac{\Delta R}{R} \approx \dfrac{w_k}{w}$$

## 3.6 Power Density vs. Process Regime (Analytical Matrix)

| Process | Power Density (W/cm²) | Interaction Time | Dominant Mechanism |
|---------|----------------------|------------------|--------------------|
| Annealing | $10^3$ – $10^4$ | seconds | Bulk heating |
| Hardening | $10^4$ – $10^5$ | 0.1–1 s | Surface heating |
| Melting/Welding (conduction) | $10^5$ – $10^6$ | 1 ms – 1 s | Melt pool conduction |
| Welding (keyhole) | $10^6$ – $10^7$ | 1–10 ms | Vapor cavity + keyhole |
| Drilling/Cutting | $10^6$ – $10^9$ | µs – ms | Vaporization |
| Ablation (excimer) | $10^7$ – $10^9$ | ns – fs | Photothermal/photochemical |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner Note:** A 2-mark question often asks "Which laser is used for metal cutting?" The correct answer is **fiber laser or Nd:YAG (1.06 µm)** because of **lower reflectivity at near-IR** compared to CO₂ at 10.6 µm. Always justify by quoting the $(1-R)$ absorption term.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Flow Diagram — Laser Material Processing Regimes

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Laser Beam Incident on Workpiece] --> B{Beam Absorbed?}
    B -- No / Reflected --> C[Reflected Energy Lost]
    B -- Yes --> D[Power Density Check]
    D --> D1[10³ – 10⁵ W/cm²]
    D --> D2[10⁵ – 10⁶ W/cm²]
    D --> D3[10⁶ – 10⁷ W/cm²]
    D --> D4[>10⁷ W/cm²]
    D1 --> P1[HEATING: Annealing, Stress Relief]
    D2 --> P2[MELTING: Welding Conduction Mode]
    D3 --> P3[KEYHOLE WELDING / CUTTING]
    D4 --> P4[VAPORIZATION / ABLATION: Drilling]
    P1 --> APP1[Application: Auto Parts Hardening]
    P2 --> APP2[Application: Seam Welding Sheets]
    P3 --> APP3[Application: Deep Penetration Welding]
    P4 --> APP4[Application: Turbine Blade Hole Drilling]
```

## 4.2 Block Architecture — Laser Welding System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LASERSRC[LASER SOURCE]
        LS[Nd:YAG / Fiber Laser 1.06 µm]
    end
    subgraph BEAMDLV[BEAM DELIVERY]
        FO[Fiber Optic Cable]
        FL[Focusing Lens]
    end
    subgraph WORKSTN[WORKSTATION]
        WP[Workpiece Clamp]
        SH[Shield Gas Nozzle Ar/He]
        XY[X-Y-Z Motion Stage]
    end
    subgraph CONTROL[CONTROL UNIT]
        PLC[PLC Controller]
        TMP[Temperature Sensor]
        VIS[Vision / Camera]
    end
    LS --> FO --> FL --> WP
    SH --> WP
    XY --> WP
    PLC --> LS
    TMP --> PLC
    VIS --> PLC
```

## 4.3 Functional Flow — Optical Fiber Splicing

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    F1[Fiber 1 Cleaved] --> ALIGN[Core Alignment via Microscope]
    F2[Fiber 2 Cleaved] --> ALIGN
    ALIGN --> GAP[Set Gap ~10 µm]
    GAP --> ARC[Electric Arc / CO2 Laser Heats Tips]
    ARC --> MELT[Both Tips Melt]
    MELT --> PUSH[Push Fibers Together]
    PUSH --> FUSE[Fusion Splice Formed]
    FUSE --> TEST[Splice Loss Measurement Target <0.1 dB]
```

## 4.4 Process Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Need to Join Materials?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Optical Fiber?}
    Q1 -- No --> Q3{Need to Remove Material?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> SP[Fusion Splicing]
    Q2 -- No --> Q4{Metals?}
    Q4 -- Yes --> WL[Laser Welding Conduction or Keyhole]
    Q4 -- No --> WL2[CO2 Laser Welding]
    Q3 -- Yes --> Q5{Through-hole or Groove?}
    Q5 -- Through-hole --> DR[Laser Drilling]
    Q5 -- Groove --> SC[Scribing or Cutting]
    Q3 -- No --> Q6{Adjust Electrical Value?}
    Q6 -- Yes --> TR[Laser Trimming]
    Q6 -- No --> Q7{Heat Treat Only?}
    Q7 -- Yes --> HT[Laser Heating Annealing Hardening]
```

## 4.5 Power Density vs. Spot Size Chart (Conceptual)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Beam Power P fixed] --> B[Spot Diameter d]
    B --> C[I = 4P / pi d squared]
    C --> D1[d = 10 mm  I = 0.0013 W/cm squared]
    C --> D2[d = 1 mm   I = 0.13 W/cm squared]
    C --> D3[d = 0.1 mm I = 13 W/cm squared]
    C --> D4[d = 0.01 mm I = 1300 W/cm squared]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — 3 Mark Questions

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
**Why are Nd:YAG lasers preferred over CO₂ lasers for metal processing?**

**Model Answer:**
Metals have a **lower reflectivity** at the Nd:YAG wavelength of **1.06 µm** (~30–60%) compared to the CO₂ wavelength of **10.6 µm** (~90–95%). Hence a larger fraction of incident power is absorbed by the metal when using Nd:YAG. Additionally, the 1.06 µm radiation can be transmitted through optical fibers, enabling flexible beam delivery for robotic welding and cutting in industrial environments. CO₂ radiation, being mid-infrared, requires articulated mirror arms.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**Distinguish between laser heating and laser melting.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Laser Heating | Laser Melting |
|-----------|---------------|---------------|
| Temperature | Below melting point | At/above melting point |
| Phase change | None | Solid → Liquid |
| Power density | $10^3$–$10^5$ W/cm² | $10^5$–$10^7$ W/cm² |
| Latent heat | Not required | $L_f$ required |
| Application | Annealing, hardening | Welding, cladding |

---

## PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Model 2024]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the **keyhole mode of laser welding**. Discuss the role of **shielding gas** and **plasma formation**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **CO₂ laser** of power **2 kW** is used to cut a 6 mm thick stainless steel plate at a speed of **1 m/min**. If the kerf width is 0.5 mm and density of steel is 7850 kg/m³, specific heat is 500 J/kg·K, melting point is 1500 °C, initial temperature is 25 °C, $L_f = 2.7 \times 10^5$ J/kg, and absorption coefficient at 10.6 µm is 5%, calculate the **process efficiency**. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Answer to Q-A (a):

**Keyhole Welding Diagram (block-level):**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    LB[Laser Beam] --> FOC[Focussed to Spot]
    FOC --> KV[Keyhole Vapour Cavity]
    KV --> MP[Melting of Side Walls]
    MP --> FR[Flow and Re-solidification behind Keyhole]
    FR --> WS[Welded Bead]
    SG[Shield Gas Ar/He] -.-> KV
    PL[Plasma Plume] -.-> KV
```

**Explanation:**

1. The laser beam is focused to a high power density ($>10^6$ W/cm²).
2. The material at the focal point **vaporizes**, creating a deep narrow cavity called the **keyhole**.
3. The keyhole behaves like a black-body trap: laser radiation enters the cavity and undergoes **multiple reflections**, greatly enhancing absorption (up to 90% even for highly reflective metals).
4. Molten metal flows around the keyhole and **re-solidifies behind it**, forming a deep, narrow weld.
5. **Shielding gas (Argon/Helium)** is blown coaxially with the beam to:
   - Prevent oxidation of the weld pool.
   - Blow away the **plasma plume** (ionized metal vapor) which would otherwise absorb/scatter the beam (plasma shielding effect).
6. Plasma formation is both useful (deep penetration) and problematic (energy loss above the workpiece).

---

### Model Answer to Q-A (b):

**Given:** $P = 2000$ W, thickness $t_k = 6$ mm = $6 \times 10^{-3}$ m, kerf width $w = 0.5$ mm = $5 \times 10^{-4}$ m, cutting speed $v = 1$ m/min = 1/60 m/s, $\rho = 7850$ kg/m³, $c = 500$ J/kg·K, $T_m = 1500$ °C, $T_0 = 25$ °C, $L_f = 2.7 \times 10^5$ J/kg, $A_{abs} = 5\% = 0.05$.

**Step 1 — Volume removed per unit length (per metre of cut):**
$$V = w \cdot t_k \cdot 1 = 5 \times 10^{-4} \times 6 \times 10^{-3} \times 1 = 3 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3/\text{m}$$

**Step 2 — Mass removed per metre:**
$$m = \rho V = 7850 \times 3 \times 10^{-6} = 2.355 \times 10^{-2} \text{ kg/m}$$

**Step 3 — Energy required per metre (melting only, assuming vaporization not required):**
$$E_{req} = m [c(T_m - T_0) + L_f]$$
$$E_{req} = 2.355 \times 10^{-2} \times [500 \times 1475 + 2.7 \times 10^5]$$
$$E_{req} = 2.355 \times 10^{-2} \times [737500 + 270000]$$
$$E_{req} = 2.355 \times 10^{-2} \times 1.0075 \times 10^6$$
$$E_{req} = 23726 \text{ J/m}$$

**Step 4 — Energy delivered per metre by the laser:**
$$E_{del} = \dfrac{P}{v} = \dfrac{2000}{1/60} = 120000 \text{ J/m}$$

**Step 5 — Energy absorbed (5% of delivered):**
$$E_{abs} = 0.05 \times 120000 = 6000 \text{ J/m}$$

**Step 6 — Process efficiency (useful vs absorbed):**
$$\eta = \dfrac{E_{req}}{E_{abs}} \times 100\% = \dfrac{23726}{6000} \times 100\% \approx 395\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examination Pitfall (KTU Valuation):** Students often compute efficiency as $E_{req}/E_{del}$ and report ~19.8%, forgetting that only 5% is absorbed. **Correct approach:** efficiency = useful energy / energy actually absorbed in the workpiece = $23726 / 6000 = 3.95$ (i.e., absorbed energy is insufficient — the process cannot proceed at this speed with only 5% absorption; this proves why **Nd:YAG with 30–40% absorption** is preferred for steel). Comment on this physical insight in your answer for full marks.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Explain **laser scribing** of silicon wafers for solar cell fabrication. Compare scribing using a **Q-switched pulsed laser** versus a **CW laser**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **Q-switched Nd:YAG laser** with pulse energy 1 J, pulse duration 10 ns, and repetition rate 1 kHz is used for drilling 0.5 mm diameter holes in an aluminum sheet. Calculate the **average power**, **peak power**, and the **number of photons per pulse** at $\lambda = 1.06\ \mu\text{m}$. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Answer to Q-B (a):

**Laser Scribing of Silicon Wafers:**

1. Silicon wafers (typically 150–200 mm diameter, ~200 µm thick) must be **diced** into individual solar cells.
2. Conventional **mechanical dicing** with a diamond saw causes chipping, kerf loss (~200 µm), and micro-cracks.
3. **Laser scribing** uses a focused pulsed laser (typically **Nd:YAG or fiber laser at 1.06 µm**, or **green 532 nm** for better absorption by Si) to trace a shallow groove (~50 µm deep).
4. The laser-induced groove acts as a **stress concentrator**. Subsequent mechanical snapping or a second laser pass separates the wafer cleanly.
5. Scribing depth must be **controlled to ~30–50% of wafer thickness** for clean fracture.

**Comparison: Q-switched pulsed vs. CW laser:**

| Parameter | Q-switched Pulsed | CW Laser |
|-----------|-------------------|----------|
| Pulse duration | ns to ps | continuous |
| Peak power | Very high (MW) | Constant (kW) |
| Heat-affected zone (HAZ) | Very small | Large |
| Scribing quality | Clean, precise | More thermal damage |
| Throughput | Lower per pulse, high repetition | Higher continuous speed |
| Best for | Microelectronics, thin wafers | Thick wafers, ceramic scribing |

---

### Model Answer to Q-B (b):

**Given:** $E_p = 1$ J, $\tau = 10 \text{ ns} = 10^{-8}$ s, repetition rate $f = 1 \text{ kHz} = 1000$ Hz, $\lambda = 1.06\ \mu\text{m}$.

**Step 1 — Average power:**
$$P_{avg} = E_p \times f = 1 \times 1000 = 1000 \text{ W} = 1 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 2 — Peak power:**
$$P_{peak} = \dfrac{E_p}{\tau} = \dfrac{1}{10^{-8}} = 10^8 \text{ W} = 100 \text{ MW}$$

**Step 3 — Energy of one photon:**
$$E_{ph} = \dfrac{h c}{\lambda} = \dfrac{6.626 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^8}{1.06 \times 10^{-6}}$$
$$E_{ph} = \dfrac{1.9878 \times 10^{-25}}{1.06 \times 10^{-6}} = 1.875 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}$$

**Step 4 — Number of photons per pulse:**
$$N_{ph} = \dfrac{E_p}{E_{ph}} = \dfrac{1}{1.875 \times 10^{-19}} = 5.33 \times 10^{18} \text{ photons/pulse}$$

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Material Processing Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $(1-R)$ term** — students use incident power directly instead of absorbed power. Loss: 2 marks.
> 2. **Mixing up $L_f$ and $L_v$** — fusion vs vaporization latent heats differ by an order of magnitude. State which phase change you are computing.
> 3. **Unit inconsistency** — Power density in W/cm² but formula derived in W/m². Always convert $d$ to **cm** when $I$ is in W/cm².
> 4. **Not stating the laser wavelength assumption** — reflectivities depend on $\lambda$. Always specify "for Nd:YAG at 1.06 µm" or "for CO₂ at 10.6 µm".
> 5. **Skipping process efficiency $\eta$** — real lasers never achieve 100% efficiency. Mention it explicitly in the formula.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Four regimes:** heating → melting → vaporization → plasma; distinguished by **power density** and **interaction time**.
- ✅ **Heating** below $T_m$ — used for annealing/hardening; power density $10^3$–$10^5$ W/cm².
- ✅ **Melting** — adds the latent heat of fusion $L_f$ to the heat balance.
- ✅ **Vaporization** — adds $L_f + L_v$ to the heat balance.
- ✅ **Scribing** — shallow groove for stress-guided fracture; uses Q-switched pulsed lasers.
- ✅ **Splicing** — fusion of optical fibers; target loss < 0.1 dB.
- ✅ **Welding modes:** **conduction** (shallow, wide) vs **keyhole** (deep, narrow, uses vapor cavity).
- ✅ **Trimming** — pulsed ablation of thin/thick-film resistors for value adjustment.
- ✅ **Wavelength rule:** **Nd:YAG/fiber (1.06 µm) for metals**, **CO₂ (10.6 µm) for non-metals**.
- ✅ **Master formula:** $P = \dfrac{\rho V [c \Delta T + L_f + L_v]}{t \cdot \eta \cdot (1-R)}$
- ✅ **Average power** = energy per pulse × repetition rate.
- ✅ **Peak power** = pulse energy ÷ pulse duration (Q-switched gives MW–GW peak).
- ✅ **Photon energy** $E_{ph} = hc/\lambda$ — needed for counting photons per pulse.
- ✅ **Process efficiency $\eta$** must be included in all power calculation problems.
- ✅ **Drilling rate** $v = h/t$ — link between depth, time, and power.
- ✅ **Shield gas (Ar/He)** prevents oxidation and clears plasma plume in welding.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Laser Gyroscope, Holography: Principles and Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT861 - OPTOELECTRONICS AND INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Laser Gyroscope, Holography: Principles and Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Laser Gyroscope

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned):**
> A **Laser Gyroscope** is an optoelectronic rotation-sensing device that measures angular velocity (or absolute rotation) by exploiting the **Sagnac Effect** — the relative optical path difference produced when two counter-propagating light beams traverse a closed rotating loop. The output beat frequency between the two beams is directly proportional to the rotational rate of the platform.

**Intuitive Analogy — The Spinning Ice Skater:**
Imagine two ice skaters running on a circular ice track in opposite directions while the entire track slowly rotates. The skater running *with* the rotation has to cover a slightly *longer* path to complete a lap, while the skater running *against* the rotation covers a *shorter* path. If both skaters run at the same speed, the one with the longer path will arrive later at the starting line. The *time difference* between their arrivals tells you exactly how fast the track is rotating — even though the skaters themselves are running at the same intrinsic speed. In a laser gyroscope, photons are the "skaters," the ring cavity is the "track," and the beat frequency is the "time difference detector."

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Metric:** The Sagnac-induced frequency difference is typically on the order of a few **Hz to kHz** for Earth-rate rotation, requiring ultra-precise phase-locking electronics.

---

### 1.2 Holography

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned):**
> **Holography** is a two-step lensless imaging technique that records both the **amplitude** and the **phase** of light waves scattered from an object (producing a *hologram*) and later reconstructs a full three-dimensional wavefront by illuminating the recorded interference pattern with a coherent reference beam.

**Intuitive Analogy — The Frozen Pond Memory:**
Think of throwing two stones into a still pond at the same time: one stone represents light from the object, the other from a reference source. The circular ripples they create **interfere** with each other, producing a complex criss-cross pattern of peaks and troughs on the water surface. If you could *freeze* that water instantly, you would have a record of *everything* about the original stones — their size, position, and timing — encoded in the ripple pattern. Later, by sending another set of ripples through the frozen pattern, the original waves re-emerge. A photograph only records the *brightness* (amplitude), but a hologram records the *ripple pattern* (amplitude + phase) — that's why holograms let you see 3D depth.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Distinction:** Photography → 2D intensity recording. Holography → 3D wavefront recording (both amplitude and phase).

---

### 1.3 Why These Topics Matter in Optoelectronics

| Aspect | Laser Gyroscope | Holography |
|---|---|---|
| **Primary Physical Principle** | Sagnac Effect | Optical Interference & Diffraction |
| **Key Light Property Used** | Frequency (coherence) | Phase (coherence) |
| **Main Output** | Beat frequency (rotation rate) | 3D reconstructed image |
| **Coherence Requirement** | Very High (laser source) | Very High (laser source) |
| **Application Domain** | Navigation, Aerospace | Security, Data Storage, Medical Imaging |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Sagnac Effect — Theoretical Foundation

The Sagnac Effect is the cornerstone of the laser gyroscope. It arises from the **Relativistic invariance of the speed of light** in a rotating frame.

**Operational Logic (Step-by-Step):**

- **Step 1 — Frame Rotation:** Consider a circular ring of radius $R$ rotating with angular velocity $\Omega$ about an axis perpendicular to the plane of the ring.
- **Step 2 — Co-rotating Beam:** A light beam traveling in the *same direction* as the rotation must traverse a longer path because the destination mirror is moving away.

$$L_{cw} = 2\pi R + \Delta L = 2\pi R + \Omega R \, t_{cw}$$

- **Step 3 — Counter-rotating Beam:** A beam traveling in the *opposite direction* traverses a shorter path.

$$L_{ccw} = 2\pi R - \Delta L = 2\pi R - \Omega R \, t_{ccw}$$

- **Step 4 — Travel Time Difference:** The difference in transit times around the loop is:

$$\Delta t = t_{cw} - t_{ccw} = \frac{4\pi R^2 \Omega}{c^2 - R^2 \Omega^2}$$

- **Step 5 — Non-Relativistic Approximation:** For $\Omega \ll c/R$ (true for any practical gyroscope), this simplifies to:

$$\Delta t \approx \frac{4 A \, \Omega}{c^2}$$

where $A = \pi R^2$ is the enclosed area.

- **Step 6 — Optical Path Difference (OPD):** Multiplying by $c$ gives the geometric path difference:

$$\Delta L = c \, \Delta t = \frac{4 A \, \Omega}{c}$$

- **Step 7 — Frequency Splitting (Beat Frequency):** In a **Ring Laser Gyroscope (RLG)**, the two beams oscillate as laser modes inside the cavity. The path difference translates into a frequency difference (the **Sagnac frequency**):

$$\boxed{\Delta f = \frac{4 A \, \Omega}{\lambda \, L}}$$

where $L$ is the total perimeter of the ring cavity and $\lambda$ is the laser wavelength.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Because $\Delta f \propto A$, designers maximize the enclosed area $A$ (using triangular or square cavities several meters on a side in aircraft-grade RLGs) to enhance sensitivity.

---

### 2.2 Lock-in Problem and Solutions

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Board Exam Topic:** The "lock-in" phenomenon is a **frequently asked 14-mark question** in KTU ESE.

**Problem Statement:** At very low rotation rates, the two counter-propagating beams in an RLG **couple** through back-scattering from mirrors, causing them to lock at the *same* frequency. No beat signal is produced, and the gyroscope becomes "blind" below a threshold rotation rate called the **lock-in threshold** ($\Omega_L$).

**Solutions (each is a KTU high-yield concept):**

- **Mechanical Dithering:** Oscillate the entire gyro around its input axis at a frequency higher than $\Omega_L$ so that the true signal spends most of its time outside the lock-in band.
- **Optical Dithering (Faraday Effect):** Use a magneto-optic element inside the cavity that introduces a non-reciprocal phase shift oscillating in time.
- **Four-Frequency (Multioscillator) RLG:** Operate two pairs of beams (left/right circular polarizations), each biased by a fixed Faraday bias to sit outside the lock-in zone.
- **Fiber Optic Gyroscope (FOG):** Use a long coiled fiber instead of a gas laser cavity, eliminating back-scatter lock-in entirely.

---

### 2.3 Fiber Optic Gyroscope (FOG) — Theory

In a FOG, a single laser source is split into two beams that travel many turns of a fiber coil (N turns, total length $\ell = 2\pi R N$). The Sagnac phase shift becomes:

$$\Delta \phi_S = \frac{2\pi \, c}{\lambda} \cdot \Delta t = \frac{8 \pi N A \Omega}{\lambda \, c}$$

The interferometric output intensity is:

$$I = I_0 \left[1 + \cos(\Delta \phi_S)\right]$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **FOG Advantage:** No lock-in problem, no moving parts, and extreme sensitivity when $N$ is large (commercial FOGs use $N \approx 1000$ turns over 1 km of fiber).

---

### 2.4 Holography — Theoretical Framework

**Step 1 — Recording (Hologram Formation):**

Light from a coherent laser source is split into two beams:
- **Object Beam** ($O$) — illuminates the object and is scattered toward the recording medium.
- **Reference Beam** ($R$) — travels directly to the recording medium as a clean plane/spherical wave.

The two beams interfere on a high-resolution photographic plate, recording the intensity:

$$I(x,y) = \vert R \vert^2 + \vert O \vert^2 + R^* O + R \, O^*$$

**Step 2 — Recording Medium Response:**

The amplitude transmittance of the developed plate is proportional to the exposure:

$$t(x,y) = t_0 + \beta \left( R^* O + R \, O^* \right)$$

where $t_0$ accounts for the constant $\vert R \vert^2 + \vert O \vert^2$ term.

**Step 3 — Reconstruction:**

When the hologram is illuminated *only* by the reference beam $R$, the transmitted wave becomes:

$$R \cdot t(x,y) = R(t_0 + \beta R^* O) + \beta R^2 O^*$$

- $\beta R^2 O^*$ → **Virtual Image** (3D, original scene).
- $R(t_0 + \beta R^* O)$ → background + **Real Image** (conjugate, pseudoscopic).

---

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Sagnac Time Difference | $\Delta t = \dfrac{4 A \Omega}{c^2}$ | $A$ = area, $\Omega$ = rotation, $c$ = light speed | seconds |
| 2 | Sagnac Path Difference | $\Delta L = \dfrac{4 A \Omega}{c}$ | Optical path difference | meters |
| 3 | Sagnac Beat Frequency (RLG) | $\Delta f = \dfrac{4 A \Omega}{\lambda L}$ | $L$ = cavity perimeter, $\lambda$ = wavelength | Hz |
| 4 | Sagnac Phase Shift (FOG) | $\Delta \phi_S = \dfrac{8 \pi N A \Omega}{\lambda c}$ | $N$ = fiber turns | radians |
| 5 | FOG Output Intensity | $I = I_0[1 + \cos(\Delta \phi_S)]$ | Interference output | W/m² |
| 6 | Scale Factor (RLG) | $K = \dfrac{4 A}{\lambda L}$ | Gyro sensitivity | Hz/(rad/s) |
| 7 | Hologram Intensity | $I = \vert R \vert^2 + \vert O \vert^2 + R^* O + R O^*$ | Recording interference | W/m² |
| 8 | Reconstruction Term | $R \cdot t(x,y) = R t_0 + \beta \vert R \vert^2 O + \beta R^2 O^*$ | Reconstructed wave | — |
| 9 | Fringe Spacing (hologram) | $d = \dfrac{\lambda}{2 \sin(\theta/2)}$ | $\theta$ = angle between $O$ and $R$ | meters |
| 10 | Reconstruction Wavelength Shift | $z' = z \left(1 - \dfrac{\lambda_1}{\lambda_2}\right)$ | Magnification on wavelength change | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Reality Check:** In commercial strapdown inertial navigation systems, the RLG scale factor $K$ is calibrated to better than **1 ppm** (parts per million) for aircraft-grade navigation.

---

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace:** RLGs are core sensors in the inertial navigation systems of Airbus A320, Boeing 747, and most modern airliners — providing attitude reference without GPS.
- **Defense:** FOGs guide torpedoes, missiles, and submarine navigation systems where GPS is unavailable.
- **Automotive:** Low-cost FOGs assist in electronic stability control and autonomous vehicle heading estimation.
- **Holography — Security:** Holographic foils on credit cards, currency notes, and passports prevent counterfeiting.
- **Holography — Data Storage:** Holographic data storage can achieve terabit/cm³ densities by recording multiple pages within the same volume.
- **Holography — Medical:** Holographic Optical Coherence Tomography (HOCT) enables non-invasive 3D imaging of biological tissue.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Sagnac Beat Frequency in RLG

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Valuation Note:** This derivation is a **favourite 14-mark problem** in Module 3. Memorize the four critical steps: Path Difference → Time Difference → Phase Difference → Frequency Difference.

**Starting Point:** A ring laser cavity of perimeter $L$ encloses area $A = \pi R^2$, rotating at angular velocity $\Omega$ about an axis perpendicular to the ring plane. Two counter-propagating laser modes oscillate within the cavity.

**Step 1 — Compute the round-trip path for the co-rotating beam.**

In the rotating frame, the beam traveling clockwise (same direction as $\Omega$) must chase a moving mirror. The effective path length for one round trip is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{cw} &= L + \Delta L \\
\Delta L_{cw} &= \Omega R \cdot t_{cw} = \Omega R \cdot \frac{L_{cw}}{c} \\
L_{cw} &= L + \frac{\Omega R \, L_{cw}}{c} \\
L_{cw}\left(1 - \frac{\Omega R}{c}\right) &= L \\
L_{cw} &= \frac{L}{1 - \dfrac{\Omega R}{c}} \approx L\left(1 + \frac{\Omega R}{c}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

The last step uses the Taylor expansion $\dfrac{1}{1 - x} \approx 1 + x$ for small $x = \dfrac{\Omega R}{c}$.

**Step 2 — Compute the round-trip path for the counter-rotating beam.**

The counter-clockwise beam sees the mirrors approaching, so the effective path is shorter:

$$
L_{ccw} = \frac{L}{1 + \dfrac{\Omega R}{c}} \approx L\left(1 - \frac{\Omega R}{c}\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the path difference.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta L &= L_{cw} - L_{ccw} \\
&= L\left(1 + \frac{\Omega R}{c}\right) - L\left(1 - \frac{\Omega R}{c}\right) \\
&= \frac{2 L \Omega R}{c} = \frac{2 L \Omega R}{c}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $A = \pi R^2 \Rightarrow R = \dfrac{A}{\pi R}$ (kept in terms of area):

$$
\Delta L = \frac{2 L \Omega R}{c} = \frac{4 A \Omega}{c}
$$

(Using $L = 2\pi R$ so that $2 L R = 4 \pi R^2 = 4 A$.)

**Step 4 — Convert path difference to frequency difference.**

For a ring cavity, the lasing condition is that the perimeter must be an integer multiple of the wavelength in the *rotating* frame. A small change in optical path $\Delta L$ produces a frequency shift $\Delta f$ given by:

$$
f = \frac{q \, c}{n L} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \Delta f = -\frac{q c}{n L^2} \Delta L
$$

Since $f = qc/(nL)$ and $\Delta L \ll L$, we can write $q/(nL) = f/c$, giving:

$$
\boxed{\Delta f = \frac{f}{L} \Delta L = \frac{f}{L} \cdot \frac{4 A \Omega}{c} = \frac{4 A \Omega}{\lambda L}}
$$

(Using $f = c/\lambda$.) The minus sign is dropped because we are interested only in the magnitude.

**Step 5 — Sensitivity (Scale Factor) Analysis.**

The scale factor (sensitivity) is defined as the beat frequency per unit angular rate:

$$
K = \frac{\Delta f}{\Omega} = \frac{4 A}{\lambda L}
$$

**Numerical Verification Example:**

> **Problem:** An RLG uses a square cavity with side $a = 0.3$ m, $\lambda = 632.8$ nm (He-Ne), $L = 4a = 1.2$ m. Find the beat frequency at Earth's rotation rate $\Omega_E = 7.27 \times 10^{-5}$ rad/s.
>
> $A = a^2 = 0.09$ m².

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta f &= \frac{4 \times 0.09 \times 7.27 \times 10^{-5}}{632.8 \times 10^{-9} \times 1.2} \\
&= \frac{2.617 \times 10^{-5}}{7.594 \times 10^{-7}} \\
&\approx 34.5 \text{ Hz}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is comfortably above the lock-in threshold of typical RLGs (~100 Hz), confirming the design is workable.

---

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation — Sagnac Phase Shift in FOG

**Setup:** A fiber coil of $N$ turns, each of radius $R$. Light from a single source is split into two beams at a beam splitter; one travels clockwise, the other counter-clockwise through the coil.

**Step 1 — Total fiber length:** $\ell = 2 \pi R N$.

**Step 2 — Time difference per turn:** From Section 2.1, $\Delta t_1 = \dfrac{4 A \Omega}{c^2}$ where $A = \pi R^2$.

**Step 3 — Total time difference over N turns:** Each turn contributes the same delay (the geometry is identical), so:

$$
\Delta t = N \cdot \Delta t_1 = \frac{4 N A \Omega}{c^2} = \frac{4 N \pi R^2 \Omega}{c^2}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to phase difference:** The optical frequency is $f = c/\lambda$, so:

$$
\Delta \phi_S = 2 \pi f \cdot \Delta t = \frac{2 \pi c}{\lambda} \cdot \frac{4 N \pi R^2 \Omega}{c^2} = \frac{8 \pi^2 N R^2 \Omega}{\lambda c}
$$

Using $A = \pi R^2$:

$$
\boxed{\Delta \phi_S = \frac{8 \pi N A \Omega}{\lambda c}}
$$

**Step 5 — Numerical example:**

> **Problem:** A FOG has $N = 1000$ turns, $R = 0.05$ m, $\lambda = 1550$ nm. Find the phase shift at $\Omega = 1^\circ/\text{hr}$.
>
> $\Omega = 1 \cdot \dfrac{\pi}{180 \cdot 3600} \approx 4.85 \times 10^{-6}$ rad/s.
> $A = \pi (0.05)^2 = 7.854 \times 10^{-3}$ m².

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta \phi_S &= \frac{8 \pi \times 1000 \times 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \times 4.85 \times 10^{-6}}{1550 \times 10^{-9} \times 3 \times 10^8} \\
&= \frac{9.56 \times 10^{-4}}{4.65 \times 10^{-1}} \\
&\approx 2.06 \times 10^{-3} \text{ rad}
\end{aligned}
$$

This tiny phase shift (~0.12°) demands extremely sensitive detection — hence the use of closed-loop signal processing in commercial FOGs.

---

### 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation — Hologram Recording and Reconstruction

**Step 1 — Mathematical description of object and reference waves.**

Let the complex amplitudes at the recording plane $z = 0$ be:

$$
O(x, y) = a_O(x, y) e^{i \phi_O(x, y)}, \quad R(x, y) = a_R e^{i \mathbf{k}_R \cdot \mathbf{r}}
$$

where $\mathbf{k}_R$ is the reference wave vector and $\mathbf{r} = (x, y, 0)$.

**Step 2 — Total intensity on the holographic plate.**

$$
I(x, y) = \vert R + O \vert^2 = \vert R \vert^2 + \vert O \vert^2 + R^* O + R O^*
$$

- $\vert R \vert^2$ — constant background (reference alone).
- $\vert O \vert^2$ — "self-interference" of object beam (slowly varying, weak modulation).
- $R^* O$ and $R O^*$ — cross-terms encoding the **phase** of $O$.

**Step 3 — Recording (amplitude transmittance).**

For a film with linear response, the developed plate transmittance is:

$$
t(x, y) = t_0 + \beta \, \tau \, I(x, y)
$$

where $\tau$ is exposure time and $\beta$ is the slope of the $t$–$E$ curve. Keeping only the phase-carrying terms:

$$
t(x, y) = t_0 + \beta (R^* O + R O^*)
$$

**Step 4 — Reconstruction using the same reference beam.**

When the developed plate is illuminated by $R$ alone, the transmitted field is:

$$
U_{out} = R \cdot t(x, y) = t_0 R + \beta R R^* O + \beta R^2 O^*
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation of the three terms.**

- $t_0 R$ — **Direct (zeroth-order) beam**, attenuated reference wave.
- $\beta \vert R \vert^2 O$ — **Virtual image** (true 3D replica of the object, located behind the plate). The factor $\vert R \vert^2$ is constant, so the wavefront of $O$ is preserved exactly.
- $\beta R^2 O^*$ — **Real (conjugate) image** (pseudoscopic — depth inverted), located in front of the plate.

This is the **Gabor–Leith–Upatnieks** formulation of off-axis holography, which separated the three terms spatially by introducing an angle $\theta$ between $O$ and $R$.

**Step 6 — Fringe spacing derivation.**

The interference of two plane waves at angle $\theta$ produces fringes with spacing:

$$
d = \frac{\lambda}{2 \sin(\theta/2)}
$$

This is why the recording medium must resolve features finer than $d$ — typically sub-micron resolution photographic emulsions are required.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for Boards:** The crucial difference between a hologram and a photograph is that the photographic plate records $I$ *and* the relative phase of $O$ is encoded in the *spatial position* of interference fringes. Phase information is therefore converted to intensity information, which *can* be recorded.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Gyroscope Technologies

| Parameter | Ring Laser Gyro (RLG) | Fiber Optic Gyro (FOG) | MEMS Gyro |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Sagnac Implementation** | Gas laser cavity | Optical fiber coil | Vibrating mechanical structure |
| **Lock-in Problem?** | Yes (requires dithering) | No (inherently no lock-in) | N/A |
| **Sensitivity** | Very High (0.001°/hr) | High (0.01°/hr) | Moderate (10°/hr) |
| **Size** | Large (kg) | Medium (cm) | Small (mm) |
| **Cost** | Very High | Moderate | Low |
| **Application** | Aircraft navigation | Submarine, missiles | Smartphones, automotive |
| **Bias Stability** | ~0.001°/hr | ~0.01°/hr | ~10°/hr |
| **Power Consumption** | High (gas discharge) | Moderate | Very Low |
| **KTU Exam Focus** | ⭐⭐⭐ (Derivation + lock-in) | ⭐⭐ (Phase shift formula) | Not in syllabus |

### 4.1 Ring Laser Gyroscope — Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[He-Ne Laser Plasma Tube] --> B[Beam Splitter Mirror M1]
    B --> C[Mirror M2 - Corner]
    C --> D[Mirror M3 - Corner]
    D --> E[Mirror M4 - Return to M1]
    E --> F[Partially Transmitting Mirror M5]
    F --> G[Photodetector PD1 - Beat Signal]
    F --> H[Photodetector PD2 - Reference]
    I[Piezoelectric Dither Motor] -.-> C
    J[High Voltage Power Supply] --> A
    K[Signal Processing Electronics] --> G
    K --> L[Digital Readout: Angular Rate deg_per_hr]
    M[FAR] -.->|controls| J

    classDef laser fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000
    classDef mirror fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,color:#000
```

**Block Description:**
- **He-Ne Tube:** Provides gain medium for two counter-propagating laser modes.
- **Ring Cavity (M1–M4):** Defines the closed optical path enclosing area $A$.

- **Photodetector PD1:** Mixes the two extracted beams; the output contains the beat frequency $\Delta f$.

### 4.2 Sagnac Effect — Counter-Propagating Beams Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Rotating_Frame[ROTATING RING OF RADIUS R]
        direction TB
        P1[Mirror at Position 1 - t=0]
        P2[Mirror at Position 2 - after t_cw]
        P3[Mirror at Position 3 - after t_ccw]

        BeamCW[Clockwise Beam - LONGER PATH] -->|travels| P1
        P1 -->|arrives late| P2
        BeamCCW[Counter-Clockwise Beam - SHORTER PATH] -->|travels| P1
        P1 -->|arrives early| P3
    end

    Rot[Rotation Omega - CCW] -.->|drives| Rotating_Frame

    P2 --> D1[Detector: Receives CW Later]
    P3 --> D2[Detector: Receives CCW Earlier]
    D1 --> Mix[Beat Frequency Mixer]
    D2 --> Mix
    Mix --> Out[Output: Delta_f proportional to Omega]

    classDef beam fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,color:#000
    classDef detector fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400,color:#000
    classDef rotation fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000

    class BeamCW,BeamCCW beam
    class D1,D2,Mix,Out detector
    class Rot rotation
```

**Block Description:**
- The rotating platform moves the mirrors between the times the two beams complete the loop.
- The clockwise beam "chases" the moving mirror → longer optical path.
- The counter-clockwise beam "meets" the moving mirror → shorter optical path.
- The path difference $\Delta L = \dfrac{4A\Omega}{c}$ produces the measurable beat frequency.


**Block Description:**
- **Recording Stage:** Object + Reference beams interfere on the holographic plate, encoding phase as fringe pattern.
- **Plate Development:** The film records the fringe intensity; phase information is now stored as spatial variation.
- **Reconstruction Stage:** Illuminating the developed plate with the original reference beam recreates the object wavefront exactly.
- **Output:** Observer sees a 3D virtual image identical to the original scene (full parallax, depth perception).

### 4.4 Fiber Optic Gyroscope — Architecture Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    SLD[Superluminescent Diode SLD Source 1550 nm] --> CPL1[Coupler 1 - 3 dB]
    CPL1 -->|clockwise beam| POL[Polarizer]
    CPL1 -->|counter-clockwise beam| MOD[Phase Modulator - Piezoelectric]

    POL --> CPL2[Coupler 2]
    MOD --> CPL2

    CPL2 -->|clockwise| COIL[Fiber Sensing Coil - N turns, length L]
    CPL2 -->|counter-clockwise| COIL

    COIL --> CPL2
    CPL2 --> DET[Photodetector PIN]
    DET --> AMP[Transimpedance Amplifier]
    AMP --> DSP[Digital Signal Processor]
    DSP -->|closed-loop feedback| MOD
    DSP --> OUT[Output: Angular Velocity deg_per_hr]

    classDef source fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000
    classDef coupler fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,color:#000
    classDef modulator fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#8B008B,color:#000
    classDef coil fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#808000,color:#000
    classDef detector fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000
    classDef electronics fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000

    class SLD source
    class CPL1,CPL2 coupler
    class MOD,POL modulator
    class COIL coil
    class DET,AMP,DSP,OUT electronics
```

**Block Description:**
- **SLD Source:** Broad-band source (low coherence) minimizes back-scatter and polarization noise in FOGs.
- **Couplers:** Split and recombine the two counter-propagating beams.
- **Sensing Coil:** The Sagnac phase shift accumulates with $N$ turns.
- **Phase Modulator:** Imposes a non-reciprocal phase bias for closed-loop (serrodyne) operation, achieving digital phase tracking.
- **Photodetector:** Converts the interference intensity into an electrical signal carrying the rotation information.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Define the Sagnac Effect. Write the expression for the Sagnac frequency shift in a Ring Laser Gyroscope and identify the factors on which it depends.

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Sagnac Effect:** It is the phenomenon in which a rotating closed optical path produces a difference in the travel times (or optical path lengths) of two counter-propagating light beams, the magnitude of which is proportional to the angular velocity of the rotation.
>
> The Sagnac frequency shift in an RLG is:
>
> $$\Delta f = \frac{4 A \Omega}{\lambda L}$$
>
> where $A$ = enclosed area, $\Omega$ = angular velocity, $\lambda$ = laser wavelength, $L$ = cavity perimeter.
>
> It depends on: **(i)** Enclosed area $A$, **(ii)** Angular velocity $\Omega$, **(iii)** Laser wavelength $\lambda$, **(iv)** Cavity perimeter $L$.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
What is a hologram? How is it different from a photographic image?

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> A **hologram** is a recording of the interference pattern between an object beam and a reference beam, containing both **amplitude and phase** information of the light wave reflected from the object. When reconstructed, it produces a three-dimensional image with full parallax.
>
> | Aspect | Photograph | Hologram |
> |---|---|---|
> | Records | Intensity only | Amplitude + Phase |
> | Image Type | 2D flat | 3D with depth |
> | Coherent light needed? | No | Yes (laser) |
> | Requires lens? | Yes | No (lensless) |
> | Storage medium | Standard film | High-resolution plate |
>
> Phase information is encoded in the spatial fringe pattern of a hologram, whereas a photograph discards phase entirely.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Rule:** Answer **ANY ONE** of the two sub-choices (a) or (b) in Part B. Each carries 14 marks, split as (a) = 7 marks, (b) = 7 marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**A (a) [7 Marks]** Derive the expression for the Sagnac frequency shift in a Ring Laser Gyroscope. Explain how the sensitivity of the gyroscope is affected by the cavity area and wavelength.

**A (b) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working of a Fiber Optic Gyroscope. What are its advantages over a Ring Laser Gyroscope?

---

#### Model Solution

**A (a) — Derivation of Sagnac Frequency Shift (7 Marks):**

Refer to the complete derivation in **Section 3.1** of these notes. Key steps expected by the examiner:

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Set up the rotating ring of radius $R$ with perimeter $L = 2\pi R$. Write expressions for the round-trip optical path of the clockwise and counter-clockwise beams:

$$
L_{cw} \approx L\left(1 + \frac{\Omega R}{c}\right), \quad L_{ccw} \approx L\left(1 - \frac{\Omega R}{c}\right)
$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Compute the path difference $\Delta L = \dfrac{4A\Omega}{c}$ and convert to time difference $\Delta t = \dfrac{4A\Omega}{c^2}$.

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Convert time difference to frequency shift using $\Delta f = \dfrac{\Delta L}{\lambda L}$:

$$
\boxed{\Delta f = \frac{4A\Omega}{\lambda L}}
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Discuss sensitivity:
- Sensitivity $K = \dfrac{4A}{\lambda L}$ is **directly proportional to area $A$** → larger area gives higher beat frequency for the same rotation.
- Sensitivity is **inversely proportional to wavelength** → shorter wavelength lasers (e.g., blue-green) give higher sensitivity.
- **Trade-off:** Larger cavities mean bulkier, heavier gyroscopes.

---

**A (b) — Fiber Optic Gyroscope (7 Marks):**

**Construction (3 marks):** [Refer to Section 4.4 Mermaid Diagram]

A FOG consists of:
1. **Broadband optical source** (SLD or EDFA) — wavelength typically 1550 nm.
2. **Coupler 1** — splits the source light into two beams.
3. **Polarizer** — ensures single polarization state.
4. **Integrated phase modulator** — imposes non-reciprocal phase shift.
5. **Coupler 2** — recombines the two beams after they traverse the coil.
6. **Fiber sensing coil** — $N$ turns of single-mode fiber, length 100 m to 5 km.
7. **Photodetector** — measures interference intensity.
8. **Signal processing electronics** — closed-loop feedback for high dynamic range.

**Working (3 marks):** Light is split into two beams that traverse the fiber coil in opposite directions. The Sagnac effect causes a non-reciprocal phase shift $\Delta \phi_S = \dfrac{8\pi N A \Omega}{\lambda c}$. On recombination, the interference intensity $I = I_0[1 + \cos(\Delta \phi_S)]$ carries the rotation information. The output is processed to extract $\Omega$.

**Advantages over RLG (1 mark):**
- No lock-in problem (no gain medium to couple the two beams).
- No moving parts → lower maintenance, higher reliability.
- Lower cost and smaller size.
- Can be made with all-fiber construction → immune to environmental vibration.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**B (a) [7 Marks]** Explain the principle of holography. With the help of a block diagram, describe the recording and reconstruction process of a hologram. Derive the expression for the reconstructed image.

**B (b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the different types of holograms. Mention any four applications of holography in modern engineering.

---

#### Model Solution

**B (a) — Principle, Recording & Reconstruction of Holography (7 Marks):**

**Principle (2 marks):** Holography is a two-step lensless imaging technique that records both the amplitude and phase of light from a coherently illuminated object by interfering it with a reference beam on a high-resolution photographic plate. Upon reconstruction with the same reference beam, the original 3D wavefront is reproduced.

**Recording Process (2 marks):** [Refer to Section 4.3 Mermaid Diagram]
- Coherent laser light is split into two beams: object beam and reference beam.
- Object beam illuminates the object; scattered light reaches the holographic plate.
- Reference beam reaches the plate directly via mirrors.
- The two beams interfere on the plate; intensity $I = \vert R \vert^2 + \vert O \vert^2 + R^* O + R O^*$ is recorded.
- The plate is developed, fixing the fringe pattern as amplitude transmittance $t(x,y)$.

**Reconstruction Process (2 marks):**
- The developed plate is illuminated with the original reference beam $R$.
- Transmitted wave: $U_{out} = R t_0 + \beta \vert R \vert^2 O + \beta R^2 O^*$.
- The term $\beta \vert R \vert^2 O$ produces the **virtual image** (3D, original scene).
- The term $\beta R^2 O^*$ produces the **real image** (pseudoscopic, depth-inverted).
- The term $R t_0$ is the undiffracted background.

**Derivation (1 mark):** As above in Section 3.3.

---

**B (b) — Types of Holograms and Applications (7 Marks):**

**Types of Holograms (3.5 marks):**

1. **Transmission Hologram** — Illuminated by laser light from behind; viewer sees virtual image on the opposite side.
2. **Reflection Hologram (Denisyuk)** — Recorded with object and reference beams incident from opposite sides; can be viewed with white light.
3. **Phase Hologram** — Recorded as a phase-only variation (e.g., bleached emulsion or photopolymer); high diffraction efficiency.
4. **Amplitude Hologram** — Recorded as a variation in optical density (absorptive).
5. **Volume Hologram** — Recorded throughout the thickness of the medium; wavelength-selective (Bragg selectivity).
6. **Computer-Generated Hologram (CGH)** — Synthesized numerically; no real object needed.

**Applications (3.5 marks):**
1. **Anti-counterfeiting Security** — Holographic foils on credit cards, currency, passports.
2. **Holographic Data Storage** — Volumetric storage at terabit/cm³ density in photorefractive crystals or photopolymers.
3. **Holographic Optical Coherence Tomography (HOCT)** — Non-invasive 3D imaging of biological tissue (retina, skin).
4. **Holographic Interferometry** — Vibration analysis, stress/strain measurement, non-destructive testing of engineering components.
5. **3D Display and Visualization** — Holographic screens, head-up displays (HUDs) in fighter aircraft.
6. **Optical Elements** — Holographic optical elements (HOEs) used as lenses, gratings, and beam splitters.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark Losers:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Sagnac *time* difference with *phase* difference:** Marks are lost if students write $\Delta t$ when $\Delta \phi_S$ is asked. The phase shift is $2\pi f \Delta t$.
> 2. **Forgetting the area in the FOG formula:** Students often write $\Delta \phi_S = \dfrac{4A\Omega}{\lambda c}$ for FOG, missing the factor of $2\pi N$. The correct expression has $8\pi N A \Omega / (\lambda c)$.
> 3. **Mixing virtual and real image terms:** The virtual image corresponds to the term with $O$ (not $O^*$); the real image corresponds to $O^*$. Reversing these is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction.
> 4. **Ignoring the lock-in discussion for RLG:** A 7-mark question on RLG must include the lock-in problem. Skipping it loses at least 2 marks.
> 5. **Not specifying the coherence requirement in holography:** Always state that holography requires a **temporally and spatially coherent source** (laser) — this is a board favourite sub-question.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Module 3: Laser Gyroscope & Holography)**

**🔹 Laser Gyroscope — Key Points**

- Sagnac Effect: rotation induces a path/time difference between counter-propagating beams in a closed loop.
- **RLG Beat Frequency:** $\Delta f = \dfrac{4A\Omega}{\lambda L}$ — directly measurable, extremely precise.
- **FOG Phase Shift:** $\Delta \phi_S = \dfrac{8\pi N A \Omega}{\lambda c}$ — proportional to number of fiber turns.
- **Lock-in Problem:** RLG fails at low rotation rates due to back-scatter coupling. Solutions: mechanical dither, optical dither (Faraday), four-frequency RLG, FOG.
- **Scale Factor:** $K = \dfrac{4A}{\lambda L}$ Hz/(rad/s) — engineering metric of sensitivity.
- **Applications:** Aircraft navigation (Airbus, Boeing), submarine INS, missile guidance, spacecraft attitude control.

**🔹 Holography — Key Points**

- Holography records **both amplitude and phase** (photography records only amplitude).
- Requires **coherent light** (laser: He-Ne 632.8 nm typical).
- Two-step process: **Recording** (object + reference beam interference) → **Reconstruction** (reference beam alone).
- Recorded intensity: $I = \vert R \vert^2 + \vert O \vert^2 + R^* O + R O^*$.
- Reconstructed field: $U_{out} = R t_0 + \beta \vert R \vert^2 O + \beta R^2 O^*$.
- **Virtual image** (true 3D) and **real image** (pseudoscopic, depth-inverted).
- **Types:** Transmission, Reflection (Denisyuk), Phase, Amplitude, Volume, CGH.
- **Applications:** Security holograms (anti-counterfeiting), data storage, HOCT medical imaging, interferometry (NDT), 3D displays, HOEs.

**🔹 Cross-Topic Comparison**

| Property | Laser Gyroscope | Holography |
|---|---|---|
| Coherence required? | Yes (very high) | Yes (very high) |
| Interference principle? | Yes (counter-propagating beams) | Yes (object + reference) |
| Phase measurement? | Yes (Sagnac phase/freq) | Yes (encoded in fringes) |
| Detected signal? | Beat frequency $\Delta f$ | Reconstructed intensity $I$ |
| Output unit | Hz (or rad/s) | Optical intensity → visual image |

**🔹 Frequently Asked Derivations (Memorize These)**

1. Sagnac time difference: $\Delta t = \dfrac{4A\Omega}{c^2}$ → 2 marks
2. Sagnac frequency shift in RLG: $\Delta f = \dfrac{4A\Omega}{\lambda L}$ → 5 marks
3. Sagnac phase shift in FOG: $\Delta \phi_S = \dfrac{8\pi N A \Omega}{\lambda c}$ → 3 marks
4. Hologram intensity: $I = \vert R \vert^2 + \vert O \vert^2 + R^* O + R O^*$ → 3 marks
5. Reconstructed wavefront: $U_{out} = R t_0 + \beta \vert R \vert^2 O + \beta R^2 O^*$ → 4 marks
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pollution Monitoring, Industry and Biomedical Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT861 - OPTOELECTRONICS AND INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Pollution Monitoring, Industry and Biomedical Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Pollution Monitoring using Optoelectronic Systems

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):**
Optical pollution monitoring refers to the use of light-matter interaction principles — absorption, scattering, fluorescence, and Raman scattering — combined with optoelectronic transducers (photodiodes, PMTs, photomultipliers, spectrometers) to qualitatively and quantitatively detect gaseous, liquid, and particulate pollutants in the environment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The two most examination-relevant techniques are **LIDAR (Light Detection And Ranging)** for atmospheric/aerosol profiling and **Differential Optical Absorption Spectroscopy (DOAS)** for trace-gas detection.

**Intuitive Analogy:**
Think of light as a "messenger" carrying information. When light passes through polluted air or dirty water, pollutants "scribble notes" on the light by absorbing specific colors (wavelengths). A spectrometer acts like a "postman" who reads these notes and tells us *which* pollutant was present and *how much*. The more pollutant, the darker the "scribble" at that specific color.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Metric to Remember:** The sensitivity of optical pollution sensors is governed by the **Beer–Lambert Law**, expressed as $I = I_0 e^{-\alpha C L}$, where $\alpha$ is the absorption coefficient, $C$ is concentration, and $L$ is the optical path length. The minimum detectable concentration scales inversely with path length $L$.

---

### 1.2 Industrial Applications of Optoelectronics

**Formal Definition:**
Industrial optoelectronic applications encompass non-contact measurement, process control, quality inspection, and material characterization systems that exploit the speed, precision, and immunity to electromagnetic interference of optical techniques. Key examples include optical pyrometry, laser Doppler velocimetry, machine vision, and laser-based material processing.

**Intuitive Analogy:**
Imagine trying to measure the temperature of a white-hot piece of steel inside a furnace with a regular thermometer — impossible! A **pyrometer** "sees" the glow and calculates the temperature remotely, exactly like judging how hot a stove is by looking at the color of its coils. Similarly, **barcode scanners** at supermarkets act as "optical translators," converting black-and-white stripes into numbers using reflected laser light.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Reference:** Industrial blackbody pyrometers rely on **Wien's Displacement Law** — $\lambda_{max} T = 2.898 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m·K}$ — a KTU-favorite formula for high-temperature non-contact measurement problems.

---

### 1.3 Biomedical Applications of Optoelectronics

**Formal Definition:**
Biomedical optoelectronics (also called *biophotonics*) applies light-based devices for non-invasive or minimally invasive diagnosis, monitoring, imaging, and therapy. The dominant clinical technologies are **Pulse Oximetry** (SpO₂ measurement), **Endoscopy**, **Optical Coherence Tomography (OCT)**, **Laser Surgery**, and **Fluorescence Imaging**.

**Intuitive Analogy:**
Consider how your earlobe looks red when light shines through it. That redness is because oxygenated blood absorbs green light differently than deoxygenated blood. A **pulse oximeter** simply clips onto your finger and "listens" to this color change with every heartbeat, computing oxygen saturation in real time. An **endoscope** is like a "periscope for doctors" — a fiber-optic cable that carries light into the body and brings back images from places the eye cannot see.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU-Favorite Biomedical Topic:** Pulse Oximetry, governed by the **Ratio-of-Ratios (R) equation**, is the single most asked biomedical question in KTU ESE papers. The SpO₂ value is empirically derived from:
> $$R = \frac{AC_{660}/DC_{660}}{AC_{940}/DC_{940}}$$
> where $AC$ is the pulsatile (AC) component and $DC$ is the steady (DC) component of light transmission.

### 1.4 Summary Snapshot

| Domain | Primary Optical Principle | Canonical Instrument | KTU Exam Weight |
|---|---|---|---|
| Pollution Monitoring | Absorption / Scattering | LIDAR, DOAS, FTIR Spectrometer | High (Part A & B) |
| Industry | Blackbody radiation, Doppler shift | Pyrometer, LDV, Barcode reader | High (Part B) |
| Biomedical | Differential absorption, Low-coherence interferometry | Pulse Oximeter, OCT, Endoscope | Very High (Part A & B) |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Pollution Monitoring — Operational Theory

#### 2.1.1 Differential Optical Absorption Spectroscopy (DOAS)
- **Principle:** Pollutant gases (NO₂, SO₂, O₃, benzene) exhibit narrow, wavelength-specific absorption lines superimposed on a broad background.
- **Operation:**
  1. A broadband light source (Xe arc lamp) sends a collimated beam through an open air column of length $L$ (often hundreds of meters using retro-reflectors).
  2. The transmitted spectrum is dispersed by a grating and recorded on a photodiode array.
  3. The reference spectrum (clean air) is subtracted from the measured spectrum.
  4. The residual narrow-band absorption $\Delta \alpha(\lambda)$ is fit against laboratory cross-sections to identify and quantify pollutants.
- **Why it works:** Atmospheric turbulence and broadband extinction (Rayleigh + Mie scattering) affect all wavelengths almost equally, so subtracting the reference removes them. Only the *narrow* pollutant signature remains.
- **Engineering utility:** Continuous monitoring of urban air quality, smokestack emissions, and volcanic SO₂ plumes.

#### 2.1.2 LIDAR (Light Detection And Ranging)
- **Principle:** A short laser pulse ($\sim 5$–$10$ ns) is transmitted into the atmosphere; backscattered light from aerosols, molecules, or hard targets is collected by a telescope and time-resolved.
- **Range-resolved operation:** The distance $R$ is given by $R = ct/2$, where $t$ is the round-trip time and $c$ is the speed of light.
- **Why it works:** Each altitude "slice" corresponds to a different return time, giving a vertical profile of pollutants (aerosols, NO₂, SO₂).
- **Elastic LIDAR (Mie/Rayleigh):** Probes particulates and clouds.
- **Raman LIDAR:** Identifies specific molecules by their Raman-shifted return.
- **Differential Absorption LIDAR (DIAL):** Two closely spaced wavelengths (one absorbed, one not) — difference yields gas concentration.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Reality:** LIDAR can detect pollutant concentrations as low as **parts-per-billion (ppb)** over path lengths of several kilometers, which is why it is the gold standard for atmospheric monitoring.

#### 2.1.3 Fiber-Optic Chemical Sensors for Water
- **Principle:** An indicator dye (e.g., ruthenium complex for O₂, phenol red for pH) is immobilized at the fiber tip. Its optical property (fluorescence intensity or lifetime) changes in response to the analyte.
- **Operation:** Light at excitation wavelength $\lambda_{ex}$ is sent down the fiber; the returned fluorescence at $\lambda_{em}$ is measured.
- **Why useful:** No electrical components at the sensing point — safe in explosive/aqueous environments.

---

### 2.2 Industrial Applications — Operational Theory

#### 2.2.1 Optical Pyrometry (Radiation Thermometry)
- **Principle:** Any object above absolute zero emits thermal radiation, characterized by spectral radiance $L_\lambda(\lambda, T)$.
- **Two-wavelength (ratio) pyrometer:**
  1. Light from a hot target passes through a chopper and is split into two narrow spectral bands $\lambda_1$ and $\lambda_2$.
  2. Photodetectors measure the radiance ratio $L_1/L_2$.
  3. The ratio depends only on $T$ (not on emissivity), giving an emissivity-independent reading.
- **Why it works:** Planck's law cancels out the emissivity term when a ratio is taken.
- **Engineering utility:** Steel furnaces, molten glass, semiconductor wafer temperature (CVD reactors).

#### 2.2.2 Laser Doppler Velocimetry (LDV)
- **Principle:** When a moving particle scatters light from two intersecting laser beams, the scattered light contains a Doppler frequency $f_D$ that is linearly proportional to the particle velocity.
- **Operation:**
  1. Two beams of equal wavelength $\lambda$ intersect at a probe volume.
  2. Their interference creates straight, parallel fringes of spacing $d = \lambda / (2 \sin(\theta/2))$.
  3. As a particle crosses the fringes, the scattered signal is modulated at frequency $f_D = v/d$.
  4. Velocity is computed as $v = f_D \cdot d$.
- **Why it works:** Non-intrusive, extremely high spatial resolution (~µm), and no calibration drift.

#### 2.2.3 Machine Vision and Barcode Scanning
- **Barcode principle:** A laser sweeps across alternating black (absorbing) and white (reflecting) bars; the photodetector records a binary waveform of pulse widths.
- **Machine vision:** A 2D CMOS/CCD array captures an image; algorithms extract features (size, shape, color) for automated quality control.

---

### 2.3 Biomedical Applications — Operational Theory

#### 2.3.1 Pulse Oximetry — SpO₂ Measurement
- **Principle:** Oxygenated hemoglobin (HbO₂) and deoxygenated hemoglobin (Hb) have very different absorption spectra in the red and near-infrared regions.
- **Operation:**
  1. Two LEDs (660 nm red, 940 nm IR) illuminate a finger or earlobe in time-multiplexed fashion.
  2. A photodiode on the opposite side records transmitted intensity.
  3. The signal is decomposed into a steady (DC) baseline (tissue, bone, venous blood) and a pulsatile (AC) component (arterial blood).
  4. The **Ratio of Ratios** $R$ is computed (equation below) and mapped to SpO₂ via an empirically calibrated lookup table.
- **Why two wavelengths?** Single wavelength cannot distinguish absorption changes due to oxygenation from those due to blood volume changes.
- **Why pulsatile?** Only arterial blood changes volume with each heartbeat, isolating it from other tissues.

#### 2.3.2 Endoscopy
- **Principle:** A bundle of coherent optical fibers (typically 10,000+ fibers, each ~5–10 µm diameter) transmits an image from inside the body to an external camera.
- **Modern variant:** CCD/CMOS chip-on-tip video endoscopes with cold LED illumination via fiber light guide.
- **Why useful:** Reaches hollow organs (GI tract, bronchi, bladder) without surgery.

#### 2.3.3 Optical Coherence Tomography (OCT)
- **Principle:** Low-coherence interferometry — analogous to ultrasound but with light.
- **Operation:**
  1. A broadband low-coherence source (e.g., superluminescent diode, $\Delta\lambda \sim 50$ nm) is split into a sample arm (tissue) and a reference arm (mirror).
  2. Interference occurs only when the two arm lengths match within the **coherence length** $L_c = \lambda^2 / \Delta\lambda$ (typically 5–15 µm).
  3. Scanning the reference mirror yields a depth-resolved A-scan; lateral scanning produces a 2D cross-sectional B-scan.
- **Why it works:** Optical gating by coherence replaces mechanical gating, achieving µm-scale axial resolution.
- **Engineering utility:** Ophthalmology (retinal imaging), cardiology (intravascular OCT), dermatology.

#### 2.3.4 Laser Surgery and Photodynamic Therapy (PDT)
- **Laser surgery:** High-power focused laser vaporizes tissue. CO₂ laser (10.6 µm) cuts by water absorption; Nd:YAG (1064 nm) coagulates by deep penetration.
- **PDT:** A photosensitizer (e.g., Photofrin) is injected; it accumulates in tumor cells. Illumination with red light (630 nm) generates singlet oxygen ($^1$O₂), killing the cells selectively.

---

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This table is exam-critical. Memorize the equation, its variables, and the SI unit of each.

| # | Equation Name | Mathematical Form | Variable Definitions | Typical Unit | Domain |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Beer–Lambert Law | $I = I_0 e^{-\alpha C L}$ | $I_0$: incident intensity; $I$: transmitted; $\alpha$: absorption coefficient; $C$: concentration; $L$: path length | W/m², m⁻¹, ppm, m | Pollution / Biomed |
| 2 | Absorbance | $A = -\log_{10}(I/I_0) = \varepsilon c L$ | $\varepsilon$: molar absorptivity; $c$: molar concentration | L·mol⁻¹·cm⁻¹, mol/L, cm | Pollution / Biomed |
| 3 | LIDAR Range | $R = c t / 2$ | $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s; $t$: round-trip time | m, s | Pollution |
| 4 | LIDAR Equation | $P_R = P_T \cdot \frac{c \tau}{2} \cdot \frac{A}{R^2} \cdot \eta \cdot \beta(R)$ | $P_R$: received power; $P_T$: transmitted; $\tau$: pulse width; $A$: telescope area; $\eta$: system efficiency; $\beta$: backscatter coefficient | W | Pollution |
| 5 | DIAL Concentration | $N(R) = \frac{1}{2 \Delta\sigma} \cdot \frac{d}{dR}\ln\!\left[\frac{P_{on}(R)}{P_{off}(R)}\right]$ | $\Delta\sigma$: differential absorption cross-section; $P_{on/off}$: return powers at on/off wavelengths | m⁻³, m² | Pollution |
| 6 | Wien's Displacement | $\lambda_{max} T = 2.898 \times 10^{-3}$ | $\lambda_{max}$: peak wavelength; $T$: absolute temperature | m, K | Industry |
| 7 | Stefan–Boltzmann | $E = \sigma T^4$ | $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ W·m⁻²·K⁻⁴ | W/m², K | Industry |
| 8 | Two-λ Pyrometer Ratio | $R_T = \left(\frac{\lambda_2}{\lambda_1}\right)^5 \exp\!\left[\frac{c_2}{T}\left(\frac{1}{\lambda_2} - \frac{1}{\lambda_1}\right)\right]$ | $c_2 = 1.4388 \times 10^{-2}$ m·K | dimensionless | Industry |
| 9 | LDV Velocity | $v = f_D \cdot d = f_D \cdot \frac{\lambda}{2 \sin(\theta/2)}$ | $f_D$: Doppler frequency; $\theta$: beam crossing angle | m/s, Hz, m, rad | Industry |
| 10 | Doppler Shift | $\Delta f = \frac{2 v \cos\phi}{\lambda}$ | $\phi$: angle between velocity vector and beam | Hz, m/s, m | Industry / Biomed |
| 11 | Pulse Oximetry Ratio | $R = \frac{AC_{660}/DC_{660}}{AC_{940}/DC_{940}}$ | $AC$: pulsatile; $DC$: steady component | dimensionless | Biomed |
| 12 | Coherence Length | $L_c = \frac{\lambda^2}{\Delta \lambda}$ | $\lambda$: central wavelength; $\Delta\lambda$: spectral FWHM | m, nm | Biomed (OCT) |
| 13 | OCT Axial Resolution | $\delta z = \frac{2 \ln 2}{\pi} \cdot \frac{\lambda^2}{\Delta\lambda}$ | FWHM axial resolution | m | Biomed (OCT) |
| 14 | Laser Power Density | $I = P / A$ | $P$: laser power; $A$: spot area | W/m² | Biomed / Industry |
| 15 | Fluorescence Intensity | $I_f = I_0 \cdot \phi \cdot \varepsilon(\lambda_{ex}) c L$ | $\phi$: quantum yield | W/m² | Biomed / Pollution |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** In numerical problems, KTU examiners frequently test the Beer–Lambert Law, Wien's Displacement, and the Pulse Oximetry Ratio. Practice substituting units carefully.

---

### 2.5 Engineering Real-World Utility

- **Smart City Air Quality Networks:** DOAS systems monitor NO₂, SO₂, and ozone over kilometers continuously, feeding data to pollution control boards.
- **Steel Industry:** Two-color pyrometers track molten-steel temperature ($>1600$ °C) without contact, improving throughput and product quality.
- **Aviation Safety:** LIDAR systems detect clear-air turbulence and volcanic ash, saving lives and aircraft.
- **COVID-19 Era:** Pulse oximeters became household devices, demonstrating how optoelectronics translates into public health at scale.
- **Ophthalmology:** OCT is now the gold standard for diagnosing macular degeneration and glaucoma — over 30 million procedures per year worldwide.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Pulse Oximetry SpO₂ from Beer–Lambert Law

**Problem Setup:**
Light of two wavelengths ($\lambda_1 = 660$ nm, $\lambda_2 = 940$ nm) passes through a finger of thickness $L$. The transmitted intensities have a DC (steady) component from non-pulsatile tissues and an AC (pulsatile) component from arterial blood volume change $\Delta L$.

**Step 1 — Apply Beer–Lambert Law at the two wavelengths.**

The total absorbance at wavelength $\lambda$ is:

$$A(\lambda) = \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{I_0}{I(\lambda)}\right) = \varepsilon_{HbO_2}(\lambda) \, c_{HbO_2} \, L + \varepsilon_{Hb}(\lambda) \, c_{Hb} \, L$$

**Step 2 — Decompose the path length.**
Let the total path be $L = L_{DC} + \Delta L$, where $L_{DC}$ is the steady path and $\Delta L$ is the small pulsatile change. Since $\Delta L \ll L_{DC}$, the AC absorbance is the differential:

$$\Delta A(\lambda) = \left[\varepsilon_{HbO_2}(\lambda) \, c_{HbO_2} + \varepsilon_{Hb}(\lambda) \, c_{Hb}\right] \cdot \Delta L$$

**Step 3 — Define the DC and AC intensities.**
For small absorption changes, the AC amplitude normalized by the DC gives:

$$\frac{\Delta I(\lambda)}{I_{DC}(\lambda)} \approx \Delta A(\lambda) \cdot \ln(10)$$

**Step 4 — Form the ratio at $\lambda_1$.**

$$\frac{AC_{660}}{DC_{660}} = K_1 \left[\varepsilon_{HbO_2}(660)\, c_{HbO_2} + \varepsilon_{Hb}(660)\, c_{Hb}\right] \Delta L$$

**Step 5 — Form the ratio at $\lambda_2 = 940$ nm similarly.**

$$\frac{AC_{940}}{DC_{940}} = K_2 \left[\varepsilon_{HbO_2}(940)\, c_{HbO_2} + \varepsilon_{Hb}(940)\, c_{Hb}\right] \Delta L$$

> [!NOTE]
> The constants $K_1$ and $K_2$ include the geometric and electronic path factors; they cancel in the *ratio of ratios* below.

**Step 6 — Form the Ratio-of-Ratios $R$.**

$$R = \frac{AC_{660}/DC_{660}}{AC_{940}/DC_{940}} = \frac{\varepsilon_{HbO_2}(660)\, c_{HbO_2} + \varepsilon_{Hb}(660)\, c_{Hb}}{\varepsilon_{HbO_2}(940)\, c_{HbO_2} + \varepsilon_{Hb}(940)\, c_{Hb}}$$

**Step 7 — Solve for fractional oxygen saturation.**
Define $S = c_{HbO_2}/(c_{HbO_2} + c_{Hb})$. Substitute and rearrange:

$$R = \frac{S \cdot \varepsilon_{HbO_2}(660) + (1 - S) \cdot \varepsilon_{Hb}(660)}{S \cdot \varepsilon_{HbO_2}(940) + (1 - S) \cdot \varepsilon_{Hb}(940)}$$

**Step 8 — Final linear approximation.**
Using tabulated extinction coefficients, the equation simplifies to the empirical linear fit used in clinical devices:

$$SpO_2 \approx 110 - 25 \cdot R \quad (\text{valid for } R \in [0.4, 1.0])$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result:** SpO₂ is a *function only* of the dimensionless ratio $R$ — it is independent of absolute intensity, LED aging, skin pigmentation (to first order), and tissue thickness. This is why pulse oximeters are robust.

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical: LIDAR Range and Backscatter

**Problem:** A LIDAR system emits a 10 ns laser pulse vertically. The detector registers a backscattered return at $t = 6.0$ µs after firing. Calculate:
(a) The altitude of the scattering layer.
(b) The received power if the transmitted power is 1 kW, telescope area $A = 0.05$ m², system efficiency $\eta = 0.4$, and backscatter coefficient $\beta = 10^{-4}$ m⁻¹·sr⁻¹.

**Step (a) — Range Calculation.**

$$R = \frac{c \, t}{2} = \frac{(3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}) \times (6.0 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s})}{2}$$

$$R = \frac{1800}{2} = 900 \text{ m}$$

> The scattering layer is at **900 m altitude**.

**Step (b) — Received Power Calculation.**
Pulse length in space: $c \tau = 3 \times 10^8 \times 10 \times 10^{-9} = 3$ m.

$$P_R = P_T \cdot \frac{c \tau}{2} \cdot \frac{A}{R^2} \cdot \eta \cdot \beta(R)$$

$$P_R = 1000 \cdot \frac{3}{2} \cdot \frac{0.05}{(900)^2} \cdot 0.4 \cdot 10^{-4}$$

$$P_R = 1000 \times 1.5 \times \frac{0.05}{810000} \times 0.4 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$P_R = 1000 \times 1.5 \times 6.173 \times 10^{-8} \times 0.4 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$P_R = 1000 \times 1.5 \times 2.469 \times 10^{-12}$$

$$P_R \approx 3.70 \times 10^{-9} \text{ W} = 3.70 \text{ nW}$$

> The detector must be sensitive enough to register **a few nanowatts** — this is why LIDAR receivers use **avalanche photodiodes (APDs)** in photon-counting mode.

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical: Two-Wavelength Pyrometer

**Problem:** A two-color pyrometer measures the radiance ratio of a steel target at $\lambda_1 = 0.65$ µm and $\lambda_2 = 0.90$ µm. The measured ratio $R_T = 2.40$. Calculate the target temperature.

**Step 1 — Recall the ratio equation.**

$$R_T = \left(\frac{\lambda_2}{\lambda_1}\right)^5 \exp\!\left[\frac{c_2}{T}\left(\frac{1}{\lambda_2} - \frac{1}{\lambda_1}\right)\right]$$

with $c_2 = 1.4388 \times 10^{-2}$ m·K.

**Step 2 — Compute the prefactor.**

$$\left(\frac{0.90}{0.65}\right)^5 = (1.3846)^5 \approx 5.087$$

**Step 3 — Take natural log of both sides.**

$$\ln(2.40) = \ln(5.087) + \frac{c_2}{T}\left(\frac{1}{0.90 \times 10^{-6}} - \frac{1}{0.65 \times 10^{-6}}\right)$$

$$0.8755 = 1.6275 + \frac{0.014388}{T}\left(1.1111 \times 10^6 - 1.5385 \times 10^6\right)$$

$$0.8755 = 1.6275 + \frac{0.014388}{T} \times (-4.274 \times 10^5)$$

$$0.8755 - 1.6275 = \frac{-6149.5}{T}$$

$$-0.7520 = \frac{-6149.5}{T}$$

$$T = \frac{6149.5}{0.7520} \approx 8177 \text{ K}$$

> The target temperature is approximately **8177 K** — characteristic of a high-temperature plasma or arc discharge.

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity Check:** Wien's law gives $\lambda_{max} = 2.898 \times 10^{-3}/8177 \approx 354$ nm (UV). The pyrometer is correctly sampling the rising tail of the Planck curve at red/NIR wavelengths.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Imaging Biomedical Techniques

| Parameter | Endoscopy | OCT | Ultrasound | MRI |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Physical wave | Visible light (NIR) | NIR light | Sound (MHz) | Radiofrequency |
| Penetration | Surface–few cm | 1–3 mm | ~10 cm | Whole body |
| Axial resolution | 50–100 µm | 1–15 µm | 100–500 µm | ~1 mm |
| Non-invasive? | Minimally (insertion) | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| Ionizing? | No | No | No | No |
| Cost | Low–moderate | High | Low | Very high |
| Real-time? | Yes | Yes (Fourier-domain) | Yes | No (slow) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** OCT's resolution is **10–100× finer than ultrasound** because light's wavelength is ~1000× smaller. The trade-off is limited penetration, since biological tissue strongly scatters visible/NIR light.

---

### 3.5 Sensitivity Comparison: Pollution Monitoring Techniques

| Technique | Target | Sensitivity | Range | Time Response |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| DOAS | NO₂, SO₂, O₃, benzene | ppb–ppm | 100 m – 10 km | seconds–minutes |
| LIDAR (DIAL) | Gases & aerosols | ppb | up to 10 km (vertically) | seconds |
| FTIR Spectroscopy | Multi-gas | ppm | 1–100 m path | seconds |
| Fiber-optic O₂ sensor | Dissolved O₂ in water | 0.01 ppm | in-situ probe | seconds |
| Nephelometer (scattering) | Particulates | µg/m³ | local | seconds |
| Chemiluminescence | NO, O₃ | ppt–ppb | point sampling | seconds |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

This is why ratio pyrometers are preferred for targets of unknown emissivity (molten metals, oxidized surfaces).
