## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Differentiate between elastic LIDAR, Raman LIDAR, and Differential Absorption LIDAR (DIAL). Mention one application of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| LIDAR Type | Principle | Return Wavelength | Key Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Elastic (Mie/Rayleigh)** | Backscatter from aerosols/molecules at the *same* transmitted wavelength | $\lambda_{out} = \lambda_{in}$ | Aerosol/cloud profiling, boundary-layer studies |
| **Raman LIDAR** | Inelastic scattering shifts wavelength by molecular vibrational frequency | $\lambda_{out} = \lambda_{in} \pm \Delta\lambda_{Raman}$ | Species-specific gas identification (N₂, O₂, H₂O) |
| **DIAL (Differential Absorption)** | Two wavelengths: one strongly absorbed by target gas ($\lambda_{on}$), one weakly absorbed ($\lambda_{off}$) | Both returned, ratio taken | Trace gas concentration profile (NO₂, SO₂, O₃) |

Each technique uses a different light–matter interaction: Rayleigh/Mie *scattering*, Raman *inelastic scattering*, and *differential absorption*. *(3 marks)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Explain the principle of two-wavelength (ratio) pyrometry. Why is it preferred over single-wavelength pyrometry?**

**Model Answer:**
A two-wavelength (ratio) pyrometer measures the spectral radiance of a hot target at two distinct wavelengths $\lambda_1$ and $\lambda_2$ and computes the *ratio* $R_T = L(\lambda_1, T)/L(\lambda_2, T)$.

From Planck's law, this ratio depends on $T$ but is *independent* of the target's surface emissivity $\varepsilon$ (since both wavelengths are emitted by the *same* surface, the same $\varepsilon$ appears in numerator and denominator and cancels out).

**Why preferred over single-wavelength pyrometry:**
1. **Emissivity independence** — works on oxidized, rough, or unknown surfaces.
2. **Immune to smoke, dust, and partial obscuration** that attenuate both wavelengths nearly equally.
3. **Self-calibrating** in the sense that the ratio is dimensionless.

A single-wavelength pyrometer, by contrast, requires the user to *input* the emissivity, which is often unknown and varies with temperature, surface condition, and viewing angle. *(3 marks)*

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Students commonly lose marks by (1) forgetting the **path-length differential logic** in pulse oximetry (no $\Delta L$ → no AC), (2) mixing up **DC and AC** components, (3) omitting the **emissivity cancellation** argument in pyrometer questions, and (4) forgetting that LIDAR uses the **factor of 2** in $R = ct/2$ because the light travels *out and back*. Always write the governing equation first; marks are awarded for structure.

---

#### **Question A [14 Marks]**

**(a) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Modified]**
**Describe the working of a LIDAR system for atmospheric pollution monitoring. Derive the LIDAR range equation and explain how DIAL measures gas concentration.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Working Principle.**
A LIDAR (Light Detection And Ranging) system emits a short, high-power laser pulse (typically Nd:YAG at 1064 nm or 532 nm) into the atmosphere. As the pulse propagates, it is scattered by molecules (Rayleigh), aerosols (Mie), and — if engineered to — absorbed by specific gas molecules. A fraction of this light is backscattered toward the instrument, collected by a telescope, and detected by a sensitive photodetector (APD or PMT). The time delay between emission and detection yields the range of the scattering/absorbing layer.

**Step 2 — Range Equation.**
Since light travels at speed $c$ and the path is *out and back*:

$$R = \frac{c \, t}{2}$$

where $t$ is the round-trip time between pulse emission and signal return.

**Step 3 — LIDAR Power Equation.**
For an elastic-scattering LIDAR, the received power from range $R$ is:

$$P_R(R) = P_T \cdot \frac{c \tau}{2} \cdot \frac{A}{R^2} \cdot \eta \cdot O(R) \cdot \beta(R) \cdot \exp\!\left[-2 \int_0^R \alpha(r) \, dr\right]$$

where:
- $P_T$: transmitted peak power
- $\tau$: pulse duration
- $A$: telescope collecting area
- $\eta$: overall optical efficiency
- $O(R)$: overlap function (geometric factor)
- $\beta(R)$: volume backscatter coefficient
- $\alpha(r)$: extinction coefficient
- The exponential term is the **two-way atmospheric transmission**.

**Step 4 — DIAL for Gas Concentration.**
In DIAL, two wavelengths are transmitted alternately:
- $\lambda_{on}$ — strongly absorbed by the target gas
- $\lambda_{off}$ — weakly absorbed (reference)

The ratio of received powers is:

$$\frac{P_{on}(R)}{P_{off}(R)} = \exp\!\left[-2 \, N(R) \, \Delta\sigma \cdot \Delta R\right]$$

Solving for the number density $N(R)$ of the absorbing gas:

$$N(R) = \frac{1}{2 \, \Delta\sigma \, \Delta R} \ln\!\left[\frac{P_{on}(R)}{P_{off}(R)}\right]$$

where $\Delta\sigma = \sigma_{on} - \sigma_{off}$ is the differential absorption cross-section, and $\Delta R$ is the range-bin thickness. By scanning the range gate, a **vertical concentration profile** of the pollutant is obtained.

**Step 5 — Significance.**
LIDAR/DIAL provides *range-resolved* pollutant data over kilometers of open path, which point samplers cannot do. *(7 marks)*

---

**(b) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
**Explain the principle of pulse oximetry. With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the measurement of blood oxygen saturation (SpO₂) using the ratio-of-ratios method.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Physiological Basis.**
Arterial blood oxygenation is the key vital sign. Oxygenated hemoglobin (HbO₂) and deoxygenated hemoglobin (Hb) have distinctly different optical absorption spectra:
- At 660 nm (red): Hb absorbs strongly; HbO₂ absorbs weakly.
- At 940 nm (IR): HbO₂ absorbs strongly; Hb absorbs weakly.

**Step 2 — Optical Setup.**
A clip-on probe houses two LEDs (660 nm and 940 nm) on one side of a finger/earlobe and a photodiode on the opposite side. The LEDs are time-multiplexed (typically 800 Hz switching) so the photodiode alternately sees red and IR transmission through the same tissue.

**Step 3 — Signal Decomposition.**
The photodiode output contains:
- A **DC component** — from non-pulsatile tissues: skin, bone, fat, venous blood.
- An **AC component** — from the small pulsatile increase in arterial blood volume with each heartbeat ($\Delta V \sim 1$–2%).

**Step 4 — Ratio-of-Ratios.**
Define for each wavelength:

$$\left(\frac{AC}{DC}\right)_\lambda = \text{fractional change in transmission}$$

Form the ratio of ratios:

$$R = \frac{(AC/DC)_{660}}{(AC/DC)_{940}}$$

**Step 5 — Empirical Calibration.**
Using the Beer–Lambert law, $R$ is a function only of the ratio of HbO₂ to total hemoglobin. Empirically, this is mapped to SpO₂ via a lookup table, with the linear fit:

$$SpO_2 (\%) = 110 - 25 R \quad \text{(for 0.4 < R < 1.0)}$$

**Step 6 — Block Diagram (refer to Section 4.2).**
The flow is: LEDs → finger → photodiode → TIA → DC/AC separation → ratio computation → SpO₂ display.

**Step 7 — Advantages.**
- Non-invasive
- Continuous and real-time
- Robust to absolute intensity variations (LED aging, skin tone) because of the *ratio-of-ratios* normalization. *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Question B [14 Marks] (Internal Choice Alternative)**

**(a) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**Describe the principle and working of Optical Coherence Tomography (OCT). Derive the expression for axial resolution and coherence length.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Principle.**
OCT is the optical analog of ultrasound imaging. It uses *low-coherence interferometry* to achieve micrometer-scale axial resolution in scattering biological tissue.

**Step 2 — Source Requirement.**
A *broadband*, low-coherence light source is essential — typically a superluminescent diode (SLD) or a femtosecond laser. For a Gaussian spectrum of central wavelength $\lambda_0$ and full-width-half-maximum bandwidth $\Delta\lambda$, the **coherence length** is:

$$L_c = \frac{4 \ln 2}{\pi} \cdot \frac{\lambda_0^2}{\Delta\lambda} \approx \frac{0.44 \, \lambda_0^2}{\Delta\lambda}$$

(With the simpler textbook form $L_c \approx \lambda^2/\Delta\lambda$ giving an order-of-magnitude estimate.)

**Step 3 — Interferometric Setup (Michelson).**
The broadband beam is split into two arms:
- **Sample arm** — focused onto the tissue.
- **Reference arm** — a movable mirror (time-domain OCT) or stationary mirror with Fourier-domain detection.

Interference fringes appear on the detector *only* when the optical path difference between the two arms is within the coherence length $L_c$. Thus, moving the reference mirror performs **optical sectioning** of the tissue — the detector records strong signal only from the depth where the path lengths match.

**Step 4 — Axial Resolution.**
Because interference occurs only over the coherence length, the *axial* (depth) resolution is:

$$\delta z = \frac{L_c}{2} = \frac{0.44 \, \lambda^2}{\Delta\lambda}$$

For $\lambda_0 = 1310$ nm and $\Delta\lambda = 50$ nm:

$$\delta z = \frac{0.44 \times (1310 \times 10^{-9})^2}{50 \times 10^{-9}} \approx 15 \, \mu m$$

**Step 5 — Lateral Resolution.**
Lateral resolution is set by the focused spot size, $\delta x = 1.22 \lambda / (2 \text{ NA})$, where NA is the numerical aperture of the objective lens — typically 10–25 µm.

**Step 6 — Imaging Modes.**
- **Time-domain OCT (TD-OCT):** Mechanical scanning of the reference arm.
- **Fourier-domain OCT (FD-OCT):** Spectrometer or swept-source + FFT — much faster and more sensitive.

**Step 7 — Applications.**
- Ophthalmology — retinal layer imaging for glaucoma, macular degeneration.
- Cardiology — intravascular OCT for plaque characterization.
- Dermatology — skin cancer margin assessment. *(7 marks)*

---

**(b) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**Explain the principle of Laser Doppler Velocimetry (LDV). Derive the expression for velocity measurement. List two advantages over mechanical probes.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Principle.**
LDV uses the **Doppler effect** on light scattered by small particles (natural contaminants, smoke, or seeded tracers) moving with the fluid flow. Two coherent laser beams of equal wavelength $\lambda$ intersect at a point called the **probe volume**.

**Step 2 — Fringe Formation.**
The two beams interfere in the intersection region, producing a pattern of straight, parallel bright and dark fringes of spacing:

$$d = \frac{\lambda}{2 \sin(\theta/2)}$$

where $\theta$ is the full angle between the two beams.

**Step 3 — Signal Generation.**
A particle moving perpendicular to the fringes (with velocity component $v_\perp$) crosses the bright fringes one by one. Each transition modulates the intensity of the scattered light at a frequency:

$$f_D = \frac{v_\perp}{d}$$

**Step 4 — Velocity Expression.**

$$\boxed{v = f_D \cdot d = f_D \cdot \frac{\lambda}{2 \sin(\theta/2)}}$$

**Step 5 — Direction Discrimination.**
The two beams are typically frequency-shifted slightly (using a Bragg cell) by $f_0$, so the Doppler frequency becomes $f_D \pm f_0$, giving a *sign* — i.e., distinguishing flow toward from flow away from the probe.

**Step 6 — Numerical Example.**
If $\lambda = 632.8$ nm (He-Ne laser), $\theta = 10°$, $f_D = 50$ kHz:

$$d = \frac{632.8 \times 10^{-9}}{2 \sin(5°)} = \frac{632.8 \times 10^{-9}}{0.1743} \approx 3.63 \, \mu m$$

$$v = 50 \times 10^3 \times 3.63 \times 10^{-6} \approx 0.18 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 7 — Advantages over mechanical probes (e.g., Pitot tubes, hot-wire anemometers):**
1. **Non-intrusive:** No physical probe in the flow, so the flow field is undisturbed — critical for boundary-layer and turbomachinery studies.
2. **No calibration drift:** The measurement is based on fundamental wavelength and geometry, so the probe never needs recalibration.
3. **Excellent spatial resolution:** Probe volume is typically $0.1 \times 0.1 \times 1$ mm³ — orders of magnitude smaller than any mechanical probe.
4. **Wide dynamic range:** Velocities from ~0.01 mm/s to supersonic (~Mach 5) flows measurable. *(7 marks)*

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision Checklist — Module 3.3**

**Pollution Monitoring**
- ☐ DOAS uses *open-path* light + retro-reflector; identifies gases by narrow absorption lines.
- ☐ LIDAR range equation always has the **factor of 2** in $R = ct/2$ (round trip).
- ☐ DIAL uses *two* wavelengths — on-line and off-line — to cancel the backscatter term.
- ☐ LIDAR can detect ppb-level concentrations over kilometers.

**Industrial Applications**
- ☐ **Wien's Displacement Law**: $\lambda_{max} T = 2.898 \times 10^{-3}$ m·K — peak of blackbody spectrum.
- ☐ **Stefan–Boltzmann Law**: $E = \sigma T^4$ — total radiated power per unit area.
- ☐ Two-color pyrometer is **emissivity-independent** — this is its key advantage.
- ☐ LDV measures velocity via the Doppler frequency of scattered light from particles crossing interference fringes.
- ☐ Barcode readers use a sweeping laser + photodiode; the binary pattern encodes data.

**Biomedical Applications**
- ☐ **Pulse oximetry uses 660 nm (red) and 940 nm (IR)** — these are the standard wavelengths.
- ☐ SpO₂ is computed from the **Ratio of Ratios** $R$; it is *independent* of absolute intensity.
- ☐ OCT axial resolution $\delta z \approx 0.44 \lambda^2 / \Delta\lambda$ — *smaller* bandwidth gives *worse* resolution.
- ☐ Endoscopy uses a *coherent* fiber bundle for image transmission; illumination is via a separate incoherent fiber bundle.
- ☐ Photodynamic therapy (PDT) requires a **photosensitizer + light + oxygen** — all three are necessary.
- ☐ OCT cannot image deeper than ~2–3 mm in tissue because of optical scattering — unlike ultrasound or MRI.

**Key Constants to Memorize**
- ☐ $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s
- ☐ $c_2 = 1.4388 \times 10^{-2}$ m·K (Wien's second radiation constant)
- ☐ $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ W·m⁻²·K⁻⁴ (Stefan–Boltzmann constant)
- ☐ $\lambda_0 T = 2.898 \times 10^{-3}$ m·K (Wien's displacement constant)
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Optical Fiber, Fiber Characteristics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT861 - OPTOELECTRONICS AND INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Optical Fiber, Fiber Characteristics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **optical fiber** is a cylindrical dielectric waveguide, typically fabricated from silica glass or plastic, that confines and guides electromagnetic energy in the form of visible, near-infrared, or infrared light along its axis by the principle of **Total Internal Reflection (TIR)**. It forms the fundamental transmission medium in modern optical communication, sensing, and instrumentation systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Concept):**
> An optical fiber is a thin, flexible, transparent filament of glass or plastic whose core refractive index ($n_1$) is marginally higher than that of its surrounding cladding ($n_2$). This refractive index differential traps light through total internal reflection, enabling low-loss, high-bandwidth propagation of optical signals.

### 1.2 Structural Anatomy of an Optical Fiber

| Layer | Material | Refractive Index | Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Core** | SiO₂ doped with GeO₂ | $n_1 \approx 1.48$ | Carries the optical signal |
| **Cladding** | Pure SiO₂ or B₂O₃ doped | $n_2 \approx 1.46$ | Confines light via TIR |
| **Buffer/Coating** | Acrylate polymer | — | Mechanical protection |
| **Jacket** | PVC / Hytrel | — | Environmental shielding |

> [!NOTE]
> Typical dimensions: **Core diameter = 8–62.5 µm**, **Cladding diameter = 125 µm** (standard telecom), and **Overall jacket diameter = 250–900 µm**.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Light Hose"

Imagine a long, transparent garden hose filled with water. If you shine a flashlight into one end at a sufficiently shallow angle, the light beam will *bounce* off the inner walls of the hose and emerge from the far end — even if the hose is bent! This is precisely the function of an optical fiber. The core acts as the water-filled region, the cladding mimics the hose's inner wall, and the "shallow angle" requirement is the **acceptance angle** of the fiber.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world Examples to Remember:**
> - **Telecommunications:** Undersea internet cables (e.g., MAREA, 200 Tbps)
> - **Medical:** Endoscopes and minimally invasive surgery tools
> - **Sensing:** Distributed temperature/strain sensing in oil wells and bridges
> - **Lighting:** Decorative fiber-optic lamps and signboards
> - **Defense:** EMI-immune data links in military aircraft

### 1.4 Why Optical Fiber Over Copper?

| Parameter | Copper Cable | Optical Fiber |
|---|---|---|
| Bandwidth | ~10 MHz·km | ~10 THz·km |
| Attenuation | 5–20 dB/km | < **0.2 dB/km** (at 1550 nm) |
| EMI Susceptibility | High | **Zero** |
| Weight | Heavy | Lightweight |
| Security | Eavesdroppable | Extremely **secure** |
| Cost (per km) | Lower (short range) | Lower (long haul) |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Snell's Law and the Foundation of Light Confinement

When a ray of light travels from an optically denser medium (higher $n$) to an optically rarer medium (lower $n$), it bends **away** from the normal. Beyond a certain angle, the ray is no longer transmitted but is reflected entirely back into the denser medium.

**Snell's Law of Refraction:**

$$n_1 \sin \theta_1 = n_2 \sin \theta_2$$

### 2.2 Critical Angle ($\theta_c$)

The **critical angle** is the angle of incidence in the denser medium ($n_1$) for which the refracted ray grazes the interface, i.e., $\theta_2 = 90°$.

$$\sin \theta_c = \frac{n_2}{n_1}$$

For $n_1 = 1.48$ and $n_2 = 1.46$:
$$\sin \theta_c = \frac{1.46}{1.48} = 0.9865 \implies \theta_c \approx 80.5°$$

### 2.3 Total Internal Reflection (TIR) — The Heart of Fiber Optics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Condition for TIR:** Light must travel from a higher-$n$ medium to a lower-$n$ medium **AND** strike the interface at an angle **greater than $\theta_c$**. Under these conditions, 100% of the optical power is reflected back — there is no refracted (transmitted) ray.

In a fiber, the core ($n_1$) is denser than the cladding ($n_2$). Light rays striking the core–cladding interface at angles exceeding $\theta_c$ are trapped and propagate via successive TIR events.

### 2.4 Acceptance Angle ($\theta_a$) and Numerical Aperture (NA)

Not all light entering the fiber face will propagate. Only rays entering within a certain **acceptance cone** will undergo TIR. The half-angle of this cone is the **acceptance angle ($\theta_a$)**.

The **Numerical Aperture (NA)** is defined as:

$$NA = n_0 \sin \theta_a$$

where $n_0$ is the refractive index of the surrounding medium (air, $n_0 \approx 1$).

For a step-index fiber, the NA simplifies to:

$$NA = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}$$

### 2.5 Mode Theory and V-Number

Light propagates in discrete electromagnetic field distributions called **modes**. The number of modes a fiber can support is governed by the **Normalized Frequency (V-number)**:

$$V = \frac{2\pi a}{\lambda} \cdot NA$$

where $a$ is the core radius and $\lambda$ is the operating wavelength.

- **V < 2.405** → **Single-mode fiber (SMF)**
- **V > 2.405** → **Multimode fiber (MMF)**

**Number of Guided Modes ($M$):**

$$M \approx \frac{V^2}{2} \quad \text{(Step-Index Multimode Fiber)}$$

$$M \approx \frac{V^2}{4} \quad \text{(Graded-Index Multimode Fiber)}$$

### 2.6 Fiber Types — A Comparative Summary

| Property | Step-Index MMF | Graded-Index MMF | Single-Mode Fiber |
|---|---|---|---|
| Index Profile | Abrupt step | Parabolic (graded) | Step (small core) |
| Core Diameter | 50–100 µm | 50–100 µm | 8–10 µm |
| NA | 0.2–0.5 | 0.2–0.3 | 0.1–0.15 |
| Modal Dispersion | **High** | **Reduced** | **Negligible** |
| Bandwidth | Low–Moderate | High | Very High |
| Cost | Lowest | Moderate | Higher (laser source) |
| Typical Use | LANs, sensors | LANs, data links | Telco, DWDM, long-haul |

### 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Bookmark this table** — it covers ~80% of numerical problems asked in KTU 2024 ESE on Module 4.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Snell's Law | $n_1 \sin \theta_1 = n_2 \sin \theta_2$ | dimensionless | — |
| 2 | Critical Angle | $\sin \theta_c = n_2 / n_1$ | rad/deg | 80°–89° |
| 3 | Numerical Aperture | $NA = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}$ | dimensionless | 0.1–0.5 |
| 4 | Acceptance Angle | $\sin \theta_a = NA / n_0$ | rad/deg | 5°–30° |
| 5 | Fractional Refractive Index ($\Delta$) | $\Delta = (n_1 - n_2) / n_1$ | dimensionless | 0.001–0.02 |
| 6 | NA (in terms of $\Delta$) | $NA \approx n_1 \sqrt{2\Delta}$ | dimensionless | — |
| 7 | V-Number | $V = (2\pi a / \lambda) \cdot NA$ | dimensionless | 0.5–50 |
| 8 | Cutoff V (SMF) | $V_c = 2.405$ (first zero of $J_0$) | — | — |
| 9 | Number of Modes (Step MMF) | $M = V^2 / 2$ | — | 10–1000 |
| 10 | Number of Modes (GRIN MMF) | $M = V^2 / 4$ | — | 5–500 |
| 11 | Attenuation Coefficient | $\alpha = (10 / L) \log_{10}(P_i / P_o)$ | dB/km | 0.15–10 |
| 12 | Output Power | $P_o = P_i \cdot 10^{-\alpha L / 10}$ | W or mW | — |
| 13 | Material Dispersion | $D_m = -\frac{\lambda}{c} \cdot \frac{d^2 n}{d\lambda^2}$ | ps/(nm·km) | 0.1–20 |
| 14 | Total Dispersion | $D_{tot} = \sqrt{D_m^2 + D_{wg}^2}$ | ps/(nm·km) | 1–20 |

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

The formulas above underpin every fiber-optic link design:

- **Link Power Budget** uses Eq. (11)–(12) to ensure the receiver gets sufficient power.
- **Bandwidth–Distance Product** uses Eq. (13)–(14) to guarantee error-free transmission.
- **System Designers** select fiber type (Step/GRIN/SMF) by computing $V$ from Eq. (7).
- **Sensor Engineers** use NA to control light-coupling efficiency into MEMS or biomedical probes.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Numerical Aperture from Snell's Law

**Given:** A step-index optical fiber with core index $n_1$, cladding index $n_2 < n_1$, surrounded by air ($n_0 = 1$). A ray strikes the fiber input face at an external angle $\theta_0$ (to the fiber axis), refracts inside the core at angle $\theta_1$ (to the axis), and strikes the core–cladding interface at angle $\phi$ (to the normal of the interface).

**Step 1 — Apply Snell's Law at the input face:**

$$n_0 \sin \theta_0 = n_1 \sin \theta_1$$

**Step 2 — Relate the ray angles using geometry:**

From the geometry of the ray within the core, the angle of incidence at the core–cladding interface and the refraction angle inside the core are complementary:

$$\theta_1 + \phi = 90° \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \theta_1 = 90° - \phi$$

$$\sin \theta_1 = \sin(90° - \phi) = \cos \phi$$

**Step 3 — Apply Snell's Law at the core–cladding interface:**

$$n_1 \sin \phi = n_2 \sin \phi'$$

For TIR to just be sustained, the refracted angle $\phi' = 90°$ (grazing emergence):

$$n_1 \sin \phi = n_2 \sin 90° = n_2$$

$$\sin \phi = \frac{n_2}{n_1} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \cos \phi = \sqrt{1 - \sin^2 \phi} = \sqrt{1 - \left(\frac{n_2}{n_1}\right)^2}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute back into Step 1:**

$$n_0 \sin \theta_a = n_1 \cos \phi = n_1 \sqrt{1 - \frac{n_2^2}{n_1^2}}$$

$$n_0 \sin \theta_a = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}$$

**Step 5 — Identify the right-hand side as the Numerical Aperture:**

$$NA = n_0 \sin \theta_a = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **single most-derived formula** in KTU Module 4. Be ready to reproduce it from first principles.

### 3.2 Derivation of Fractional Index Difference ($\Delta$) in Terms of NA

Starting from:

$$NA^2 = n_1^2 - n_2^2 = (n_1 - n_2)(n_1 + n_2)$$

Since for weakly guiding fibers $n_1 \approx n_2$, we approximate $n_1 + n_2 \approx 2n_1$:

$$NA^2 \approx 2n_1(n_1 - n_2)$$

Dividing both sides by $n_1^2$:

$$\frac{NA^2}{n_1^2} \approx \frac{2(n_1 - n_2)}{n_1} = 2\Delta$$

$$\Delta = \frac{n_1 - n_2}{n_1} \approx \frac{NA^2}{2n_1^2}$$

### 3.3 Derivation of Output Power After Length $L$ of Fiber

Let the input power be $P_i$ and the attenuation coefficient be $\alpha$ in **dB/km**.

**Step 1 — Definition of attenuation in dB:**

$$\text{Loss (dB)} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{P_i}{P_o}\right) = \alpha L$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $P_o$:**

$$\log_{10}\left(\frac{P_i}{P_o}\right) = \frac{\alpha L}{10}$$

$$\frac{P_i}{P_o} = 10^{\alpha L / 10}$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange:**

$$P_o = P_i \cdot 10^{-\alpha L / 10}$$

This is the **link power-budget equation** used in every optical communication design.

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A step-index fiber has $n_1 = 1.50$, $n_2 = 1.45$, core diameter $2a = 50 \,\mu m$, and operates at $\lambda = 1300 \,nm$. Calculate (a) NA, (b) acceptance angle, (c) V-number, and (d) number of guided modes.

**Solution (a) — NA:**

$$NA = \sqrt{(1.50)^2 - (1.45)^2} = \sqrt{2.2500 - 2.1025} = \sqrt{0.1475} \approx 0.384$$

**Solution (b) — Acceptance angle (in air, $n_0 = 1$):**

$$\sin \theta_a = NA = 0.384$$

$$\theta_a = \sin^{-1}(0.384) \approx 22.6°$$

**Solution (c) — V-number:**

$$V = \frac{2\pi a}{\lambda} \cdot NA = \frac{2\pi (25 \times 10^{-6})}{1.3 \times 10^{-6}} \cdot 0.384$$

$$V = \frac{2\pi \times 25}{1.3} \times 0.384 = \frac{157.08}{1.3} \times 0.384 \approx 120.83 \times 0.384 \approx 46.4$$

**Solution (d) — Number of modes (step-index MMF):**

$$M = \frac{V^2}{2} = \frac{(46.4)^2}{2} = \frac{2153.0}{2} \approx 1076 \text{ modes}$$

> [!TIP]
> This is a classic KTU numerical — three to four marks each on (a)–(d). Notice how the V-number is the gateway to mode count.

### 3.5 Dispersion in Optical Fibers

Dispersion causes pulse broadening and limits bandwidth. Three principal types:

1. **Modal Dispersion** (dominant in MMF): Different modes travel along different path lengths and arrive at different times.
2. **Material Dispersion** ($D_m$): Different wavelengths in a pulse travel at different speeds due to wavelength-dependent $n(\lambda)$.
3. **Waveguide Dispersion** ($D_{wg}$): Arises from the wavelength dependence of the modal distribution in the waveguide structure.

For SMF, **chromatic dispersion** is the sum:

$$D_{chr}(\lambda) = D_m(\lambda) + D_{wg}(\lambda)$$

A useful empirical relation (Sellmeier fit for silica):

$$D_{chr}(\lambda) \approx \frac{S_0}{4}\left(\lambda - \frac{\lambda_0^4}{\lambda^3}\right) \quad \text{[ps/(nm·km)]}$$

where $S_0 \approx 0.092 \, \text{ps/(nm}^2\text{·km)}$ and $\lambda_0 \approx 1310 \,nm$ is the **zero-dispersion wavelength**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Optical Fiber Link

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    srcA[Electrical Source] --> encA[Serializer and Line Coder]
    encA --> drvA[LED or Laser Driver]
    drvA --> txA[Optical Transmitter E O Converter]
    txA --> cxA[Optical Fiber Cable Core Cladding Buffer Jacket]
    cxA --> rxA[Optical Receiver Photodiode O E Converter]
    rxA --> ampA[Transimpedance Amplifier]
    ampA --> decA[Deserializer and Decoder]
    decA --> loadA[Electrical Load or Display]
    cxA --> splA[Splitter or Coupler]
    splA --> sena[Sensor Probe Optional]
    sena -.-> cxA
```

> [!NOTE]
> This block diagram emphasizes the signal path from the electrical domain → optical domain → fiber channel → optical domain → electrical domain. The optional sensor branch is included because **PEICT861 (Optoelectronics & Instrumentation)** also covers fiber-optic sensing.

### 4.2 Ray Propagation: TIR in a Step-Index Fiber

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Incident Ray in Air] --> nodeB[Refracts Into Core]
    nodeB --> nodeC{Strikes Core Cladding Interface}
    nodeC --> nodeD[Angle phi less than theta c]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Ray Refracts into Cladding and is LOST]
    nodeC --> nodeF[Angle phi greater than theta c]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Total Internal Reflection Occurs]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Ray Propagates by Successive TIR Bounces]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Ray Emerges at the Fiber Output]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This decision flow mirrors the TIR physics: the *first branch* ($\phi < \theta_c$) represents a leaky mode, while the *second branch* ($\phi > \theta_c$) represents a **bound mode** that is guided along the fiber.

### 4.3 Refractive Index Profiles — Step vs Graded Index

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeP[Step Index Profile] --> nodeP1[Core n1 is CONSTANT]
    nodeP1 --> nodeP2[Cladding n2 is CONSTANT]
    nodeP2 --> nodeP3[Sharp Jump at r equals a]

    nodeQ[Graded Index Profile] --> nodeQ1[Core n varies with r]
    nodeQ1 --> nodeQ2[Parabolic decrease toward r equals a]
    nodeQ2 --> nodeQ3[Continuously graded transition]
```

### 4.4 Attenuation Spectrum in Silica Fiber (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeWL[Wavelength Increase] --> nodeWL1[800 nm Region]
    nodeWL1 --> nodeWL2[1300 nm Local Minimum]
    nodeWL2 --> nodeWL3[1550 nm Absolute Minimum 0.15 dB per km]
    nodeWL3 --> nodeWL4[OH Peak at 1383 nm Water Absorption]
    nodeWL4 --> nodeWL5[1600 nm Plus Infrared Tail Rising]
```

> [!TIP]
> The three "**telecommunication windows**" to memorize: **850 nm**, **1310 nm**, and **1550 nm**. The 1550 nm window is preferred for long-haul because attenuation is minimum *and* EDFA (Erbium-Doped Fiber Amplifier) gain peaks here.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`** Define **Numerical Aperture (NA)** of an optical fiber. Mention its significance in fiber-optic communication.

**Model Answer:**

The **Numerical Aperture (NA)** of an optical fiber is a dimensionless quantity that describes the light-gathering ability of the fiber. It is defined as:

$$NA = n_0 \sin \theta_a = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}$$

where $\theta_a$ is the **acceptance angle**, $n_1$ is the core refractive index, and $n_2$ is the cladding refractive index.

**Significance:**
- Determines the **cone of acceptance** — only rays within this cone are guided via TIR.
- A higher NA means easier light coupling (looser source alignment tolerance).
- Higher NA increases the **V-number**, hence the number of guided modes, which in turn increases modal dispersion.
- Used to compute the **maximum coupling efficiency** between a source (LED/laser) and the fiber.

**(3 marks — full credit)**



**Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`** Explain the phenomenon of **Total Internal Reflection (TIR)** and list the **two conditions** necessary for it to occur in an optical fiber.

**Model Answer:**

**Total Internal Reflection** is the optical phenomenon in which a light ray, traveling from an optically denser medium to a rarer medium, is completely reflected back into the denser medium at the interface — with **100% efficiency** — instead of being refracted into the rarer medium.

**Two Necessary Conditions:**
1. The light must travel from a medium of **higher refractive index** to a medium of **lower refractive index** (i.e., $n_1 > n_2$).
2. The angle of incidence at the interface must be **greater than the critical angle** ($\phi > \theta_c$).

**Mathematically:**

$$n_1 > n_2 \quad \text{AND} \quad \phi > \theta_c = \sin^{-1}\left(\frac{n_2}{n_1}\right)$$

In an optical fiber, the core has $n_1 > n_2$ (cladding), so TIR is the underlying mechanism for light confinement.

**(3 marks — full credit)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> In 14-mark derivations, students routinely *lose 3–4 marks* by:
> (1) Forgetting to mention the **physical assumption** (e.g., $n_1 \approx n_2$ for weak guiding) used in any approximation.
> (2) Not writing the **units** of final answers (dB, ps/nm·km, µm, etc.).
> (3) Skipping the **substitution step** when computing numerical values — examiners want to see the arithmetic.
> (4) Omitting the **diagram** for ray optics problems (always draw a labeled ray diagram for at least 1–2 marks).

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]`

**(a)** Derive an expression for the **Numerical Aperture (NA)** of a step-index optical fiber in terms of the core and cladding refractive indices. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A step-index multimode fiber has $n_1 = 1.48$, $n_2 = 1.46$, core diameter $50 \,\mu m$, and is operated at $850 \,nm$. Determine: (i) Numerical Aperture, (ii) Acceptance angle, (iii) Normalized frequency $V$, and (iv) Number of guided modes. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

We consider a meridional ray entering the fiber end-face from air ($n_0 = 1$) at an external angle $\theta_0$ to the fiber axis.

**Step 1:** At the air–core interface, apply Snell's Law:

$$n_0 \sin \theta_0 = n_1 \sin \theta_1$$

**Step 2:** From the geometry, the angle of incidence at the core–cladding interface is:

$$\phi = 90° - \theta_1 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \sin \theta_1 = \cos \phi$$

**Step 3:** For TIR to occur, the refracted ray at the core–cladding interface must be at $\phi' = 90°$ (grazing). Apply Snell's Law:

$$n_1 \sin \phi = n_2 \sin 90° = n_2 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \sin \phi = \frac{n_2}{n_1}$$

**Step 4:** Therefore:

$$\cos \phi = \sqrt{1 - \sin^2 \phi} = \sqrt{1 - \frac{n_2^2}{n_1^2}} = \frac{\sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}}{n_1}$$

**Step 5:** Substituting into Step 1 with $\theta_0 = \theta_a$ (the limiting acceptance angle):

$$n_0 \sin \theta_a = n_1 \cdot \frac{\sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}}{n_1} = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}$$

**Step 6:** By definition, the Numerical Aperture is $NA = n_0 \sin \theta_a$. Hence:

$$\boxed{NA = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}}$$

The acceptance angle is then $\theta_a = \sin^{-1}(NA)$ for $n_0 = 1$.

**(7 marks — full credit)**

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Given:** $n_1 = 1.48$, $n_2 = 1.46$, $2a = 50 \,\mu m$ $\Rightarrow a = 25 \,\mu m = 25 \times 10^{-6} \,m$, $\lambda = 850 \,nm = 850 \times 10^{-9} \,m$.

**(i) Numerical Aperture:**

$$NA = \sqrt{(1.48)^2 - (1.46)^2} = \sqrt{2.1904 - 2.1316} = \sqrt{0.0588} \approx 0.2425$$

**$NA \approx 0.243$ (dimensionless)**

**(ii) Acceptance angle:**

$$\sin \theta_a = NA = 0.2425 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \theta_a \approx 14.04°$$

**$\theta_a \approx 14°$**

**(iii) Normalized frequency (V-number):**

$$V = \frac{2\pi a}{\lambda} \cdot NA = \frac{2\pi \times 25 \times 10^{-6}}{850 \times 10^{-9}} \times 0.2425$$

$$V = \frac{157.08 \times 10^{-6}}{850 \times 10^{-9}} \times 0.2425 = 184.8 \times 0.2425 \approx 44.81$$

**$V \approx 44.8$**

**(iv) Number of guided modes (Step-Index MMF):**

$$M = \frac{V^2}{2} = \frac{(44.81)^2}{2} = \frac{2007.9}{2} \approx 1004 \text{ modes}$$

**$M \approx 1004$ modes**

**(7 marks — full credit)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]`

**(a)** With the help of a **labeled diagram**, explain the **structure of an optical fiber** and describe the propagation of light through it using the **concept of modes**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the different **types of optical fibers** (step-index multimode, graded-index multimode, single-mode) in terms of refractive index profile, core diameter, NA, and typical applications. A graded-index fiber has a parabolic index profile $n(r) = n_1 \sqrt{1 - 2\Delta (r/a)^2}$ with $n_1 = 1.50$, $\Delta = 0.01$, and $a = 25 \,\mu m$, operating at $\lambda = 1300 \,nm$. Calculate the **number of guided modes**. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Diagram (Textual Representation):**

```
[ Jacket | Buffer | Cladding (n2) ║ Core (n1) ║ Cladding (n2) | Buffer | Jacket ]
       ←————————————— 125 µm standard ——————————————→
                ←————— 8 to 62.5 µm core —————→
```

**Structure:**
- **Core** ($r \le a$, $n_1$): Central region where light propagates. Diameter 8–62.5 µm.
- **Cladding** ($a \le r \le b$, $n_2 < n_1$): Surrounds the core and confines light via TIR. Outer diameter typically 125 µm.
- **Buffer Coating**: Protects against microbending losses. Diameter ~250 µm.
- **Jacket**: Outer protective PVC/Hytrel sheath. Diameter 900 µm.

**Modes:**
A **mode** is a self-consistent electromagnetic field distribution that propagates down the fiber with a specific phase velocity, satisfying Maxwell's equations and boundary conditions. Geometrically, each mode corresponds to a particular zigzag angle of a ray in the core.

- **Meridional rays** cross the fiber axis and propagate via TIR through the center.
- **Skew rays** spiral around the axis without crossing it.
- Each mode carries a fraction of the total optical power. The number of modes is governed by the **V-number**.

**(7 marks — full credit for diagram + explanation)**

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Comparison Table:**

| Property | Step-Index MMF | Graded-Index MMF | Single-Mode |
|---|---|---|---|
| Index Profile | $n(r) = n_1$ for $r \le a$, $n_2$ for $r > a$ | $n(r) = n_1 \sqrt{1 - 2\Delta (r/a)^2}$ | Step (small core) |
| Core Diameter | 50–100 µm | 50–100 µm | 8–10 µm |
| NA | 0.2–0.5 | 0.2–0.3 | 0.10–0.15 |
| Bandwidth | Low | High | Very High |
| Application | LAN, sensors, short links | LAN, telecom, data | Long-haul, DWDM, CATV |

**Numerical — Number of modes for GRIN fiber:**

**Step 1 — Compute NA using the relation $NA \approx n_1 \sqrt{2\Delta}$:**

$$NA = 1.50 \times \sqrt{2 \times 0.01} = 1.50 \times \sqrt{0.02} = 1.50 \times 0.1414 \approx 0.2121$$

**Step 2 — Compute V-number:**

$$V = \frac{2\pi a}{\lambda} \cdot NA = \frac{2\pi \times 25 \times 10^{-6}}{1.3 \times 10^{-6}} \times 0.2121$$

$$V = 120.83 \times 0.2121 \approx 25.63$$

**Step 3 — Number of modes (GRIN):**

$$M = \frac{V^2}{4} = \frac{(25.63)^2}{4} = \frac{656.9}{4} \approx 164 \text{ modes}$$

**$M \approx 164$ modes**

**(7 marks — full credit)**

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4 (Introduction to Optical Fiber & Characteristics)**

- **Three structural layers** of a fiber: **Core** ($n_1$), **Cladding** ($n_2 < n_1$), **Jacket/Buffer**.
- **Light guidance mechanism** is **Total Internal Reflection (TIR)** — needs $n_1 > n_2$ AND incidence angle $> \theta_c$.
- **Critical angle:** $\sin \theta_c = n_2 / n_1$.
- **Numerical Aperture:** $NA = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2} = n_0 \sin \theta_a$. Higher NA = better light gathering, but more modes and more dispersion.
- **Acceptance cone half-angle:** $\theta_a = \sin^{-1}(NA)$ in air.
- **V-number:** $V = (2\pi a / \lambda) \cdot NA$. Cutoff for SMF is **$V_c = 2.405$**.
- **Number of modes:** $M = V^2/2$ (step MMF) and $M = V^2/4$ (graded MMF).
- **Three telecommunication windows:** **850 nm** (short reach), **1310 nm** (zero dispersion), **1550 nm** (minimum loss, EDFA gain).
- **Attenuation equation:** $\alpha = (10/L) \log_{10}(P_i / P_o)$ in dB/km.
- **Output power:** $P_o = P_i \cdot 10^{-\alpha L / 10}$.
- **Dispersion types:** Modal (MMF), Material, Waveguide, Chromatic (SMF) — all limit bandwidth.
- **Zero-dispersion wavelength** of silica: $\lambda_0 \approx 1310 \,nm$.
- **Key design choice:** SMF for long-haul/high-bandwidth; MMF (GRIN) for LANs/cost-sensitive links; Step MMF only for very short, low-bandwidth use.
- **Always mention units** in final answers (µm, nm, dB, ps/(nm·km), W).
- **Always draw a ray diagram** for TIR/acceptance angle problems — it earns easy 1–2 marks.
- **Remember the weak-guiding approximation** $n_1 \approx n_2$ when deriving expressions involving $\Delta$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **End of Module 4 Notes — Introduction to Optical Fiber, Fiber Characteristics.**
> *Prepared in accordance with the KTU 2024 Scheme outcome-based education framework. Aligned to COs and Revised Bloom's Taxonomy (Understand, Apply, Analyze).*

topics.title: Light Guidance Through Fibers, Different Types of Fibers, Losses in the Optical Fiber, Dispersion, Optical Time Domain Reflectometer, Advantages and Disadvantages of Optical Fibers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT861 - OPTOELECTRONICS AND INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Light Guidance Through Fibers, Different Types of Fibers, Losses in the Optical Fiber, Dispersion, Optical Time Domain Reflectometer, Advantages and Disadvantages of Optical Fibers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Light Guidance Through Optical Fibers

**Optical Fiber** is a cylindrical dielectric waveguide that guides optical frequencies (light waves) through the principle of **Total Internal Reflection (TIR)**. It consists of three concentric regions:

1. **Core**: The central region (diameter 8–50 µm for single mode, 50–100 µm for multimode) made of glass/plastic with a higher refractive index $n_1$.
2. **Cladding**: Surrounds the core with a slightly lower refractive index $n_2$ ($n_2 < n_1$).
3. **Buffer/Coating**: Outer protective polymer layer.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Total Internal Reflection (TIR)** is the phenomenon where a light ray traveling from a higher refractive index medium to a lower refractive index medium is completely reflected back into the denser medium when the angle of incidence exceeds the **critical angle** $\theta_c$. This is the fundamental physical principle behind light propagation in optical fibers.

**Condition for TIR**: Angle of incidence $\theta_i > \theta_c$, where
$$\sin \theta_c = \frac{n_2}{n_1}$$

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Snooker Ball & Slide Analogy"

Imagine rolling a marble down a steep, polished slide (cladding) into a glass-walled tube (core). If the marble hits the glass wall at a very shallow angle, it bounces back perfectly — this is TIR. If it hits at a steep angle, it punches through and escapes. Light behaves the same way inside an optical fiber: only rays striking the core-cladding boundary at angles greater than the critical angle are "trapped" and guided.

> [!NOTE]
> **Acceptance Angle ($\theta_a$)**: The maximum angle (measured from the fiber axis) at which an entering light ray will still be totally internally reflected. Rays entering within this angle are successfully guided. Beyond this, they leak out through the cladding.

---

### 1.3 Acceptance Cone & Numerical Aperture (NA)

The light entering the fiber within the **acceptance cone** (a 3D cone of half-angle $\theta_a$) is propagated. Outside this cone, light refracts through the cladding and is lost.

**Numerical Aperture (NA)** is a dimensionless figure of merit representing the light-gathering ability of the fiber:

$$NA = n_0 \sin \theta_a = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}$$

where $n_0 \approx 1$ for air, so $NA \approx \sin \theta_a$.

A higher NA means the fiber can collect more light — like a wider funnel. Typical NA values: **0.1** (single mode) to **0.4** (multimode).

---

### 1.4 Classification of Optical Fibers

Optical fibers are classified based on **refractive index profile** and **mode of propagation**:

| Classification Basis | Types |
|---|---|
| Refractive Index Profile | Step-Index Fiber (SIF), Graded-Index Fiber (GIF) |
| Number of Modes | Single-Mode Fiber (SMF), Multi-Mode Fiber (MMF) |
| Material | Glass fiber, Plastic fiber |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Step-Index Fiber**: The refractive index changes abruptly (a "step") from $n_1$ in the core to $n_2$ in the cladding, with constant $n_1$ across the core.
>
> **Graded-Index Fiber**: The refractive index of the core decreases radially from maximum at the center to minimum at the cladding interface following a parabolic profile.

---

### 1.5 Losses in Optical Fibers (Attenuation)

**Attenuation** is the gradual loss of optical power as light travels through the fiber, measured in **dB/km**. The total transmission loss is:

$$\alpha_{dB/km} = -\frac{10}{L} \log_{10}\left(\frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}}\right)$$

The three primary loss mechanisms are:

1. **Absorption Losses**: Material absorption (intrinsic — OH⁻ ion absorption peak at **1383 nm**, **950 nm**, and **725 nm**), impurity absorption (transition metals Fe, Cu, Co).
2. **Scattering Losses**: Rayleigh scattering (dominant at short wavelengths, $\propto \lambda^{-4}$), Mie scattering (from imperfections).
3. **Bending Losses**:
   - **Macro-bending losses**: Losses due to large-radius bends (mm scale).
   - **Micro-bending losses**: Losses due to microscopic distortions (µm scale).

---

### 1.6 Dispersion in Optical Fibers

**Dispersion** is the phenomenon where light pulses spread out as they travel along the fiber, causing **Inter-Symbol Interference (ISI)** and limiting bandwidth-distance product.

Types of dispersion:

| Type | Origin | Nature |
|---|---|---|
| **Modal Dispersion** | Different path lengths of rays | Multimode fibers only |
| **Material Dispersion** | $n(\lambda)$ varies with wavelength | Intrinsic |
| **Waveguide Dispersion** | Geometry of fiber (V-number) | Intrinsic |
| **Polarization Mode Dispersion (PMD)** | Different group velocities of orthogonal polarizations | SMF |

---

### 1.7 Optical Time Domain Reflectometer (OTDR)

The **OTDR** is the optical equivalent of an electrical Time Domain Reflectometer (TDR). It is used to **characterize, test, and fault-locate** an optical fiber.

**Working Principle**: It injects a series of narrow optical pulses into one end of the fiber and measures the **back-scattered (Rayleigh)** and **back-reflected (Fresnel)** light as a function of time. From this, the OTDR constructs a trace showing attenuation vs. distance, identifying splices, connectors, bends, and breaks.

> [!NOTE]
> **Backscatter Coefficient**: The fraction of incident power returned to the source. It is extremely small (~10⁻⁶ to 10⁻⁸), so OTDRs use high-sensitivity APDs and signal averaging.

---

### 1.8 Advantages and Disadvantages of Optical Fibers

**Advantages**:
- **Immune to EMI/RFI** — operates in electrically noisy environments.
- **Enormous bandwidth** — THz-range capacity.
- **Low attenuation** — **0.2 dB/km** at 1550 nm.
- **Lightweight and small diameter** — easier installation.
- **Secure** — extremely difficult to tap.
- **No short-circuit or fire risk**.
- **Long repeater spacing** (>100 km).

**Disadvantages**:
- **High initial cost** for cables, connectors, splicers.
- **Fragility** — glass fibers break under stress.
- **Specialized tools** required for splicing and termination.
- **Repair is complex and costly**.
- **Power limitation** — cannot carry high-power signals.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Snell's Law and Total Internal Reflection — Theoretical Foundation

When a ray travels from a medium of refractive index $n_1$ into one of refractive index $n_2$, the angles of incidence $\theta_i$ and refraction $\theta_r$ obey **Snell's Law**:

$$n_1 \sin \theta_i = n_2 \sin \theta_r$$

**The Critical Angle** $\theta_c$ is the angle of incidence in the denser medium ($n_1$) for which the angle of refraction becomes $90^\circ$ in the rarer medium ($n_2$):

$$n_1 \sin \theta_c = n_2 \sin 90^\circ = n_2$$

$$\therefore \sin \theta_c = \frac{n_2}{n_1}$$

- **Why TIR happens**: When $\theta_i > \theta_c$, $\sin \theta_r = (n_1/n_2) \sin \theta_i > 1$, which is mathematically impossible for a real angle. Hence all energy is reflected. This is TIR.

---

### 2.2 Numerical Aperture — Step-by-Step Logic

Consider a ray entering the fiber core from air ($n_0 = 1$) at an angle $\theta_0$ to the fiber axis.

**Step 1**: Apply Snell's law at the air-core boundary (front face):
$$\sin \theta_0 = n_1 \sin \theta_1$$
where $\theta_1$ is the refracted angle inside the core.

**Step 2**: The ray then hits the core-cladding interface at angle $\theta_i = 90^\circ - \theta_1$. For TIR: $\theta_i \geq \theta_c$.

**Step 3**: $\sin \theta_i = \cos \theta_1 \geq \sin \theta_c = n_2/n_1$.

**Step 4**: Substitute $\cos \theta_1 = \sqrt{1 - \sin^2 \theta_1}$ and use $\sin \theta_1 = \sin \theta_0 / n_1$:

$$\sqrt{1 - \frac{\sin^2 \theta_0}{n_1^2}} \geq \frac{n_2}{n_1}$$

$$\frac{n_1^2 - \sin^2 \theta_0}{n_1^2} \geq \frac{n_2^2}{n_1^2}$$

$$n_1^2 - \sin^2 \theta_0 \geq n_2^2$$

$$\sin^2 \theta_0 \leq n_1^2 - n_2^2$$

**Step 5**: Maximum angle is when equality holds, defining the acceptance angle:

$$NA = \sin \theta_a = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}$$

---

### 2.3 Types of Fibers — Detailed Analysis

#### (A) Step-Index Single-Mode Fiber (SMF)
- Core diameter ≈ **8–10 µm**.
- Only one path (fundamental mode LP₀₁) propagates.
- **Zero modal dispersion** (since only one mode).
- Used in **long-haul telecommunications**, **submarine cables**.

#### (B) Step-Index Multi-Mode Fiber (MMF)
- Core diameter ≈ **50–200 µm**.
- Many modes propagate simultaneously.
- High modal dispersion → **low bandwidth**.
- NA ≈ 0.2–0.5.

#### (C) Graded-Index Multi-Mode Fiber
- Core diameter ≈ **50–100 µm**.
- Refractive index varies parabolically:
$$n(r) = n_1 \sqrt{1 - 2\Delta\left(\frac{r}{a}\right)^\alpha}$$
where $\alpha = 2$ for parabolic index, $a$ is core radius, $\Delta = (n_1 - n_2)/n_1$ is relative index difference.
- Higher-order modes travel in longer helical paths but **faster** (since $n$ is lower away from axis), so they arrive at the same time as axial rays → **reduced modal dispersion**.
- Bandwidth–distance product ≈ **500 MHz·km to 2 GHz·km**.

---

### 2.4 Loss Mechanisms in Optical Fibers

**(i) Absorption Loss**
- Intrinsic: Material resonance absorption in the UV (band tail) and IR (atomic vibrations).
- Impurity: Transition metals cause peaks near 700–1000 nm; OH⁻ ion peaks at 725, 950, 1383 nm.
- Attenuation constant:
$$\alpha_{abs} = \text{(material-dependent)}$$

**(ii) Rayleigh Scattering Loss**
- Caused by microscopic density fluctuations frozen-in during fiber manufacture.
- Loss coefficient:
$$\alpha_R = \frac{8\pi^3}{3\lambda^4} \left(n_1^2 - 1\right) k_B T_f \beta_T$$
where $k_B$ is Boltzmann constant, $T_f$ is fictive temperature, $\beta_T$ is isothermal compressibility.
- Dominant below 1500 nm; falls as $\lambda^{-4}$.

**(iii) Bending Losses**
- **Macrobending**: $\alpha_M = A \exp(-R/R_c)$, where $R$ is bend radius, $R_c$ critical radius.
- **Microbending**: Loss due to coupling between guided and radiation modes from small-scale perturbations.

**Total Loss**:
$$\alpha_{total} = \alpha_{abs} + \alpha_R + \alpha_{M} + \alpha_{\mu}$$

Typical values: **0.35 dB/km at 1300 nm**, **0.2 dB/km at 1550 nm**.

---

### 2.5 Dispersion Analysis

**Total Dispersion** in a fiber is the sum of all dispersion components:

$$\tau_{total} = \sqrt{\tau_{modal}^2 + \tau_{material}^2 + \tau_{waveguide}^2 + \tau_{PMD}^2}$$

**(i) Modal (Intermodal) Dispersion**
- For step-index MMF:
$$\Delta t_{modal} = \frac{L \cdot n_1 \cdot \Delta}{c}$$
where $\Delta$ is relative index difference, $L$ is fiber length.
- For graded-index (parabolic) MMF: drastically reduced (≈$\Delta/2$).

**(ii) Material Dispersion**
- Arises because $dn/d\lambda \neq 0$.
$$\Delta t_{mat} = \frac{L \cdot \lambda}{c} \left\vert \frac{d^2 n}{d\lambda^2} \right\vert$$

**(iii) Waveguide Dispersion**
- Arises due to wavelength dependence of mode propagation constant $\beta(\omega)$.
$$\Delta t_{wg} = \frac{L}{c} \frac{dN_g}{d\lambda} \Delta\lambda$$
where $N_g$ is group index.

**(iv) Polarization Mode Dispersion (PMD)**
- Significant only in single-mode fibers above ~10 Gbps.
$$\Delta t_{PMD} = D_{PMD} \sqrt{L}$$
where $D_{PMD}$ is PMD coefficient (ps/√km).

---

### 2.6 Optical Time Domain Reflectometer (OTDR) — Operating Principle

The OTDR functions on three key physical phenomena:

1. **Rayleigh Backscattering**: Microscopic inhomogeneities scatter light in all directions. A fraction returns to the source.
2. **Fresnel Reflection**: At discrete interfaces (connectors, splices, fiber ends), a discrete reflected pulse occurs.
3. **Time-of-Flight Measurement**: Knowing the speed of light in glass ($c/n_1$), the time delay maps directly to distance along the fiber.

**Distance to event**:
$$d = \frac{c \cdot t}{2 n_1}$$

The factor of 2 accounts for round-trip travel.

**Dynamic range and dead zone**: These define the OTDR's measurement capability. **Event Dead Zone** is the minimum distance between two reflective events that can be distinguished.

---

### 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\sin \theta_c = n_2/n_1$ | Critical angle condition | dimensionless |
| 2 | $NA = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}$ | Numerical Aperture | dimensionless |
| 3 | $\theta_a = \sin^{-1}(NA)$ | Acceptance angle | degrees/radians |
| 4 | $V = (2\pi a/\lambda) NA$ | Normalized frequency (V-number) | dimensionless |
| 5 | $N_{modes} \approx V^2/2$ (MMF) | Number of guided modes | count |
| 6 | $N_{modes} = 1$ for $V < 2.405$ | SMF condition | dimensionless |
| 7 | $\alpha_{dB} = -10/L \log(P_{out}/P_{in})$ | Attenuation | dB/km |
| 8 | $P_{out} = P_{in} \cdot 10^{-\alpha L/10}$ | Power after length L | W or dBm |
| 9 | $\Delta t_{modal} = (L n_1 \Delta)/c$ | Modal dispersion (SIF) | s |
| 10 | $d = c t/(2 n_1)$ | OTDR distance | m |
| 11 | $BW \cdot L = 0.187/\Delta t$ | Bandwidth-distance product | Hz·km |
| 12 | $n(r) = n_1 \sqrt{1 - 2\Delta (r/a)^\alpha}$ | Graded index profile | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For a step-index multimode fiber with NA = 0.3, $n_1 = 1.5$, and operating wavelength 850 nm with core radius $a = 25$ µm, $V \approx 55.4$, giving $N_{modes} \approx 1534$ modes — a key number to remember.

---

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Telecommunications**: SMF with zero modal dispersion is the backbone of the internet (submarine cables span 10,000+ km).
- **Medical endoscopy**: Coherent fiber bundles transmit images from inside the body.
- **Sensing**: Fiber Bragg Gratings sense temperature, strain, and pressure in bridges and oil wells.
- **Military**: Tethered munition guidance, secure data links (immune to EMP).
- **Data centers**: 100G/400G/800G optical Ethernet links use MMF (OM3/OM4) for short reaches.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Numerical Aperture from Snell's Law

**Problem**: Derive the expression for Numerical Aperture (NA) of a step-index optical fiber using Snell's law at the input face and TIR at the core-cladding boundary.

**Given**: Core index $n_1$, cladding index $n_2$ ($n_2 < n_1$), surrounding medium index $n_0 = 1$ (air).

**Step 1**: A ray enters the front face of the fiber at angle $\theta_0$ to the fiber axis. Apply Snell's law at the air-core interface:

$$n_0 \sin \theta_0 = n_1 \sin \theta_1$$

Since $n_0 = 1$:
$$\sin \theta_0 = n_1 \sin \theta_1 \quad \text{...(i)}$$

**Step 2**: Inside the core, the ray strikes the core-cladding interface. By geometry, the angle of incidence at this interface is:

$$\theta_i = 90^\circ - \theta_1$$

**Step 3**: For TIR (just at the threshold of acceptance), the angle of incidence equals the critical angle $\theta_c$:

$$\theta_i = \theta_c \implies 90^\circ - \theta_1 = \theta_c$$

$$\theta_1 = 90^\circ - \theta_c$$

$$\sin \theta_1 = \cos \theta_c$$

**Step 4**: Substitute into (i):

$$\sin \theta_0 = n_1 \cos \theta_c$$

**Step 5**: From the critical angle formula:

$$\cos \theta_c = \sqrt{1 - \sin^2 \theta_c} = \sqrt{1 - \left(\frac{n_2}{n_1}\right)^2} = \frac{\sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}}{n_1}$$

**Step 6**: Substitute:

$$\sin \theta_0 = n_1 \cdot \frac{\sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}}{n_1} = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}$$

**Step 7**: By definition, NA equals this maximum value of $\sin \theta_0$:

$$\boxed{NA = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}}$$

This is the **maximum NA of a step-index fiber**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Critical Angle for a Silica Fiber

**Given**: $n_1 = 1.48$ (core), $n_2 = 1.46$ (cladding).

**Step 1**: Apply critical angle condition:

$$\sin \theta_c = \frac{n_2}{n_1} = \frac{1.46}{1.48} = 0.9865$$

**Step 2**: Compute:

$$\theta_c = \sin^{-1}(0.9865) \approx 80.55^\circ$$

This means any ray striking the boundary at $> 80.55^\circ$ from the normal will undergo TIR.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Modal Dispersion in Step-Index Fiber

**Problem**: A multimode step-index fiber has $n_1 = 1.5$, $\Delta = 0.01$, $L = 1$ km. Calculate the maximum pulse broadening due to modal dispersion.

**Step 1**: The axial ray (mode) travels along the fiber axis with velocity $v = c/n_1$. Time taken:

$$t_{min} = \frac{L \cdot n_1}{c}$$

**Step 2**: The highest-order mode travels at the critical angle $\theta_c$ to the axis. Its path zig-zags; the path length is $L/\cos\theta_c \approx L/\sin\theta_c$ (for shallow angles near the axis, $\theta_c \approx 90^\circ$).

For the extreme ray entering at acceptance angle $\theta_a$, the effective path length becomes:

$$L_{eff} = \frac{L}{\sin \theta_c} = \frac{L \cdot n_1}{n_2}$$

**Step 3**: Maximum time:

$$t_{max} = \frac{L \cdot n_1}{c \cdot \sin \theta_c} = \frac{L \cdot n_1^2}{c \cdot n_2}$$

**Step 4**: Pulse broadening (difference):

$$\Delta t = t_{max} - t_{min} = \frac{L \cdot n_1}{c}\left(\frac{n_1}{n_2} - 1\right) = \frac{L \cdot n_1}{c} \cdot \frac{n_1 - n_2}{n_2}$$

**Step 5**: Since $\Delta = (n_1 - n_2)/n_1 \implies n_1 - n_2 = \Delta n_1$, and $n_2 \approx n_1$:

$$\Delta t_{modal} \approx \frac{L \cdot n_1 \cdot \Delta}{c}$$

**Step 6**: Numerical evaluation:

$$\Delta t_{modal} = \frac{(1 \times 10^3)(1.5)(0.01)}{3 \times 10^8} = \frac{15}{3 \times 10^8} = 5 \times 10^{-8}\ \text{s} = 50\ \text{ns}$$

**Step 7**: Corresponding bandwidth-length product:

$$BW \cdot L = \frac{0.187}{\Delta t} = \frac{0.187}{50 \times 10^{-9}} = 3.74 \times 10^6\ \text{Hz·km} = 3.74\ \text{MHz·km}$$

This extremely low bandwidth is why SMF is used for long-distance high-bit-rate links.

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Number of Modes in a Multimode Step-Index Fiber

**Step 1**: The **V-number** (normalized frequency) is defined as:

$$V = \frac{2\pi a}{\lambda} NA$$

where $a$ is core radius, $\lambda$ is operating wavelength.

**Step 2**: The total number of guided modes for a step-index fiber is:

$$M = \frac{V^2}{2}$$

**Step 3**: Derivation sketch: Each mode occupies a "cell" in phase space with area $\pi^2/a^2$ for radial and $\pi/(a)$ for azimuthal modes. The total allowed modes up to propagation constant $\beta$ inside the fiber equals $V^2/2$ (Beer-Lambert-like counting).

**Step 4**: For SMF condition: $V < 2.405$ (the first zero of Bessel function $J_0$). At $V = 2.405$, only the fundamental LP₀₁ mode propagates.

**Example**: For $a = 25$ µm, NA = 0.3, $\lambda = 850$ nm:

$$V = \frac{2\pi (25 \times 10^{-6})}{850 \times 10^{-9}} \cdot 0.3 = 184.7 \cdot 0.3 \approx 55.4$$

$$M = \frac{(55.4)^2}{2} \approx 1534\ \text{modes}$$

---

### 3.5 Derivation: Power Loss Over Fiber Length

**Problem**: Optical power of 1 mW is launched into a fiber of length 50 km. Attenuation coefficient $\alpha = 0.5$ dB/km. Find output power.

**Step 1**: Total attenuation in dB:

$$\alpha L = 0.5 \times 50 = 25\ \text{dB}$$

**Step 2**: Convert to linear ratio using:

$$\frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = 10^{-\alpha L / 10} = 10^{-25/10} = 10^{-2.5}$$

**Step 3**: Compute:

$$10^{-2.5} = 10^{-3} \times 10^{0.5} = 0.001 \times 3.162 = 3.162 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 4**: Therefore:

$$P_{out} = 1\ \text{mW} \times 3.162 \times 10^{-3} = 3.162\ \mu\text{W}$$

This shows how a 25-dB loss reduces power by ~316×.

---

### 3.6 Derivation: OTDR Distance Resolution

**Problem**: An OTDR launches a 100 ns pulse into a fiber with $n_1 = 1.5$. Find the minimum distinguishable distance between two reflective events (spatial resolution).

**Step 1**: The pulse occupies a length in the fiber:

$$L_{pulse} = \frac{c \cdot \tau}{n_1} = \frac{(3 \times 10^8)(100 \times 10^{-9})}{1.5} = 20\ \text{m}$$

**Step 2**: Two events separated by less than this will overlap on the OTDR trace.

**Step 3**: Since light travels to the event and back:

$$\Delta d_{min} = \frac{L_{pulse}}{2} = 10\ \text{m}$$

This is the **event dead zone** of the OTDR for 100-ns pulses.

---

### 3.7 Analytical Matrix — Attenuation vs. Wavelength Windows

| Window | Wavelength Range | Attenuation | Primary Cause |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1st | 850 nm | 2–3 dB/km | Rayleigh scattering + OH⁻ peak |
| 2nd | 1300 nm (O-band) | 0.35 dB/km | Material dispersion minimum |
| 3rd (C-band) | 1550 nm | 0.20 dB/km | Lowest attenuation |
| 4th (L-band) | 1625 nm | 0.25 dB/km | Bend loss increases |

> [!NOTE]
> Modern DWDM systems use the **C-band (1530–1565 nm)** and **L-band (1565–1625 nm)** for minimal attenuation and highest bandwidth.

---

### 3.8 Comparative Performance Matrix — Fiber Types

| Property | SMF | MMF (Step-Index) | MMF (Graded-Index) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Core diameter | 8–10 µm | 50–200 µm | 50–100 µm |
| NA | 0.1–0.15 | 0.2–0.5 | 0.2–0.3 |
| Modal dispersion | None | Very high | Low |
| Bandwidth·Distance | >500 GHz·km | <50 MHz·km | ~1–2 GHz·km |
| Cost (source) | High (laser) | Low (LED) | Low (LED) |
| Application | Long-haul, submarine | LAN (legacy) | LAN, data centers |
