```mermaid
        absorp --> imp["IMPURITY ABSORPTION OH ION PEAK AT 1383 NM"]

        scatter --> ray["RAYLEIGH SCATTERING INVERSE LAMBDA POWER FOUR"]
        scatter --> mie["MIE SCATTERING FROM IMPERFECTIONS"]

        bend --> macro["MACROBENDING LARGE RADIUS BENDS"]
        bend --> micro["MICROBENDING MICROSCOPIC DISTORTIONS"]

        coupling --> splice["SPLICE LOSS TYPICAL 0.1 DB"]
        coupling --> conn["CONNECTOR LOSS TYPICAL 0.3 DB"]
    end

    totLoss --> absorp
    totLoss --> scatter
    totLoss --> bend
    totLoss --> coupling
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip**: Always draw the ray diagram with at least three rays — axial, meridional (zigzag), and a leaky ray that escapes through cladding — when explaining light guidance. This fetches full marks.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]**
*Define Numerical Aperture (NA) of an optical fiber. Derive its expression in terms of core and cladding refractive indices.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark)**: Numerical Aperture is a dimensionless quantity that measures the light-gathering ability of an optical fiber. It is defined as the sine of the maximum acceptance angle.

$$NA = \sin \theta_a$$

**Derivation (2 marks)**: Applying Snell's law at the air-core interface and the TIR condition at the core-cladding interface:

$$n_1 \sin(90^\circ - \theta_c) = n_0 \sin \theta_a$$

With $n_0 = 1$ and $\sin \theta_c = n_2/n_1$, this reduces to:

$$\boxed{NA = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}}$$

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]**
*What is Total Internal Reflection? State the necessary condition for TIR to occur.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks)**: Total Internal Reflection is the optical phenomenon in which a light ray traveling from a medium of higher refractive index ($n_1$) to a medium of lower refractive index ($n_2$) is completely reflected back into the denser medium at the boundary, with no refraction into the rarer medium.

**Condition (1.5 marks)**:
1. The ray must travel from a **denser medium to a rarer medium** (i.e., $n_1 > n_2$).
2. The **angle of incidence** must be **greater than the critical angle** $\theta_c$, where:
$$\sin \theta_c = \frac{n_2}{n_1}$$

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1**

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model]**

**(a) Explain with a neat diagram the principle of light propagation through an optical fiber. Derive the expression for Numerical Aperture and Acceptance Angle of a step-index fiber. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution**:

**Step 1 — Light propagation principle (2 marks)**:
An optical fiber is a cylindrical dielectric waveguide consisting of a central **core** (refractive index $n_1$) surrounded by **cladding** (refractive index $n_2$, where $n_2 < n_1$). Light rays entering the fiber within the **acceptance cone** undergo **total internal reflection (TIR)** at the core-cladding boundary, thus getting guided along the fiber.

**Step 2 — Diagram (2 marks)**:

```
              Acceptance Cone
                   \  |  /
                    \ | /
                ----\|/----
               /    O    \      <-- Fiber core
              /  /  |  \  \
             /  /   |   \  \   <-- TIR rays
            /  /    |    \  \
```

Three rays must be shown: an axial ray, a meridional zig-zag ray (TIR), and a leaky ray escaping through cladding.

**Step 3 — Derivation of NA (3 marks)**:
At the air-core front face, Snell's law gives:
$$\sin \theta_0 = n_1 \sin \theta_1$$

For TIR at the core-cladding boundary, the angle of incidence there is $90^\circ - \theta_1 \geq \theta_c$, where:
$$\sin \theta_c = \frac{n_2}{n_1}$$

Combining:
$$\sin \theta_a = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2} = NA$$

**Acceptance angle**:
$$\theta_a = \sin^{-1}(NA) = \sin^{-1}\sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}$$

---

**(b) Discuss the different types of optical fibers based on refractive index profile and mode of propagation. Compare their bandwidth and applications. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution**:

**Classification — Refractive Index Profile (3 marks)**:

1. **Step-Index Fiber (SIF)**: Refractive index is constant in the core ($n_1$) and drops abruptly to $n_2$ in the cladding. Sharp step boundary.

2. **Graded-Index Fiber (GIF)**: Refractive index varies continuously, maximum at the center, decreasing parabolically towards cladding. Profile: $n(r) = n_1\sqrt{1 - 2\Delta(r/a)^2}$.

**Classification — Mode of Propagation (2 marks)**:

3. **Single-Mode Fiber (SMF)**: Core diameter 8–10 µm; $V < 2.405$; only fundamental LP₀₁ mode propagates. No modal dispersion.

4. **Multi-Mode Fiber (MMF)**: Core diameter 50–200 µm; many modes propagate; high modal dispersion.

**Comparison Table (2 marks)**:

| Property | SMF | Step-Index MMF | Graded-Index MMF |
|---|---|---|---|
| Bandwidth | >500 GHz·km | ~50 MHz·km | ~1–2 GHz·km |
| Source | Laser | LED | LED |
| Application | Submarine, long-haul | LAN (legacy) | LAN, data centers |

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]**

**(a) Explain the various loss mechanisms in optical fibers. Discuss the wavelength dependence of attenuation and identify the transmission windows. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution**:

**Step 1 — Absorption Losses (2 marks)**:
- **Intrinsic absorption**: Caused by electronic transitions in UV and molecular vibrations in IR bands.
- **Impurity absorption**: OH⁻ ion produces strong absorption peaks at 725 nm, 950 nm, and especially 1383 nm (hydroxyl peak). Transition metals (Fe, Cu, Co) cause peaks in the visible region.

**Step 2 — Scattering Losses (2 marks)**:
- **Rayleigh scattering**: Caused by microscopic density fluctuations frozen during manufacture. Loss $\propto \lambda^{-4}$. Dominant below 1500 nm.
- **Mie scattering**: From larger imperfections like bubbles, voids, and core-cladding interface irregularities. Loss roughly independent of wavelength.

**Step 3 — Bending Losses (1 mark)**:
- **Macrobending**: Large-radius bends cause radiation loss; loss $\propto \exp(-R/R_c)$.
- **Microbending**: Small-scale distortions from external pressure couple guided modes to radiation modes.

**Step 4 — Wavelength dependence & transmission windows (2 marks)**:
Attenuation vs. wavelength shows three windows:
- **850 nm window**: 2–3 dB/km (used in legacy LANs with low-cost sources).
- **1300 nm window (O-band)**: 0.35 dB/km (zero material dispersion).
- **1550 nm window (C-band)**: 0.20 dB/km (lowest attenuation — used in long-haul DWDM).

Modern systems use 1550 nm; research fibers reach 0.15 dB/km near 1570 nm.

---

**(b) With a block diagram, explain the working principle of Optical Time Domain Reflectometer (OTDR). Mention its applications. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution**:

**Step 1 — Principle (2 marks)**:
The OTDR injects narrow, high-peak-power optical pulses (typical pulse width: 10 ns–10 µs, wavelength 1310/1550 nm) into one end of a fiber and analyzes the **backscattered (Rayleigh)** and **back-reflected (Fresnel)** light returning from various points along the fiber.

**Step 2 — Block Diagram (2 marks)**:

```
┌─────────────┐    ┌────────────┐    ┌──────────────┐
│ Pulse       │───>│ Pulsed     │───>│ Directional  │
│ Generator   │    │ Laser Diode│    │ Coupler      │
└─────────────┘    └────────────┘    └──────┬───────┘
                                            │
                                       ┌────▼────┐
                                       │ Fiber   │
                                       │ Under   │
                                       │ Test    │
                                       └────┬────┘
                                            │ (backscatter)
                                       ┌────▼──────┐    ┌─────────────┐
                                       │ Avalanche │───>│ Amplifier & │
                                       │ Photodiode│    │ Averager    │
                                       └───────────┘    └──────┬──────┘
                                                             │
                                                     ┌───────▼──────┐
                                                     │ Display Trace│
                                                     │ Attenuation  │
                                                     │ vs Distance  │
                                                     └──────────────┘
```

**Step 3 — Working (2 marks)**:
- Pulses are launched into the fiber; light backscatters continuously due to Rayleigh scattering.
- The OTDR measures the backscatter power as a function of time.
- Distance to event: $d = ct/(2n_1)$.
- A graph of backscatter power (in dB) vs. distance shows a slope proportional to attenuation.
- Step discontinuities indicate connectors/splices; sharp spikes indicate Fresnel reflections; sharp drops indicate fiber breaks.

**Step 4 — Applications (1 mark)**:
- Locating fiber breaks and faults.
- Measuring splice and connector losses.
- Determining fiber length and attenuation coefficient.
- Verifying installed cable quality.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert time units**: Always convert ns to seconds before using $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s. A 50 ns answer is **incorrect** — it must be $50 \times 10^{-9}$ s.
> 2. **Missing the factor of 2 in OTDR distance**: $d = ct/(2n)$, not $ct/n$. The light travels to the event AND back.
> 3. **Confusing NA with relative index difference $\Delta$**: NA is dimensionless but depends on $n_1$ and $n_2$. Don't write $NA = \Delta$.
> 4. **Drawing only axial rays**: KTU examiners expect at least 3 rays in the diagram — axial, meridional, and leaky ray — to demonstrate understanding.
> 5. **Stating "TIR occurs when light goes from rarer to denser medium"** — this is the **opposite** of the truth. TIR requires travel from denser to rarer medium.
> 6. **Mixing up modal and material dispersion**: Modal dispersion is in MMF; material dispersion is intrinsic and exists in all fibers.
> 7. **Skipping units in numerical answers**: Always include dB/km for attenuation, MHz·km for bandwidth-distance product, ps for pulse broadening.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

**Light Guidance Fundamentals**:
- Optical fiber relies on **Total Internal Reflection (TIR)** at the core-cladding boundary.
- Condition: $n_1 > n_2$ and $\theta_i > \theta_c$.
- Critical angle: $\sin \theta_c = n_2/n_1$.

**Numerical Aperture (NA)**:
- $NA = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}$
- Higher NA = better light collection.
- Typical range: 0.1 (SMF) to 0.5 (MMF).

**Fiber Types**:
- **Step-Index**: Abrupt index change; either SMF or MMF.
- **Graded-Index**: Parabolic index profile; reduces modal dispersion.
- **SMF**: $V < 2.405$; no modal dispersion; long-haul use.
- **MMF**: $V > 2.405$; cheap sources; LAN/data centers.

**Losses**:
- **Absorption**: OH⁻ peak at 1383 nm is the worst impurity.
- **Rayleigh scattering**: $\propto \lambda^{-4}$; dominant at short wavelengths.
- **Bending**: Macrobending + Microbending.
- **Total attenuation**: $\alpha$ (dB/km) = 0.2 at 1550 nm, 0.35 at 1300 nm.

**Dispersion**:
- **Modal dispersion**: dominant in MMF; reduced by GIF.
- **Material dispersion**: due to $dn/d\lambda$; zero near 1300 nm in silica.
- **Waveguide dispersion**: geometry-dependent; important in SMF.
- **PMD**: only in SMF at high bit rates.

**OTDR**:
- Uses Rayleigh backscatter and Fresnel reflections.
- Distance formula: $d = ct/(2n_1)$.
- Measures attenuation, splice/connector loss, fault location.

**V-Number (Normalization)**:
- $V = (2\pi a/\lambda) NA$
- $V < 2.405$ → SMF
- $V > 2.405$ → MMF; $M \approx V^2/2$ modes.

**Transmission Windows**:
- **O-band**: 1260–1360 nm (zero dispersion).
- **C-band**: 1530–1565 nm (minimum loss).
- **L-band**: 1565–1625 nm (extended DWDM).

**Merits**: EMI immunity, low loss, high BW, security, lightweight.
**Demerits**: Cost, fragility, complex splicing, specialist tools.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Connectors and Splices, Applications of Optical Fibres: Optical Fibre Communication, Selection of Optical Fibers, Fibre Optic Sensors, Measurement of Temperature, Pressure, Displacement, Strain, Acceleration and Fluid Level
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT861 - OPTOELECTRONICS AND INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Connectors and Splices, Applications of Optical Fibres: Optical Fibre Communication, Selection of Optical Fibers, Fibre Optic Sensors, Measurement of Temperature, Pressure, Displacement, Strain, Acceleration and Fluid Level

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Connectors and Splices — Definition

In an optical fibre link, light must travel from the source through fibres, across joints, into detectors, and through various passive components. The **interconnection devices** that make such a link possible are of two fundamentally different kinds:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Connector (Optical Fibre Connector):** A *removable, reusable* mechanical device used to align and join the cleaved ends of two optical fibres (or a fibre and an active device such as a source/detector) so that optical power is transferred from one to the other with minimum loss and minimum back-reflection.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Splice (Optical Fibre Splice):** A *permanent* (or semi-permanent) joint formed between two fibre ends, designed to couple optical power with low loss. Once made, a splice is generally not intended to be repeatedly disconnected.

### Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)

Imagine you are stringing Christmas lights along a long balcony.
- A **connector** is like the **plug-and-socket** that lets you unplug the lights from the wall and move them to another room. It is convenient, can be re-used, but is slightly lossy and a bit bulky.
- A **splice** is like the **soldered joint** between two wires deep inside the cable. Once soldered, it is small, low-loss, but cannot be undone easily.

Both exist in optical fibres for the very same engineering reasons: connectors give you *flexibility and modularity*; splices give you *lowest loss and ruggedness* for permanent links.

## 1.2 Classification of Optical Connectors

By alignment mechanism, the major families are:

1. **Butt-joint (Ferrule-and-Sleeve) Connectors** — most common (e.g., ST, SC, FC, LC).
2. **Expanded-Beam Connectors** — uses a lens to collimate, then re-focus.
3. **Biconic Connectors** — conical ferrule alignment.
4. **SMA Connectors** — precision-machined metal alignment sleeve (legacy).

## 1.3 Classification of Splices

1. **Fusion Splice** — fibres are melted and fused by an electric arc.
2. **Mechanical Splice** — fibres are aligned mechanically and held by an index-matching gel or epoxy.
   - **V-Groove Mechanical Splice**
   - **Elastomeric Splice**
   - **Tube / Capillary Splice**
   - **Rotary Mechanical Splice**

## 1.4 Applications of Optical Fibres — Overview

Optical fibres have evolved far beyond telecommunications. They are now crucial **sensing elements** because:
- They are immune to **electromagnetic interference (EMI)**.
- They operate in **hazardous / explosive** environments safely (no sparks).
- They are small, light, chemically inert, and immune to corrosion.
- They can sense a wide variety of physical parameters.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Two Major Application Domains**
> 1. **Optical Fibre Communication** — long-haul, high-bandwidth data transport.
> 2. **Fibre Optic Sensors** — measurement of Temperature, Pressure, Displacement, Strain, Acceleration, Fluid Level, Refractive Index, Chemical concentration, etc.

## 1.5 Fibre Optic Sensors — High-Level Categories

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Classification by Modulation Principle:**
> 1. **Intensity-Modulated Sensors** — measurand changes transmitted light intensity.
> 2. **Phase-Modulated (Interferometric) Sensors** — measurand changes optical phase.
> 3. **Wavelength-Modulated Sensors** — measurand shifts Bragg / fluorescence wavelength.
> 4. **Polarization-Modulated Sensors** — measurand alters state of polarization.
> 5. **Distributed Sensors** — measurand is measured as a function of position along the fibre (using OTDR, Raman/Brillouin scattering).

## 1.6 Selection of Optical Fibres — The Three Pillars

When selecting a fibre for a given application, three questions dominate:

1. **What distance?** → Determines **attenuation budget** and whether **single-mode or multi-mode** is required.
2. **What bandwidth?** → Determines **modal dispersion** requirement → graded-index vs. step-index.
3. **What environment?** → Determines **coating, jacket, operating wavelength, and sensor compatibility**.

Standard decision metric: **Silica fibres** at **850 nm, 1300 nm, 1550 nm**; **Plastic Optical Fibres (POF)** for short, low-cost links.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Connectors — Operational Principle

A connector must perform three engineering functions simultaneously:

1. **Mechanical Alignment** — bring the two fibre cores within micrometres of perfect coaxial alignment.
2. **Optical Coupling** — minimize Fresnel reflection at the air-glass interface.
3. **Physical Protection** — protect the fibre ends from contamination, scratches, and bending.

The dominant loss mechanisms at a connector are:
- **Lateral (transverse) misalignment** — displacement `d` between core centres.
- **Angular misalignment** — tilt angle `\theta` between fibre axes.
- **Longitudinal (gap) separation** — axial gap `s` between fibre end faces.
- **Fresnel reflection** — index mismatch at the air gap.
- **End-face quality** — scratches, dirt, polishing defects.

## 2.2 Splices — Operational Principle

### 2.2.1 Fusion Splice

- A precision fusion splicer **strips, cleaves, and aligns** the fibres.
- An **electric arc** momentarily melts the fibre tips to ~ 2000 °C, fusing them.
- Loss typically **0.05 – 0.1 dB** per splice.
- Used in **long-haul telecom**, undersea cables, and **FTTH (Fibre-to-the-Home)**.

### 2.2.2 Mechanical Splice

- Fibres are placed in a precision V-groove / tube and held with epoxy or clamping.
- **Index-matching gel** (n ≈ 1.46 for silica) fills the air gap to suppress Fresnel reflection.
- Loss typically **0.1 – 0.5 dB** per splice.
- Used in **field repair**, **temporary joints**, and **FTTH drop cables**.

## 2.3 Optical Fibre Communication — System Architecture

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Block Diagram of an Optical Fibre Communication System:**
> `Source → Modulator → Driver → Fibre Tx → Connector/Splice → Fibre Link (with Repeaters/EDFA) → Connector/Splice → Photodetector → Amplifier → Demodulator → Output`
>
> **Key Sub-Systems:**
> - **Optical Source:** LED (MM, low-cost, short-haul) or Laser Diode (SM, long-haul, high-bit-rate).
> - **Optical Fibre:** SM or MM, with repeaters / EDFAs for long distances.
> - **Photodetector:** PIN photodiode (low bias) or APD (high sensitivity).
> - **Multiplexing:** WDM (Wavelength-Division Multiplexing) for capacity multiplication.

**Design equation (simplified power budget):**

$$P_{rx} = P_{tx} - L_f - L_c - L_s - M$$

where $P_{rx}$ is received power, $P_{tx}$ is transmitted power, $L_f$ is total fibre attenuation loss, $L_c$ is connector loss, $L_s$ is splice loss, and $M$ is the safety margin.

## 2.4 Fibre Optic Sensors — Working Principle

A **fibre optic sensor** uses the fibre itself (or a transducer attached to it) as the sensing element. The measurand modulates one or more of the optical properties of the light travelling inside the fibre.

### 2.4.1 Intensity-Modulated Sensors
The simplest class. Measurand changes intensity; output is detected directly.

- **Microbend sensor** — measurand-induced microbending causes loss.
- **Macrobend sensor** — large bends couple light out of the core.
- **Evanescent-wave sensor** — measurand alters refractive index near the core.
- **Reflective / shutter sensor** — measurand modulates reflected light.

### 2.4.2 Phase-Modulated (Interferometric) Sensors
Measurand-induced phase shift is converted to intensity change via interference.

- **Mach-Zehnder interferometer**
- **Michelson interferometer**
- **Sagnac interferometer** (rotation sensing)
- **Fabry-Perot interferometer**

### 2.4.3 Wavelength-Modulated Sensors
- **Fibre Bragg Grating (FBG)** sensors — measurand shifts Bragg wavelength.

The Bragg condition is:

$$\lambda_B = 2 n_{eff} \Lambda$$

where $n_{eff}$ is the effective refractive index of the core and $\Lambda$ is the grating period.

## 2.5 Measurement of Temperature

> [!NOTE]
> **Principle:** Temperature changes induce both refractive index change (thermo-optic effect) and dimensional change (thermal expansion) in the fibre, modulating the transmitted intensity, phase, or Bragg wavelength.

**Common Architectures:**

1. **Fibre Bragg Grating (FBG) Temperature Sensor** — wavelength shift on heating.
2. **Interferometric Temperature Sensor** — phase shift in Mach-Zehnder.
3. **Fluorescence-Decay Temperature Sensor** — decay time of doped material depends on T.
4. **Liquid-Crystal / Thermochromic Temperature Sensor** — intensity-based.

## 2.6 Measurement of Pressure

> [!NOTE]
> **Principle:** Pressure deforms the fibre (microbending, macrobending, or stretch), modulating intensity, phase, or polarization.

**Common Architectures:**

1. **Microbend Pressure Sensor** — pressure plate presses the fibre against a corrugated surface.
2. **Fabry-Perot Pressure Sensor** — pressure deflects a diaphragm, changing cavity length.
3. **Polarimetric Pressure Sensor** — pressure-induced birefringence.

## 2.7 Measurement of Displacement

> [!NOTE]
> **Principle:** Displacement of a target reflects, attenuates, or modulates light in the fibre.

**Common Architectures:**

1. **Reflective Displacement Sensor** — light is reflected from a moving target; intensity varies with distance.
2. **Modal-Mode Interferometer** — displacement changes mode coupling.
3. **FBG Displacement Sensor** — strain induced by a mechanical linkage.

## 2.8 Measurement of Strain

> [!NOTE]
> **Principle:** Strain changes the fibre's refractive index (photo-elastic effect) and length, modulating phase or Bragg wavelength.

**Common Architectures:**

1. **FBG Strain Sensor** — wavelength shift proportional to strain.
2. **Interferometric Strain Sensor** — phase shift measured via Mach-Zehnder / Michelson.
3. **Polarimetric Strain Sensor** — birefringence changes with strain.

## 2.9 Measurement of Acceleration (Vibration)

> [!NOTE]
> **Principle:** A seismic mass attached to the fibre generates strain proportional to acceleration.

**Common Architectures:**

1. **FBG Accelerometer** — FBG bonded to a cantilever with a mass; acceleration bends the cantilever.
2. **Interferometric Accelerometer** — mass displacement changes interferometer path difference.
3. **Intensity-Based Accelerometer** — mass movement modulates intensity.

## 2.10 Measurement of Fluid Level

> [!NOTE]
> **Principle:** Fluid level changes optical properties at the fibre-fluid interface (Fresnel reflection, refractive index change, or thermal/evanescent coupling).

**Common Architectures:**

1. **Fresnel Reflection Level Sensor** — light reflected from liquid surface changes with level.
2. **Total Internal Reflection (TIR) Sensor** — level changes the condition for total internal reflection at the fibre tip.
3. **Distributed Temperature Sensing (DTS)** — uses Raman scattering along the fibre.

## 2.11 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Description / Units |
|---|---------|---------|---------------------|
| 1 | Fresnel Reflection Coefficient | $R = \left(\dfrac{n_1 - n_2}{n_1 + n_2}\right)^2$ | Power reflected at an interface; dimensionless |
| 2 | Insertion Loss (Connector / Splice) | $L_i = -10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}}\right)$ | Loss in **dB**; positive value |
| 3 | Return Loss | $R_L = -10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_r}{P_i}\right)$ | Ratio of back-reflected to incident power; in **dB** |
| 4 | Lateral Misalignment Loss (approx., $d<a$) | $L_{lat} \approx -10 \log_{10}\!\left[1 - \dfrac{2d}{\pi a}\right]$ dB | $d$ = offset, $a$ = core radius |
| 5 | Angular Misalignment Loss | $L_\theta \approx -10 \log_{10}\!\left[\exp\!\left(-\left(\dfrac{\theta}{\theta_0}\right)^2\right)\right]$ | $\theta$ = tilt, $\theta_0$ = tolerance |
| 6 | Gap Loss (Fresnel at air gap) | $L_{gap} = -10 \log_{10}\!\left[1 - R\right]$ | For $n_1 = 1.46, n_2 = 1.0$ → $R \approx 0.034$ → $L \approx 0.148$ dB |
| 7 | FBG Bragg Condition | $\lambda_B = 2 n_{eff} \Lambda$ | Centre wavelength reflected by grating |
| 8 | FBG Temperature Sensitivity | $\Delta\lambda_B = \lambda_B \left(\alpha + \xi\right) \Delta T$ | $\alpha$ = thermal expansion, $\xi$ = thermo-optic coeff. |
| 9 | FBG Strain Sensitivity | $\Delta\lambda_B = \lambda_B \left(1 - p_e\right) \epsilon$ | $p_e$ = effective strain-optic coefficient (~0.22 for silica) |
| 10 | Power Budget Equation | $P_{rx} = P_{tx} - L_f - L_c - L_s - M$ | All powers in **dBm**, all losses in **dB** |
| 11 | Microbend Loss Coefficient | $\dfrac{\Delta P}{P} \approx K \cdot \Delta N$ | $K$ = sensitivity, $\Delta N$ = microbend count |
| 12 | Phase Change in Interferometer | $\Delta\phi = \dfrac{2\pi n L}{\lambda}$ | Phase shift due to $L$ change |
| 13 | Fluid-Level Reflective Sensor Output | $V_{out} \propto \dfrac{1}{\left(d - h\right)^2}$ | $h$ = liquid level, $d$ = fibre-to-detector distance |

## 2.12 Engineering Utility

| Domain | Why Optical Fibre? |
|--------|-------------------|
| Power-station transformer monitoring | Immunity to **50/60 Hz EMI** and high-voltage isolation |
| Aircraft structural health | Lightweight, multiplexable, **distributed sensing** over wings |
| Oil & gas downhole | Operates at **>200 °C**, in **H₂S / explosive** atmospheres |
| Medical endoscopy | Biocompatible, sterilizable, flexible |
| Defence / perimeter intrusion | Tamper-proof, **EMI-immune**, long-range |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Fresnel Reflection Loss

When light passes from medium 1 (refractive index $n_1$, the fibre core) into medium 2 (refractive index $n_2$, air gap), a fraction is reflected back at the interface.

**Step 1: Amplitude reflection coefficient for perpendicular incidence.**

The boundary condition at the interface requires continuity of the tangential electric field. For a wave incident normally from medium 1 onto medium 2:

$$r = \dfrac{n_1 - n_2}{n_1 + n_2}$$

This is the ratio of the reflected electric field amplitude to the incident electric field amplitude.

**Step 2: Convert amplitude coefficient to power coefficient.**

Since optical power is proportional to the *square* of the electric field amplitude:

$$R = \vert r \vert^{2} = \left(\dfrac{n_1 - n_2}{n_1 + n_2}\right)^{2}$$

**Step 3: Evaluate for a typical silica-air interface.**

For a silica fibre, $n_1 = 1.46$; for air, $n_2 = 1.00$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
R &= \left(\dfrac{1.46 - 1.00}{1.46 + 1.00}\right)^{2} \\
&= \left(\dfrac{0.46}{2.46}\right)^{2} \\
&= \left(0.18699\right)^{2} \\
&\approx 0.03497
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Convert to insertion loss in dB.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{Fresnel} &= -10 \log_{10}\!\left(1 - R\right) \\
&= -10 \log_{10}\!\left(1 - 0.03497\right) \\
&= -10 \log_{10}\!\left(0.96503\right) \\
&\approx 0.154\ \text{dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** A single silica-air interface reflects approximately **3.5 %** of the incident power, equivalent to a loss of about **0.15 dB**. This is why **index-matching gel** (n ≈ 1.46) is used in splices and connectors — it eliminates the air gap.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** The Fresnel loss is *intrinsic* to any dielectric interface. It cannot be reduced by better mechanical alignment — only by **eliminating the index discontinuity**.

## 3.2 Derivation of Lateral Misalignment Loss

Consider two step-index fibres of core radius $a$ with a lateral (transverse) offset $d$ between their core centres. Assume uniform power distribution across the core (a reasonable approximation for MM fibres).

**Step 1: Overlap area.**

For $d \le 2a$, the area of overlap between the two circular cores is:

$$A_{overlap} = 2a^{2} \cos^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{d}{2a}\right) - \dfrac{d}{2}\sqrt{4a^{2} - d^{2}}$$

**Step 2: Coupling efficiency (overlap ratio).**

$$\eta = \dfrac{A_{overlap}}{\pi a^{2}}$$

For *small* offsets ($d \ll a$), this simplifies approximately to:

$$\eta \approx 1 - \dfrac{2d}{\pi a}$$

**Step 3: Convert to dB.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{lat} &= -10 \log_{10}\!\left(\eta\right) \\
&\approx -10 \log_{10}\!\left(1 - \dfrac{2d}{\pi a}\right) \\
&\approx \dfrac{4.343 \cdot 2d}{\pi a} \\
&\approx \dfrac{2.76\, d}{a}\ \text{dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Numerical Example:** For $a = 50\ \mu m$ (MM fibre) and $d = 5\ \mu m$:

$$L_{lat} \approx \dfrac{2.76 \cdot 5}{50} = 0.276\ \text{dB}$$

For SM fibres with $a \approx 4\ \mu m$, even $d = 1\ \mu m$ gives:

$$L_{lat} \approx \dfrac{2.76 \cdot 1}{4} = 0.69\ \text{dB}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Lateral misalignment is the *dominant* loss mechanism in single-mode connectors. MM fibres are far more tolerant.

## 3.3 Derivation of FBG Temperature Sensitivity

**Step 1: Start with Bragg condition.**

$$\lambda_B = 2 n_{eff} \Lambda$$

**Step 2: Take total differential.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta \lambda_B &= 2 \Lambda \cdot \Delta n_{eff} + 2 n_{eff} \cdot \Delta \Lambda \\
&= 2 n_{eff} \Lambda \left(\dfrac{\Delta n_{eff}}{n_{eff}} + \dfrac{\Delta \Lambda}{\Lambda}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Substitute definitions of thermal coefficients.**

- Thermal expansion: $\dfrac{\Delta \Lambda}{\Lambda} = \alpha \cdot \Delta T$, where $\alpha$ = thermal expansion coefficient of silica ≈ $0.55 \times 10^{-6}$ /°C.
- Thermo-optic effect: $\dfrac{\Delta n_{eff}}{n_{eff}} = \xi \cdot \Delta T$, where $\xi$ = thermo-optic coefficient of silica ≈ $8.6 \times 10^{-6}$ /°C.

**Step 4: Final expression.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta \lambda_B &= \lambda_B \left(\xi + \alpha\right) \Delta T \\
&= \lambda_B \cdot K_T \cdot \Delta T
\end{aligned}
$$

where $K_T = \xi + \alpha$ is the **temperature sensitivity** (≈ $9.15 \times 10^{-6}$ /°C for silica).

**Step 5: Numerical evaluation.**

For $\lambda_B = 1550$ nm and $\Delta T = 100$ °C:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta \lambda_B &= 1550 \times 10^{-9} \times 9.15 \times 10^{-6} \times 100 \\
&= 1.418 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{m} \\
&= 1.418\ \text{nm}
\end{aligned}
$$

This wavelength shift is easily resolved by an **optical spectrum analyser** or an **interrogator** with 1 pm resolution.

## 3.4 Derivation of FBG Strain Sensitivity

**Step 1: Start from Bragg condition (again).**

$$\lambda_B = 2 n_{eff} \Lambda$$

**Step 2: Take total differential under strain $\epsilon$.**

$$\dfrac{\Delta \lambda_B}{\lambda_B} = \dfrac{\Delta n_{eff}}{n_{eff}} + \dfrac{\Delta \Lambda}{\Lambda}$$

**Step 3: Substitute strain terms.**

- Mechanical strain: $\dfrac{\Delta \Lambda}{\Lambda} = \epsilon$.
- Photo-elastic effect: $\dfrac{\Delta n_{eff}}{n_{eff}} = -p_e \cdot \epsilon$, where $p_e$ = effective strain-optic coefficient ≈ $0.22$ for silica.

**Step 4: Final expression.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{\Delta \lambda_B}{\lambda_B} &= \epsilon - p_e \epsilon \\
&= \left(1 - p_e\right) \epsilon
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence:

$$\Delta \lambda_B = \lambda_B \left(1 - p_e\right) \epsilon$$

For silica, $1 - p_e \approx 0.78$.

**Step 5: Numerical evaluation.**

For $\lambda_B = 1550$ nm and $\epsilon = 1000\ \mu \epsilon$ (0.1 % strain):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta \lambda_B &= 1550 \times 10^{-9} \times 0.78 \times 1000 \times 10^{-6} \\
&= 1.209 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{m} \\
&= 1.209\ \text{nm}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.5 Derivation of Reflective Fluid-Level Sensor Response

A typical reflective fluid-level sensor uses two fibres (one transmitting, one receiving) facing a reflective liquid surface at distance $h$ below the fibre tips.

**Step 1: Cone of illumination.**

Light exits the transmitting fibre within a numerical-aperture cone. At distance $h$, the illuminated area is:

$$A_{illum} = \pi \left(NA \cdot h + a\right)^{2}$$

**Step 2: Light captured by receiving fibre.**

Only the portion of the reflected cone re-entering the receiving fibre within its own NA contributes to the signal. By similar triangles:

$$A_{captured} \propto \dfrac{a^{2}}{\left(2h\right)^{2}}$$

**Step 3: Output voltage of photodetector.**

$$V_{out} = R_{pd} \cdot P_{captured} \propto \dfrac{K}{\left(2h\right)^{2}}$$

where $R_{pd}$ is photodetector responsivity (A/W) and $K$ is a constant depending on fibre NA, surface reflectivity, and electronic gain.

**Step 4: Inverse-square law for level.**

Thus, $V_{out}$ decreases as $1/h^{2}$ — a **non-linear** response that requires calibration or linearization (e.g., logarithmic amplifier or microcontroller table lookup).

## 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Connector vs. Splice

| Parameter | Connector | Splice |
|-----------|-----------|--------|
| Removability | Yes | No |
| Typical Loss | 0.2 – 1.0 dB | 0.05 – 0.3 dB |
| Return Loss | 20 – 60 dB (APC) | 40 – 60 dB (fusion) |
| Cost per joint | High | Low (fusion machine expensive, but per-joint cheap) |
| Field installation | Easy | Requires skill |
| Use case | Patch panels, equipment ports | Long-haul trunks, repairs |

## 3.7 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Sensor Architectures

| Measurand | Most Common Sensor | Modulation Type | Typical Sensitivity |
|-----------|---------------------|-----------------|----------------------|
| Temperature | FBG | Wavelength | ~ 10 pm/°C |
| Pressure | Microbend / Fabry-Perot | Intensity / Phase | ~ 1 pm/kPa |
| Displacement | Reflective pair | Intensity | ~ 1 % / mm |
| Strain | FBG / Interferometric | Wavelength / Phase | ~ 1 pm/$\mu\epsilon$ |
| Acceleration | FBG with seismic mass | Wavelength | ~ 10 pm/g |
| Fluid Level | Fresnel reflection | Intensity | Depends on geometry |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Differentiate between an optical fibre connector and an optical fibre splice. Mention one typical application of each. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Connector | Splice |
|---------|-----------|--------|
| Removability | Removable, reusable | Permanent |
| Typical Loss | 0.2 – 1.0 dB | 0.05 – 0.3 dB |
| Typical Use | Patch panels, equipment ports | Cable joints, FTTH drops |
| Cost | Higher per joint | Lower per joint |

**Applications:** Connectors are used at equipment interfaces (e.g., transceivers); fusion splices are used in long-haul trunk lines and undersea cables.

---

### Q2. State and explain the Fresnel reflection loss at a silica–air interface. How is it minimized in optical fibre joints? `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

When light travelling in a silica core ($n_1 = 1.46$) encounters an air gap ($n_2 = 1.00$), the reflection coefficient is:

$$R = \left(\dfrac{n_1 - n_2}{n_1 + n_2}\right)^{2} = \left(\dfrac{0.46}{2.46}\right)^{2} \approx 0.035$$

Equivalent loss ≈ **0.15 dB** per interface. It is minimized by using **index-matching gel** (n ≈ 1.46) or by **physical contact (PC)** ferrule-end geometry in connectors, eliminating the air gap altogether.

---

### Q3. List any **three** measurands that can be sensed using optical fibres, along with the type of modulation used in each case. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**Model Answer:**

1. **Temperature** → Wavelength modulation (FBG) or phase modulation (Mach-Zehnder).
2. **Pressure** → Intensity modulation (microbend) or phase modulation (Fabry-Perot).
3. **Strain** → Wavelength modulation (FBG) or polarization modulation (polarimetric).

---

### Q4. What is a Fibre Bragg Grating (FBG)? Write its Bragg condition. `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

An FBG is a periodic variation of the refractive index inscribed in the core of an optical fibre using UV interference. It reflects a narrow wavelength band centred at the **Bragg wavelength** given by:

$$\lambda_B = 2 n_{eff} \Lambda$$

where $n_{eff}$ is the effective refractive index of the core mode and $\Lambda$ is the grating period.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### **Question A** (14 Marks)

**(a)** Explain in detail the different types of **splices** used in optical fibre communication systems. Compare fusion and mechanical splicing with respect to loss, ease of installation, and typical applications. **(7 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(b)** Derive the expression for **Fresnel reflection loss** at an optical fibre joint. Discuss the techniques used to minimize this loss in connectors and splices. **(7 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a)

**Step 1: Definition.** A splice is a permanent joint between two optical fibres.

**Step 2: Fusion Splice.**
- Fibres are stripped, cleaved, and aligned in a fusion splicer.
- An electric arc melts the fibre ends (~ 2000 °C), fusing them.
- Loss: 0.05 – 0.1 dB; return loss > 60 dB.
- Applications: long-haul telecom, undersea cables, FTTH trunks.

**Step 3: Mechanical Splices.**

1. **V-Groove Splice:** Two fibres rest in a precision-machined V-groove; index-matching gel fills the gap.
2. **Elastomeric Splice:** A flexible cross-section aligns fibres automatically.
3. **Tube/Capillary Splice:** Fibres inserted into a precision tube; epoxy or gel secures them.
4. **Rotary Splice:** Rotational alignment of fibres inside a sleeve.

**Step 4: Comparison.**

| Parameter | Fusion | Mechanical |
|-----------|--------|-----------|
| Loss | 0.05 – 0.1 dB | 0.1 – 0.5 dB |
| Skill | Trained technician | Moderate |
| Equipment | Splicer (₹₹₹) | Cheap |
| Field use | Yes | Yes |

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b)

**Step 1: Boundary condition at interface.**
At normal incidence, the amplitude reflection coefficient is:

$$r = \dfrac{n_1 - n_2}{n_1 + n_2}$$

**Step 2: Power reflection.**

$$R = \vert r \vert^{2} = \left(\dfrac{n_1 - n_2}{n_1 + n_2}\right)^{2}$$

**Step 3: Evaluate for silica–air.**

$$R = \left(\dfrac{0.46}{2.46}\right)^{2} \approx 0.035 \Rightarrow L \approx 0.15\ \text{dB}$$

**Step 4: Loss in dB.**

$$L_{Fresnel} = -10 \log_{10}\!\left(1 - R\right)$$

**Step 5: Minimization techniques.**

- **Index-matching gel** (n ≈ 1.46) — eliminates air gap.
- **Physical contact (PC) connectors** — ferrule ends touch directly.
- **Angled Physical Contact (APC) connectors** — 8° angle sends reflections out of the core.
- **Anti-reflection coating** — quarter-wave dielectric layer.

---

### **Question B** (14 Marks — Alternative)

**(a)** Discuss the working principle of a **Fibre Bragg Grating (FBG)** based temperature sensor. Derive the expression for its wavelength shift with temperature. **(7 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**(b)** Explain the **fibre optic communication system** with a neat block diagram. State the role of each block. **(7 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a)

**Step 1: Bragg condition.**

$$\lambda_B = 2 n_{eff} \Lambda$$

**Step 2: Differential with respect to temperature.**

$$\Delta \lambda_B = 2 \Lambda \Delta n_{eff} + 2 n_{eff} \Delta \Lambda$$

**Step 3: Substitute thermo-optic and expansion coefficients.**

$$\Delta \lambda_B = \lambda_B \left(\xi + \alpha\right) \Delta T$$

where $\xi \approx 8.6 \times 10^{-6}$ /°C and $\alpha \approx 0.55 \times 10^{-6}$ /°C.

**Step 4: Practical readout.**
A broadband source (SLED) illuminates the FBG; the reflected wavelength is read using an optical spectrum analyser or interrogator. A shift of ~ 10 pm/°C is typical for $\lambda_B \approx 1550$ nm.

**Step 5: Significance.**
FBG sensors are *wavelength-encoded*, hence **immune to intensity fluctuations** and can be multiplexed along a single fibre (many gratings at different wavelengths).

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b)

**Block Diagram (text):**

`Source → Modulator → Driver → Connector → Fibre Link (with EDFA repeaters) → Connector → Photodetector → Amplifier → Demodulator → Output`

**Functions:**

1. **Optical Source (LED/LD):** Converts electrical signal to optical carrier.
2. **Modulator + Driver:** Impresses information onto the optical carrier (direct or external modulation).
3. **Optical Fibre:** Transmits the modulated light with low loss and high bandwidth.
4. **Connector/Splice:** Couples fibre to source, detector, and other fibre segments.
5. **Repeater / EDFA:** Amplifies optical signal in long-haul links.
6. **Photodetector (PIN/APD):** Converts optical signal back to electrical.
7. **Amplifier:** Boosts the weak electrical signal.
8. **Demodulator:** Recovers the original information.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Units error:** Mixing dBm and dB in the power-budget equation (always use the same units).
> 2. **Forgetting return loss:** Many students compute only insertion loss; KTU examiners explicitly ask about Fresnel *back*-reflection.
> 3. **Confusing FBG reflectivity vs. transmission:** An FBG *reflects* the Bragg wavelength, not transmits. Drawing it incorrectly loses 2 marks.
> 4. **Numerical substitution:** When asked to evaluate for silica, use $n = 1.46$ — not 1.5 — and the resulting $R \approx 3.5\%$.
> 5. **Selection-of-fibre question:** Always justify with *both* bandwidth and distance; do not write generic answers.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Connector vs. Splice:** Connector is *removable*; splice is *permanent*. Fusion splice has the lowest loss (~ 0.05 dB).
- **Fresnel Reflection:** $R = ((n_1 - n_2)/(n_1 + n_2))^2$; ≈ 3.5% for silica–air. Minimized via index-matching gel or PC/APC geometry.
- **Insertion Loss:** $L_i = -10 \log_{10}(P_{out}/P_{in})$ in dB.
- **Return Loss:** $R_L = -10 \log_{10}(P_r / P_i)$ in dB.
- **Lateral misalignment** dominates in SM fibres; ~ 0.7 dB loss for $d = 1\ \mu m$ in a 4-$\mu m$ core.
- **FBG equation:** $\lambda_B = 2 n_{eff} \Lambda$.
- **FBG temperature sensitivity:** $\Delta \lambda_B = \lambda_B (\alpha + \xi) \Delta T \approx 10\ \text{pm/°C}$ at 1550 nm.
- **FBG strain sensitivity:** $\Delta \lambda_B = \lambda_B (1 - p_e) \epsilon \approx 1.2\ \text{pm/}\mu\epsilon$ at 1550 nm.
- **Fibre Optic Sensors** use intensity, phase, wavelength, or polarization modulation.
- **Microbend sensor** for pressure/force: measurand modulates bending loss.
- **Fabry-Perot sensor** for pressure/displacement: cavity length modulation.
- **Mach-Zehnder sensor** for strain/temperature: phase-shift detection.
- **Fresnel reflective level sensor:** $V_{out} \propto 1/(2h)^2$ (inverse-square).
- **Selection of fibre:** distance + bandwidth + environment → SM vs. MM, step vs. graded.
- **System link budget:** $P_{rx} = P_{tx} - L_f - L_c - L_s - M$ — keep all quantities in dB / dBm.
- **Optical fibre communication** advantages: EMI-immune, secure, low loss (~ 0.2 dB/km at 1550 nm), huge bandwidth.
- **Interferometric sensors** are the most sensitive; FBG sensors are the most practical for multiplexing.

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU Revision Tip:** Memorize *all four* derivations (Fresnel, lateral misalignment, FBG temperature, FBG strain) along with the numerical constants ($\alpha$, $\xi$, $p_e$). These appear almost every exam cycle in some form.
